theme: Creation's Goodness and the Genealogy of Immanuel

questions.text: Genesis 1 repeats the refrain 'and God saw that it was good' seven times, culminating in the seventh day being blessed and set apart before any command has been given. What does it suggest that Shabbat exists before the Torah does?

In Genesis 2:18, Adonai says He will make Adam an 'ezer kenegdo' - a helper corresponding to him. Since 'ezer' is elsewhere used of God himself (Psalm 121:2), what does this word tell you about the partnership God designed?

Matthew opens his account with a genealogy rooted in Abraham and David and calls Yeshua 'Immanuel - God with us.' How does that title echo the intimacy of Adonai forming Adam from dust and breathing life directly into him in Genesis 2:7?

Proverbs 1 warns against joining those who 'lie in wait for blood' and insists that fear of Adonai is where wisdom begins. What is one concrete way you can choose wisdom's voice over the crowd's this week?

Psalm 1 pictures the righteous as a tree planted by streams of water, echoing the rivers flowing out of Eden in Genesis 2:10-14. Discuss what it means for a life to be 'planted' near a source, in a world Genesis 1 has just called very good.

theme: Cain's Anger and Herod's Rage Against the King

questions.text: In Genesis 4:6-7, Adonai tells Cain that sin is 'crouching at the door' and that its desire is for him, yet he must rule over it. What does this image of a crouching animal suggest about how temptation actually works?

The Hebrew word 'teshuqah' (desire) appears in Genesis 4:7 describing sin's desire for Cain, and also in 3:16 describing the woman's desire for her husband. What connection might this rare, repeated word be drawing between the two texts?

Psalm 2 declares 'You are My Son, today I have begotten You' while the kings of the earth rage against Adonai's anointed. How does Herod's murderous rage against the infant King in Matthew 2 reenact this ancient pattern of earthly power resisting God's anointed one?
Proverbs 2 promises that wisdom and discretion will guard you and keep you from the 'strange path.' What is one boundary or practice you could put in place this week to guard your own heart?

Cain asks Adonai, 'Am I my brother's keeper?' Discuss that question alongside Herod's slaughter of the children of Bethlehem and Rachel weeping for her children in Matthew 2:16-18 - what does Scripture ask of us regarding responsibility for the vulnerable?

theme: Noah Finds Favor; the Beloved Son Is Named

questions.text: In Genesis 5, every genealogy entry ends with the grim refrain 'and he died' - except Enoch, who 'walked with God, and he was not, for God took him.' What makes Enoch's account stand apart from the long chain of death surrounding it?

Noah is the first person in the Torah said to have found 'chen' - favor or grace - in the eyes of Adonai (Genesis 6:8). Why might it matter that this key word first appears attached to a man about to be spared from judgment?

At Yeshua's baptism in Matthew 3:17, a voice from heaven says, 'This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.' How does this declaration echo Noah, a righteous man preserved through judgment, and Psalm 3's confidence that Adonai is 'a shield around me'?
Proverbs 3:5-6 urges, 'Trust in Adonai with all your heart, and lean not on your own understanding.' What is one decision or worry this week where you could practice that kind of trust?

Genesis 6 describes the earth as 'filled with violence (chamas)' before the flood. Discuss how Noah's finding favor amid pervasive corruption, and David's experience in Psalm 3 of being surrounded by 'many who rise against me,' speak to remaining faithful when the surrounding culture has gone badly wrong.

theme: The Ark Rests and Yeshua Overcomes the Tempter

questions.text: In Genesis 8:1, 'God remembered Noah' marks the turning point where the flood waters begin to recede. Notice the sequence of the raven that doesn't return and the dove that comes back empty, then with an olive leaf, then not at all - what does this tell you?

The Hebrew word for Noah's ark, 'tevah,' is the very same word used later for the basket that carries baby Moses down the Nile (Exodus 2:3). What might this shared word suggest about how these two deliverance stories echo each other?

Yeshua fasts forty days and nights in the wilderness in Matthew 4, paralleling the forty days and nights of flooding rain in Genesis 7. Where the flood generation failed under judgment, how does Yeshua's use of Torah to answer the tempter mark a different outcome?
Proverbs 4:23 instructs, 'Guard your heart, for from it flow the springs of life.' What is one specific input - a habit, relationship, or media choice - you could guard more carefully this week?

The number forty recurs across Scripture: the flood's forty days, Israel's forty years in the wilderness, Yeshua's forty days of testing. Discuss what this pattern suggests about how God uses extended periods of testing to bring about renewal.

theme: The Rainbow Covenant and Torah Fulfilled, Not Abolished

questions.text: In Genesis 9, the sign of God's covenant - the bow in the clouds - is given not only to Noah but to 'every living creature,' making this the broadest covenant in Scripture. Why might God have chosen a sign visible to the whole world?

The rainbow is called an 'ot ha-berit' - a sign of the covenant - in Genesis 9:12-13. What is the significance of God choosing a natural, recurring phenomenon as a permanent, visible reminder of His promise?

Yeshua declares in Matthew 5:17, 'Do not think I came to abolish the Torah or the Prophets... but to fulfill.' How does this statement of covenant continuity align with the permanence of the Noahic covenant, sealed with the promise 'never again'?
Proverbs 5 warns against the 'strange woman' and counsels, 'drink water from your own cistern.' What is one practical way you could guard the integrity of your closest relationships this week?

Genesis 9's account of Noah's drunkenness and the curse pronounced on Canaan has been badly misused throughout history to justify racism and slavery. Discuss how to read this difficult passage responsibly alongside Genesis 10's Table of Nations, which traces all humanity back to one shared family.

theme: Babel Scattered, Abram Called to Bless the Nations

questions.text: In Genesis 11, the builders of Babel try to 'make a name' for themselves and avoid being scattered, yet Adonai scatters them anyway. Genesis 12 immediately reverses this: Adonai promises to make Abram's name great instead. What is the contrast pointing to?

Adonai's call to Abram in Genesis 12:1, 'lech lecha,' is often translated 'go forth' but more literally means 'go for yourself.' What might this phrasing suggest about what the journey would mean for Abram personally, not just geographically?

Genesis 12:3 promises that through Abram 'all the families of the earth will be blessed.' Yeshua tells His talmidim in Matthew 6 to 'seek first His kingdom' - how does the blessing of the nations depend on people trusting God's promises the way Abram did when he left Ur?

Matthew 6:25-34 instructs us not to worry about tomorrow. What is one concrete anxiety this week where you could practice the kind of trust Abram displayed in leaving his security behind at Adonai's call?

Discuss the contrast between the tower builders trying to secure their own name through collective effort, and Abram simply receiving a name and blessing as a gift in response to God's call. What does this suggest about the difference between human ambition and divine calling?

theme: Melchizedek's Blessing and the Narrow Gate to Life

questions.text: In Genesis 14, Melchizedek, king of Salem and 'priest of God Most High,' brings out bread and wine to bless Abram - and Abram gives him a tenth of everything before anything is asked. What makes this brief encounter so unusual?

The name 'Malki-Tzedek' combines 'melek' (king) and 'tzedek' (righteousness), and he rules from 'Shalem' - the root of both Jerusalem and shalom. What does this cluster of meanings suggest about the kind of rule his name and title portray?

Melchizedek's unique priest-king role, later picked up in Psalm 110 as a type of the Messiah's eternal priesthood outside the Levitical line, stands behind Matthew 7's teaching about the wise builder whose house stands on rock. How do both point toward something more lasting than the ordinary order of things?
Proverbs 7 warns a young man about being lured off course by the seductive path. What is one boundary or association you could examine this week to guard your own steps?

Abram refuses any spoils from the king of Sodom, 'so you cannot say, I have made Abram rich,' yet freely tithes to Melchizedek. Discuss what this reveals about Abram's view of blessing and reputation, and how it compares with Yeshua's warning in Matthew 7 about knowing a tree by its fruit.

theme: Faith Reckoned as Righteousness Before the Law

questions.text: Genesis 15:6 states that 'Abram believed in Adonai, and He credited it to him as righteousness' - before Abram is circumcised or given any commandment to keep. Why might it matter that this declaration comes purely on the basis of trust?
The Hebrew verb 'he'emin' (believed/trusted) in Genesis 15:6 shares its root with the word 'amen.' What does that shared root suggest about the nature of the trust Abram is exercising?

Abram's faith is credited to him as righteousness in Genesis 15, prior to any law-keeping. In Matthew 8, Yeshua marvels at a Roman centurion's faith, saying, 'I have not found such great faith in Israel.' What do both accounts suggest about faith as the basis on which God reckons righteousness?

Proverbs 8 pictures wisdom calling out publicly in the streets and gates, available to anyone who will listen. What is one way you could actively seek out wisdom this week rather than waiting for it to find you?

In Genesis 16, Hagar, an Egyptian servant mistreated by Sarai, becomes the first person in Scripture to give God a name - 'El Roi,' the God who sees me. Discuss what her story of flight, encounter, and return suggests about God's care for the marginalized and for those outside Abraham's immediate household.

theme: Covenant Sealed, Laughter Turned to Healing Faith

questions.text: In Genesis 17, Adonai renames Abram and Sarai as Abraham and Sarah and institutes circumcision as the sign of the covenant; in chapter 18, Sarah laughs to herself at the promise of a son in her old age. Notice how these two chapters pair a covenant sign with a very human reaction of doubt.

The covenant sign given in Genesis 17 is called 'brit milah,' the covenant of circumcision - a mark cut into the flesh itself. What might it mean that Abraham's covenant with Adonai is sealed in the body rather than only spoken or written?

In Matthew 9, Yeshua tells a paralyzed man 'your sins are forgiven,' prompting the question, 'who can forgive sins but God alone?' How does this authority compare to the impossible, laughter-inducing promise given to elderly, barren Sarah - both point to a power beyond ordinary human capacity.
Yeshua says that new wine needs new wineskins (Matthew 9:17). What old pattern or assumption might need to give way this week for you to receive what God wants to do?

Abraham boldly negotiates with Adonai over Sodom's fate, asking, 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justly?' Discuss what this dialogue suggests about the nature and possibility of intercessory prayer, and compare it with Yeshua's authority to forgive sins and heal in Matthew 9.

theme: Sodom's Judgment and the Sending of the Twelve

questions.text: In Genesis 19, Lot's wife looks back toward Sodom and becomes a pillar of salt, even after the angels physically grab the family's hands to hurry them out. What does her hesitation reveal about the pull of what is being left behind?

Lot pleads to flee to the small town of Zoar rather than the mountains - its very name means 'small' or 'little thing.' What might this detail suggest about Lot's continuing reluctance to fully trust Adonai's rescue?

Sending out the twelve in Matthew 10:15, Yeshua says it will be 'more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah' on judgment day than for towns that reject His messengers. Why might Yeshua reach for this particular historical judgment as His comparison?

Proverbs 10 contrasts the diligent hand with the lazy one and the tongue of the righteous with that of the wicked. Pick one specific contrast from this chapter and name a way to practice its wiser side this week.

Genesis 19-20 includes the disturbing episode of Lot's daughters and Abraham again passing Sarah off as his sister to Abimelech, repeating the same deception from chapter 12. Discuss what it means that Scripture records its major figures' failures so plainly rather than smoothing them over.

theme: The Binding of Isaac and Rest for the Weary

questions.text: In Genesis 22, Abraham tells his servants 'we will worship and then we will come back to you' - plural - even though he believes he is about to sacrifice Isaac. What does that small pronoun suggest about what Abraham expected God to do?

Abraham names the place of the near-sacrifice 'Adonai Yireh' - Adonai will see, or provide - from the root 'ra'ah,' to see. How does this name reframe the whole ordeal, given his earlier answer to Isaac that 'God will provide the lamb'?

The binding of Isaac takes place on Mount Moriah, later identified with Jerusalem's Temple Mount, where a father offers his beloved son and a ram is provided as a substitute. How does this scene anticipate Matthew 11:28, where Yeshua invites the weary and burdened to find rest in Him?

Abraham rises 'early in the morning' to obey, despite not understanding how the promise to Isaac could survive the command he'd been given. What is one act of obedience you could take this week even without full understanding?

Discuss the moral and theological weight of God testing Abraham by commanding Isaac's sacrifice. How do we hold together the horror of the command with the way the narrative resolves, and what does the episode teach about the nature and cost of faith?

theme: A Bride Sought for Isaac, Something Greater Than Jonah

questions.text: In Genesis 23, Abraham insists on paying full price for the cave of Machpelah rather than accepting it as a gift from the Hittites. What might this insistence reveal about why he wanted a clear, undisputed legal claim to land in Canaan?

'Machpelah,' the name of the cave Abraham purchases as a burial site, means 'double' or 'doubled.' Considering it becomes the burial place for multiple patriarchs and matriarchs, what significance might this name carry?

In Matthew 12, Yeshua declares 'something greater than Jonah is here' and 'something greater than the Temple is here.' How does Genesis 24's careful, prayerful search for a bride for Isaac, carrying the covenant line forward, anticipate a story that culminates in someone 'greater' still?

The servant in Genesis 24 prays specifically for guidance and watches for Adonai's answer at the well. What is one decision this week where you could pray with that same specificity rather than a vague request?

Abraham insists his son not marry a Canaanite woman but instead find a wife from his own kindred, while the servant relies on a very specific answered prayer to identify Rebekah. Discuss the balance this account suggests between practical wisdom and depending on God's guidance in major life decisions.

theme: Esau's Birthright Traded for a Bowl of Stew

questions.text: In Genesis 25:34, Esau sells his birthright to Jacob for a bowl of red stew, and the text bluntly comments, 'thus Esau despised his birthright.' What details in the scene suggest this was less about desperation and more about settled disregard?

The Hebrew word 'bechorah' (birthright) that Esau trades away entailed a double inheritance portion, family leadership, and a share in the covenant blessing. Why might it matter that Esau is condemned for 'despising' this specific inheritance?

Matthew 13's parables compare the kingdom of heaven to a hidden treasure and a pearl of great price, worth selling everything to obtain. How does Esau's willingness to trade something of lasting worth for momentary appetite stand in direct contrast to the wisdom these parables commend?

In Genesis 26, Isaac responds to conflict over wells by moving on and redigging rather than fighting the Philistines for territory. What is one conflict this week where a similarly patient response might serve you better than insisting on your claim?

Discuss the ethics of Jacob taking advantage of his brother's hunger to extract the birthright. What does the narrative's largely neutral tone toward Jacob's shrewdness suggest, and how should readers evaluate morally ambiguous patriarchal figures who nonetheless carry forward God's covenant plan?

theme: Jacob's Ladder and Bread Multiplied in the Wilderness

questions.text: In Genesis 28, Jacob dreams of a stairway reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending, and Adonai renews to him the covenant promise given to Abraham and Isaac. Notice that Jacob is alone, fleeing his brother's anger, when this vision comes - what does the timing suggest?
Jacob names the place of his vision 'Beit-El' - house of God - renaming what had been called Luz. Why might this renaming matter for how the site would be remembered by later generations of Israel?

Yeshua later tells Natan'el he will see 'heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man,' directly alluding to Jacob's vision. How does Matthew 14's account of Yeshua feeding five thousand in a desolate place echo God's provision meeting Jacob, a fugitive alone in the wilderness?
Jacob vows, 'if God will be with me... then Adonai will be my God.' What is one unexpected or 'wilderness' place in your life this week where you could deliberately notice and name God's presence?

Discuss the deception Jacob and Rebekah practice on the blind, aging Isaac to steal Esau's blessing. How does the narrative hold Jacob accountable for this through his subsequent decades of exile and being deceived himself by Laban, and what does this teach about God working through flawed people without excusing their wrongdoing?

theme: Leah's Longing and a Gentile Woman's Persistent Faith

questions.text: In Genesis 29, Jacob is deceived by Laban into marrying Leah instead of Rachel - the deceiver deceived, in a kind of poetic justice. Notice how Leah names her first sons in a way that traces her longing to be loved by Jacob.

Leah names her fourth son 'Yehudah' (Judah), meaning 'praise,' saying, 'this time I will praise Adonai.' Why might it be significant that the tribe carrying the messianic line begins with a name rooted in praise rather than vindication?

In Matthew 15, a Canaanite woman's persistent faith - 'even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters' table' - earns Yeshua's commendation of her 'great faith.' How does God's grace reaching this Gentile woman parallel His care for Leah, the unloved wife through whom the line of Judah nonetheless comes?
Yeshua teaches in Matthew 15:11 that it is not what goes into the mouth that defiles a person but what comes out of it. What is one specific way you could guard your speech this week?

Discuss the painful family dynamics of Jacob's household - two sisters competing for their husband's love through childbearing, even using their servants as surrogates. What does this account suggest about the consequences of favoritism and polygamy, themes that resurface in the Joseph story soon to come?

theme: Wrestling at Peniel, Confessing the Messiah

questions.text: In Genesis 32, Jacob wrestles all night with a mysterious 'man' until daybreak, refuses to let go without a blessing, and comes away renamed and limping. Notice both the physical cost and the timing, right before facing Esau.

The name 'Yisra'el' (Israel) is explained within the text itself as 'you have struggled with God and with men and have prevailed.' What does it mean that the nation's name comes from a wrestling match rather than a straightforward victory?

Kefa's confession in Matthew 16, 'You are the Messiah, the Son of the living God,' marks a pivotal turning point of recognition. How does this moment parallel Jacob receiving a new name and identity at Peniel, 'for I have seen God face to face'?

Before meeting Esau, Jacob prays an honest, specific, fearful prayer (Genesis 32:9-12), naming exactly what he dreads. What is one feared conversation this week where you could pray that specifically rather than vaguely?

Discuss the strange, physical wrestling match between Jacob and the divine figure - who initiates it, what it costs Jacob, and why the blessing only comes after a costly struggle. Compare this with Yeshua's call in Matthew 16:24 to take up one's cross.

theme: Reconciled with Esau, Transfigured on the Mountain

questions.text: In Genesis 33, Jacob bows seven times before Esau and tells him, 'to see your face is like seeing the face of God' - deliberately echoing his encounter at Peniel. Notice how this peaceful reunion is followed immediately by the violence of chapter 34.

The Hebrew word 'panim' (face) links Jacob seeing God's face at Peniel (32:30) with seeing Esau's face 'like the face of God' (33:10). What might this repeated word suggest about how Jacob is processing both encounters?

At the Transfiguration in Matthew 17, Moses and Elijah appear with Yeshua and a voice declares, 'This is My beloved Son... listen to Him.' How does this glory that changes the appearance of Yeshua's face connect to the pattern of divine encounters transforming a person, as with Jacob at Peniel?

Jacob approaches his wronged brother first, with humility and generous gifts, rather than waiting to be approached. What is one relationship this week where you could take that same first step toward reconciliation?

Discuss the brutal response of Simeon and Levi to Shechem's violation of Dinah in Genesis 34 - deceiving the Shechemites into circumcision and then slaughtering them. How does the text, along with Jacob's later rebuke of this act in Genesis 49, evaluate this 'justice'?

theme: Rachel's Death Near Bethlehem, Forgiving Without Limit

questions.text: In Genesis 35, Rachel dies giving birth near Bethlehem, naming her son with her last breath 'Ben-oni' (son of my sorrow), which Jacob then renames 'Binyamin.' Notice how father and dying mother give the same child two very different names.

Rachel's name 'Ben-oni' (son of my sorrow) is overruled by Jacob's renaming, 'Binyamin' (son of the right hand, or of strength). What does this shift from grief to favor suggest about how loss and hope can be held together?

Matthew 18's parable of the lost sheep declares 'it is not the will of your Father that one of these little ones should perish.' How does this assurance of God's care resonate with a son born out of his mother's death and immediately renamed from sorrow to strength?

Matthew 18 teaches forgiving not seven times but seventy times seven, through the parable of the unforgiving servant. What is one concrete step you could take this week to extend forgiveness to someone who has genuinely wronged you?

Reuben's sin with Bilhah is mentioned in a single verse (35:22) yet carries lasting consequences, costing him his firstborn status (Genesis 49:3-4). Discuss what this teaches about how consequences for sin can unfold slowly, and about the tension between mercy and justice within a family.

theme: Joseph Sold by His Brothers, All Things Possible

questions.text: In Genesis 37, Joseph's two dreams - sheaves bowing, then sun, moon, and stars bowing - provoke his brothers' escalating hatred, especially alongside his ornamented tunic. Notice how the coat meant to mark him as favored becomes the false evidence used to convince Jacob his son is dead.

Joseph's 'ketonet passim' - his ornamented tunic - is the same type of garment worn by royal daughters elsewhere in Scripture (2 Samuel 13:18). What might this detail suggest about how deliberately Jacob was setting Joseph apart from his brothers?

Yeshua tells the rich young ruler in Matthew 19:26, 'with man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible.' How does Joseph's reversal - from a pit and slavery to becoming Egypt's second-in-command - embody this same truth?

Judah, confronted by Tamar in Genesis 38, is forced to admit 'she is more righteous than I' after judging her harshly while hiding his own failure. What is one area this week where you could be more honest about a hidden failure of your own before judging someone else's?

Discuss the disturbing, complicated story of Judah and Tamar tucked into the middle of the Joseph narrative - Tamar's resourcefulness in securing the justice denied her, and Judah's public confession. What does this account contribute to the larger story leading toward King David and the Messiah?

theme: Joseph's Integrity in Prison, Greatness Through Serving

questions.text: Genesis 39 repeats the phrase 'Adonai was with Joseph' both in Potiphar's house and later in prison, even as his circumstances objectively worsen. Notice how Joseph's success is described as coming through God's presence rather than through changing circumstances.

When resisting Potiphar's wife, Joseph asks, 'how then could I do this great evil, and sin against God?' Consider how his appeal to sin against God, rather than merely against Potiphar, reframes what is really at stake.

Yeshua says in Matthew 20:28 that 'the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many.' How does Joseph's pattern of faithful service while wrongly imprisoned, and his eventual position to save many, anticipate this servant-path to exaltation?

Joseph resists temptation while entirely alone and unwatched, with no one to hold him accountable in the moment. What is one area of personal integrity this week where you could practice that same standard?

Joseph remains forgotten in prison for two full years after correctly interpreting the cupbearer's dream, who simply forgets him (Genesis 40:23). Discuss what this delay teaches about the gap between righteous faithfulness and immediate reward, and how to hold onto trust in God's timing.

theme: Joseph Exalted in Egypt, the King Enters Jerusalem

questions.text: In Genesis 41, Pharaoh's two dreams of fat and lean cows lead to Joseph's rise from prisoner to second-in-command of Egypt in a single day. Notice how completely his brothers' earlier dreams of sheaves and stars bowing come true in chapters 42-43, though they don't recognize him.

Joseph names his two Egyptian-born sons 'Menashe' (God has made me forget) and 'Ephraim' (God has made me fruitful), interpreting his own suffering theologically even before his brothers arrive. What does this naming reveal about how Joseph made sense of his hardship?

Matthew 21 records Yeshua's triumphal entry into Jerusalem, hailed by crowds who will later turn against Him. How does Joseph - an exalted ruler initially unrecognized by his own brothers, who must eventually bow before him - anticipate this pattern of a deliverer not recognized by his own?
Joseph names his son Ephraim because 'God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.' What is one current hardship you could try reframing this week as a place where God may yet bring fruit?

Discuss why Joseph tests his brothers - accusing them of spying, demanding Benjamin's presence - rather than revealing himself the moment he recognizes them. What does this drawn-out process suggest about discerning genuine repentance versus rushing toward reconciliation?

theme: Judah's Substitution and Joseph's Tearful Revelation

questions.text: In Genesis 44, Judah delivers an impassioned speech offering himself as a substitute slave in Benjamin's place, to spare their father Jacob further grief. Notice in chapter 45 how loudly Joseph weeps before revealing himself, so that the Egyptians outside hear it.

Joseph tells his brothers that God sent him ahead 'l'michyah' - to preserve life, or for survival. How does this word reframe his entire ordeal, from the pit to Egypt, as purposeful rather than merely tragic?

Psalm 22 opens with the cry Yeshua quotes from the cross, 'My God, my God, why have You forsaken me?' and moves from vivid suffering to worldwide vindication and praise. How does Judah's willingness to substitute himself for Benjamin foreshadow, however imperfectly, the pattern of suffering turned to deliverance that his own tribe's greater son will one day fulfill?
Joseph forgives his brothers despite the real, deep betrayal they inflicted on him. What is one step you could take this week to extend grace to someone who has genuinely wronged you?

Joseph tells his brothers in 45:5-8, 'it was not you who sent me here, but God.' Discuss how this statement shapes a biblical view of providence and human responsibility together - does it excuse the brothers' original sin, or simply reframe its ultimate outcome?

theme: Israel Settles in Goshen, Yeshua Weeps Over Jerusalem

questions.text: In Genesis 46, Adonai speaks to Jacob in a night vision at Beersheba, telling him not to be afraid to go to Egypt because 'I will go down with you... and I will also surely bring you back up.' Notice the list of seventy persons who make this journey together.

The family settles in 'Goshen,' a fertile region set apart because Egyptians considered shepherds detestable (Genesis 46:34). What significance might this deliberate separation carry for preserving the family's distinct identity in a foreign land?

Yeshua laments over the city in Matthew 23:37, 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often I wanted to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings.' How does this pattern of longing and promised return echo Adonai's assurance to Jacob that going down to Egypt would not be the end of the story?
Joseph uses his position of authority to provide generously for his family during the famine. What is one way you could be a source of provision or generosity for family or community this week?

Discuss the deliberate separation of Jacob's family into Goshen, kept apart from Egyptian culture because of their profession as shepherds. What does this early example of maintaining distinct identity while living in a foreign land suggest about the ongoing question of Jewish identity amid diaspora and assimilation?

theme: Jacob's Blessing on Judah and the Coming Shiloh

questions.text: In Genesis 48, Jacob deliberately crosses his hands to bless the younger Ephraim above the older Manasseh, despite Joseph's attempt to correct him, saying only, 'I know, my son, I know.' Notice how chapter 49 continues this pattern of upended expectation across all twelve blessings.

Genesis 49:10 promises the scepter will not depart from Judah 'until Shiloh comes' - a famously debated term long read by Jewish interpreters as pointing to a future ruler. What do you make of the ambiguity surrounding this word, given how central it became to later messianic expectation?

Genesis 49:10's prophecy that the ruler's staff will remain with Judah until Shiloh comes, and 'the obedience of the peoples is his,' stands as a foundational messianic prophecy pointing to Yeshua's Davidic and Judahic lineage. How does this ancient promise connect with Yeshua's own teaching in Matthew 24 about His future return?

Jacob blesses the younger son above the older, deliberately reversing the expected order. What is one place this week where you might need to hold your own expectations of order or seniority loosely, trusting God's plans instead?

Jacob's deathbed blessings vary widely - Reuben's instability named, Simeon and Levi's violence condemned, Judah's exaltation announced. Discuss how these blessings reflect each son's earlier actions in the narrative, and what this teaches about blessing as also being an honest reckoning with character and consequence.

theme: Joseph's Enduring Faith and Faithful Stewardship

questions.text: In Genesis 50, Joseph's dying instruction is that his bones be carried out of Egypt whenever God brings deliverance - a promise not fulfilled for centuries. In Exodus 1, the midwives Shiphrah and Puah defy Pharaoh's order to kill Hebrew infant boys because they 'feared God.'

Joseph tells his brothers in Genesis 50:20, 'you meant (chashav) evil against me, but God meant (chashav) it for good' - using the same Hebrew verb for both human and divine intention. What does sharing this one word for two very different intentions suggest about how providence works?

Matthew 25's parables of the sheep and goats and of the talents both turn on faithful action taken at personal risk or cost. How does the midwives' risky, faithful defiance of Pharaoh's decree, and Joseph's confidence that deliverance was still coming even in death, embody the same kind of faithful stewardship these parables commend?

The parable of the ten virgins stresses keeping oil in reserve and staying ready. What is one specific way you could prepare or stay spiritually ready this week rather than waiting until the moment demands it?

Joseph's statement that God meant his suffering 'for good, to bring about... the saving of many lives' is immediately followed in Exodus 1 by 'a new king arose who did not know Joseph.' Discuss what this sudden reversal from favor to oppression teaches about how quickly a minority community's security can change, and how the vulnerable ought to be treated.

theme: The Burning Bush and the Last Supper's New Covenant

questions.text: In Exodus 3, the bush burns but is not consumed, and Moses is told to remove his sandals because the ground he stands on is holy. Notice what draws Moses to the bush in the first place - not the fire, but the fact that it isn't being consumed.

Adonai reveals His name to Moses as 'Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh' - I AM WHO I AM, or I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE - the root behind the Tetragrammaton, YHVH. What does this open-ended, self-existent name suggest about who God is?

At the Last Supper in Matthew 26, Yeshua says of the cup, 'this is My blood of the covenant.' How does this covenant-making meal connect to the God who reveals Himself at the burning bush as the One who has 'surely seen the affliction of My people' and is about to act to deliver them?
Moses offers a string of excuses at the burning bush - 'who am I,' 'they will not believe me.' What is one call or task this week you could obey despite feeling inadequate to it?

Moses kills an Egyptian and flees Egypt in Exodus 2, yet decades later this same flawed man is called as Israel's deliverer. Alongside Kefa's denial of Yeshua in Matthew 26, discuss what these accounts teach about how God works through people's failures rather than requiring a flawless record before using them.

theme: Moses Confronts Pharaoh, Yeshua Forsaken on the Cross

questions.text: In the strange episode at the lodging place (Exodus 4:24-26), Adonai seeks to kill Moses, and Zipporah circumcises their son, touching his feet with the blood and calling Moses 'a bridegroom of blood.' Notice how abruptly and briefly this unsettling scene is told.

Zipporah's phrase 'chatan damim' - bridegroom of blood - ties this puzzling episode to the sign of circumcision and covenant. What might this phrase suggest about the seriousness with which Adonai treats covenant obligations, even for His chosen deliverer?

Matthew 27 records Yeshua quoting Psalm 22 from the cross, 'My God, my God, why have You forsaken Me,' amid mockery and apparent defeat. How does Pharaoh's response of increasing the Israelites' burden right after Moses' first confrontation - making things worse before they get better - parallel the pattern of the cross appearing as ultimate defeat before resurrection reverses it?

The Israelites grow angry at Moses when Pharaoh demands bricks without straw, right after their hope had been raised. What is one situation this week where things may get harder right after a step of obedience, and how could you keep trusting despite that?

Discuss why God allows the Israelites' suffering to intensify immediately after Moses' first confrontation with Pharaoh, rather than granting swift relief. Compare this with the apparent delay and silence of God during Yeshua's arrest and crucifixion - what does this pattern suggest about the relationship between obedience, suffering, and God's timing?

theme: God's Name Reaffirmed, the Tomb Found Empty

questions.text: In Exodus 6, Adonai tells Moses, 'I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as El Shaddai, but by My name Adonai I did not make Myself known to them.' Notice in chapter 7 how Aaron's staff, turned into a snake, swallows the staffs of Egypt's own magicians.

God contrasts His self-revelation to the patriarchs as 'El Shaddai' with the fuller revelation of His name 'Adonai' (YHVH) now given to Moses. What might this progressive unveiling of God's name suggest about how God reveals Himself gradually across generations?

Yeshua's Great Commission in Matthew 28 - 'go and make talmidim of all nations... I am with you always' - is a commissioning built on promised presence. How does this parallel Adonai's reaffirmed covenant promise to Moses, 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God'?

Moses grows discouraged when Pharaoh's response worsens Israel's situation and the people won't listen 'because of their broken spirit' (Exodus 6:9). What is one place this week where you might need to persist in obedience even though your message or effort isn't initially well received?

The plague turning the Nile to blood directly confronted Egypt's worship of the Nile and its god Hapi. Compare this with the guards at Yeshua's tomb being bribed to spread a false story in Matthew 28:11-15 - what do both accounts suggest about how entrenched power responds to undeniable displays of God's activity?

theme: Plagues on Egypt, the Kingdom Breaking In

questions.text: In Exodus 8, Pharaoh's magicians can replicate the plague of frogs but cannot reproduce the gnats, forcing them to admit, 'this is the finger of God.' Notice too how God distinguishes Goshen from the rest of Egypt, sparing it from the plagues of flies, livestock disease, and hail.

The magicians' admission 'etzba Elohim' - the finger of God - in Exodus 8:19 marks the moment even Pharaoh's own experts concede a power beyond their skill. What does it mean that this concession comes from Pharaoh's own court rather than from Israel?

In Mark 1, unclean spirits immediately recognize and cry out against Yeshua's authority - 'I know who You are, the Holy One of God!' How does this instant recognition compare with Pharaoh's magicians being forced to acknowledge 'the finger of God' at work against them?

God distinguishes and protects Goshen even while plagues devastate the rest of Egypt. What is one area of turmoil around you this week where you could trust God's protective care rather than simply absorbing the surrounding anxiety?

Pharaoh repeatedly hardens his heart, briefly relents, then reverses course after each plague, while in Mark 1 a whole town gathers eagerly at Yeshua's door for healing, yet He withdraws to pray alone. Discuss what these contrasting responses to divine power suggest about different postures toward God.

theme: Approaching Judgment and the Son of Man's Authority

questions.text: In Exodus 10, Pharaoh's own officials plead with him to let Israel go before the locusts finish devastating the land. Notice too the darkness in verse 21 that could actually 'be felt' for three days, while the Israelites in Goshen had light the whole time.

Proverbs 30:4 asks, 'Who has ascended to heaven and come down?... What is His name, and what is His son's name, if you know?' What kind of answer do you think Agur expected to this riddle-like question, and why leave it open?

Agur's ancient riddle in Proverbs 30:4 about one who ascends and descends from heaven, and his son's name, strikingly anticipates Mark 2, where Yeshua, as 'Son of Man,' claims authority on earth to forgive sins and declares Himself 'Lord even of the Sabbath.' How does Mark 2 answer Agur's centuries-old question?
In Mark 2:1-5, four friends tear open a roof to lower a paralyzed man down to Yeshua. What is one way you could show that same persistent, resourceful faith on behalf of someone else this week?

Pharaoh's own officials pragmatically ask, 'do you not yet realize that Egypt is ruined?' yet Pharaoh remains hardened, just as the religious leaders in Mark 2 object to Yeshua eating with tax collectors and to His Sabbath actions. Discuss what both scenes suggest about how entrenched authority can resist even overwhelming evidence when it threatens power or tradition.

theme: Passover Deliverance and a Life of Valor

questions.text: In Exodus 12, the Israelites are told to eat the Passover meal with cloaks tucked in, sandals on, and staff in hand. What does this posture of readiness suggest about how Adonai wanted His people to experience that first night of freedom?
The word 'Pesach' means 'to pass over' or 'to spare.' How does knowing this root meaning shape your understanding of the blood applied to the doorposts in Exodus 12:13?

The Passover lamb had to be without blemish, its blood applied so the household would be spared (Exodus 12:5-7). How does this foreshadow Yeshua as our Passover Lamb, even as today's reading in Mark 3 shows him teaching and healing?

Proverbs 31's woman of valor is praised for strength, wisdom, and fearless provision for her household. What is one concrete way you can bring that same steady courage into your responsibilities this week?

In Mark 3:6, the Pharisees and Herodians begin plotting Yeshua's death right after he heals a man's withered hand on Shabbat. What does it say about the human heart when an act of compassion becomes the trigger for conspiracy?

theme: Through the Sea and the Song That Followed

questions.text: Exodus 15:20-21 shows Miriam the prophetess leading the women in dance and tambourine after the sea closed over Pharaoh's army. Why might it matter that this celebration of deliverance was led by a woman?

The Song of the Sea asks, 'Mi kamocha ba'elim, Adonai?' - 'Who is like You, Adonai, among the mighty?' (Exodus 15:11). What does this question reveal about how Israel understood Adonai compared to Egypt's gods?
When Yeshua calms the storm in Mark 4:39-41 and the disciples ask, 'Who then is this, that even wind and sea obey him?' how does this echo the sea's obedience to Adonai at the Reed Sea?
Israel had to move forward toward the sea before the way was fully clear. Where in your life this week do you need to step out in faith before you can see the whole outcome?

Only days after singing the Song of the Sea, Israel grumbles about bitter water at Marah (Exodus 15:22-24). Why doesn't dramatic deliverance automatically produce lasting trust, and what does that suggest about the difference between a mountaintop moment and settled faith?

theme: Manna, the Struck Rock, and Amalek's Attack

questions.text: Aaron and Hur stood on either side of Moses, holding up his hands until sunset so Joshua's army would prevail over Amalek (Exodus 17:12). What does this detail reveal about the nature of spiritual leadership and the need for support?

When Israel first saw manna, they asked 'man hu?' - 'what is it?' - which became the substance's name (Exodus 16:15). What does this bewilderment suggest about how unfamiliar God's provision can look?

Moses strikes the rock at Massah and Meribah and water pours out for the people (Exodus 17:6). How does this life-giving water from a struck rock anticipate Yeshua, later described as the spiritual Rock that accompanied Israel?

Israel was told to gather only enough manna for one day, except before Shabbat. What would it look like for you to trust God for 'daily bread' this week rather than trying to secure more than you need?

The Gerasene demoniac in Mark 5 lives uncontrollably among the tombs until Yeshua restores him. Compare his instability to Israel's own quickness to panic in Exodus 16-17 despite recent deliverance - what does true restoration require in each case?

theme: Wise Counsel at Sinai, Rejection at Nazareth

questions.text: Jethro, Moses' Midianite father-in-law, advises him to appoint capable men as judges over thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens (Exodus 18:21). What does it mean that this crucial organizational wisdom came from someone outside Israel?
At Sinai, Adonai calls Israel His 'segulah' - a treasured possession - among all peoples (Exodus 19:5). What does this word convey about Israel's relationship to God beyond mere obedience?

Adonai promises Israel will be 'a kingdom of priests and a holy nation' (Exodus 19:6). How does Yeshua's compassion for the crowds in Mark 6:34, 'like sheep without a shepherd,' reflect God's ongoing desire to shepherd His treasured people well?
Moses nearly wore himself out judging every dispute alone until Jethro intervened. Is there a burden you're carrying alone that needs to be shared or delegated?

Yeshua is rejected in his hometown synagogue: 'a prophet is not without honor except in his hometown' (Mark 6:4). Why might familiarity breed unbelief, and have you seen this dynamic in your own community or family?

theme: The Ten Words and What Truly Defiles

questions.text: After hearing the voice from the mountain, the people trembled and asked Moses to speak to them instead of God directly (Exodus 20:18-19). What does their fear reveal about the weight of encountering Adonai's holiness firsthand?
In Hebrew the Ten Commandments are called 'Aseret HaDibrot' - the Ten Words, not simply a legal code. How might thinking of them as spoken words rather than a checklist change how you approach them?

Yeshua teaches in Mark 7:15 that it is not what goes into a person that defiles but what comes from the heart. How does this get at the same root concern as the Ten Words given at Sinai, which govern the heart's posture, not merely external acts?
Proverbs 4:23 says, 'guard your heart, for from it flow the springs of life.' What is one heart-level, not just behavioral, change you sense you need to make this week?

In Mark 7, Yeshua confronts the practice of 'Corban,' where a vow could override caring for one's parents, calling it a tradition that nullifies God's Word. Where might well-intentioned tradition today crowd out Torah's deeper intent?

theme: Justice for the Stranger, Confession of the Messiah

questions.text: Exodus 23:9 commands, 'do not oppress a stranger, since you yourselves know the feelings of a stranger, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt.' What makes this shared memory of suffering a distinctive basis for ethics?
The Hebrew word 'ger' - stranger or sojourner - appears repeatedly in these chapters regarding just treatment. How does Israel's own history as 'ger' in Egypt shape the weight of this command?

Kefa's confession, 'You are the Mashiach,' in Mark 8:29 is the turning point of Mark's Gospel. How does Adonai's promise in Exodus 23:20-23 to send a messenger bearing His very Name ahead of Israel anticipate a coming figure who fully carries God's presence?
Exodus 22-23 repeatedly protects the poor, the widow, the orphan, and the sojourner. Who is one vulnerable person in your circle you can concretely serve this week?

Right after correctly confessing Yeshua as Messiah, Kefa rebukes him for predicting his suffering (Mark 8:31-33). What does this gap between right confession and full understanding suggest about how faith can be genuine yet incomplete?

theme: Covenant Blood, the Mercy Seat, and Transfigured Glory

questions.text: Exodus 24:9-11 describes Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders going up the mountain where they saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence. Why is it striking that this encounter is described so plainly, without terror?

The gold cover of the ark, where blood was sprinkled on Yom Kippur, is called the 'kapporet' - related to the root for atonement or covering. What does its placement beneath the cherubim suggest about where God's presence and forgiveness meet?

On the mountain of transfiguration, Yeshua appears in dazzling glory alongside Moses and Elijah (Mark 9:2-8), echoing the Sinai theophany of Exodus 24. How do these two mountain scenes together deepen your understanding of who Yeshua is?
The elders saw God and simply ate and drank - fellowship with Him included ordinary table communion. What would it look like for you to sense God's presence in an unspectacular moment this week?

Mark 9:43-47 records Yeshua's hyperbolic instruction to cut off a hand or pluck out an eye that causes sin. How should we interpret such extreme language responsibly, and what does it reveal about how seriously he takes sin's danger?

theme: Behind the Veil, and the Cost of Following

questions.text: The inner veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place was woven with cherubim in blue, purple, and scarlet (Exodus 26:31-33). What does the craftsmanship invested in a barrier meant to restrict access suggest about the weight of God's holiness?

The tabernacle is called the 'mishkan,' from the root 'shakan' - to dwell, the same root behind the later term 'Shekinah' for God's manifest presence. What does this name tell you about the structure's purpose?

The bronze altar stood at the courtyard's entrance, so every worshiper passed sacrifice before going further (Exodus 27:1-8). How does the rich young ruler's unwillingness to give up his wealth in Mark 10:17-22 illustrate the real cost of approaching God fully rather than partway?
Access to the tabernacle's inner spaces was tightly restricted and costly. In light of that, does your reverence match the ease with which you can approach God today?

Yeshua's teaching on divorce in Mark 10:2-12 is famously difficult. How do you hold together his high view of marriage's original design with pastoral compassion for those whose marriages have broken?

theme: Priestly Garments and a House of Prayer

questions.text: The high priest's breastpiece held twelve stones, one engraved for each tribe, worn over his heart whenever he entered the sanctuary (Exodus 28:29). What does it mean that he literally carried the names of all Israel close to his heart before God?
The gold plate on the high priest's turban read 'Kadosh L'Adonai' - Holy to Adonai (Exodus 28:36). Why place this declaration on the forehead, the most visible part of the priest?

When Yeshua overturns the tables in the Temple he quotes Isaiah, saying it should be 'a house of prayer for all nations' (Mark 11:17). How does this connect to the priesthood's role in Exodus 28-29 of representing all the community before God?
The high priest bore the tribes' names on his shoulders and heart. Who are the people you are called to carry before God in prayer this week?

Mark 11 pairs the cursing of a fruitless fig tree with the cleansing of the Temple. What do you think this pairing communicates about fruitlessness in worship, and how do we discuss it without unfairly caricaturing Israel's institutions rather than the specific corrupt leadership of that moment?

theme: Anointed for Service, the Greatest Commandment

questions.text: Bezalel and Oholiab are described as filled with the Ruach of God with skill and craftsmanship to build the tabernacle's furnishings (Exodus 31:1-6). What does it suggest that artistic and technical skill is described here as a Spirit-given gift?
Adonai calls the Sabbath an 'ot' - a sign - forever between Himself and Israel (Exodus 31:13,17). What does calling Shabbat a sign, rather than merely a rule, suggest about its ongoing purpose?

Psalm 40:6-8 declares, 'sacrifice and offering You did not desire... I delight to do Your will, O my God.' How does this anticipate Yeshua's answer in Mark 12:29-31, naming love of God and neighbor as the greatest commandments?
The widow in Mark 12:41-44 gave two small coins - all she had - while others gave from surplus. What would sacrificial, not merely convenient, generosity look like for you this week?

In Mark 12:35-37, Yeshua raises the puzzle of Psalm 110: how can the Messiah be both David's son and David's Lord? Why does this question matter, and how would you explain it to someone unfamiliar with the psalm?

theme: The Golden Calf and the Mercy That Follows Judgment

questions.text: When confronted about the golden calf, Aaron claims, 'I threw the gold into the fire, and out came this calf,' as though it appeared on its own (Exodus 32:24). What does this evasion reveal about how people minimize responsibility for their choices?

After the calf incident, Adonai proclaims His own character to Moses: 'Adonai, Adonai, El rachum v'chanun' - merciful and gracious, slow to anger (Exodus 34:6-7). Why might this self-revelation come precisely after Israel's worst failure so far?

Moses' face shone after being in Adonai's presence, so much that he wore a veil before the people (Exodus 34:29-35). How might this glimpse of veiled, reflected glory deepen your appreciation for Messiah's unveiled radiance shown at the transfiguration?
Moses interceded fervently for Israel even after their grave betrayal with the calf. Is there someone whose failure you're tempted to write off whom you could intercede for this week?

Moses smashes the stone tablets in anger (Exodus 32:19), yet Adonai's mercy is proclaimed shortly after in chapter 34. How do righteous anger and extended mercy coexist here, and what does that teach about processing our own anger toward wrongdoing?

theme: Overflowing Generosity and the Night of Betrayal

questions.text: Exodus 36:6-7 records that Moses had to command the people to stop bringing offerings for the tabernacle because they had brought more than enough - a rare moment of overgiving in Scripture. What does this scene suggest about generosity that is freely, not reluctantly, given?

Those who brought materials for the tabernacle are described as having a 'nadiv lev' - a willing or generous heart (Exodus 35:5,21-22). How does this phrase capture something different from mere obligation?

At the Last Supper, Yeshua reinterprets the Passover bread and cup as his body and blood of the covenant (Mark 14:22-24). How does his voluntary, wholehearted self-giving mirror the 'nadiv lev' generosity of Exodus 35, even as Judas simultaneously bargains his betrayal for silver?
Consider your own pattern of giving - time, resources, attention to God. Does it more resemble the restrained-only-because-overflowing generosity of Exodus 36, or something more grudging?

Peter, so confident he would never deny Yeshua, does so three times within hours (Mark 14:66-72). What is the difference between communal, high-energy devotion and the private test of faith under real pressure?

theme: Finished Furnishings and a Torn Veil

questions.text: Exodus 38:24-31 gives a precise, transparent accounting of exactly how much gold, silver, and bronze were used in constructing the tabernacle. Why might such meticulous public record-keeping matter for sacred offerings?

The menorah is described as pure gold, hammered out of one piece (Exodus 37:17-24). What does the labor-intensive, singular construction of the lampstand suggest about the value placed on the light it represented in God's dwelling?

At the moment of Yeshua's death, the Temple veil - descendant of the very curtain crafted from these instructions - is torn from top to bottom (Mark 15:38). What does this act signify about access to God's presence, given the barriers built so carefully in Exodus?
The Israelites gave an account of every measure of precious metal used for the tabernacle. In what area of your life could you practice greater integrity about what you offer to God and others?

The centurion at the cross declares, 'truly this man was the Son of God' (Mark 15:39), spoken by an outsider to the covenant. What does it mean that recognition of Yeshua's identity comes from such an unexpected witness?

theme: Glory Fills the Tabernacle, the Tomb Is Found Empty

questions.text: Exodus 40:33-35 notes that Moses finished the work, and then the glory of Adonai filled the tabernacle so fully that even Moses could not enter. What does this sequence - human labor completed, then divine glory arriving - suggest about the relationship between our work and God's presence?

The Hebrew word 'kavod,' translated glory, carries a root sense of weightiness or substance. How does thinking of God's glory as something with weight change how you picture the cloud filling the Mishkan?

The glory that filled the tabernacle excluded even Moses from entering, yet the empty tomb in Mark 16 is a space the women enter freely, discovering Yeshua already risen. What does this contrast suggest about how the resurrection changes access to God's presence?
Moses had to finish ordinary, tangible work before glory arrived. What practical task is in front of you this week that prepares space for God's presence, even if it feels unglamorous?

Exodus closes with glory too overwhelming to enter, while Mark's earliest ending shows the women fleeing the empty tomb in fear and silence (Mark 16:8). How does holy fear eventually give way to bold proclamation across these two accounts?

theme: Offerings Wholly Given, a Throne Announced Forever

questions.text: The burnt offering (olah) required a male without blemish and was entirely consumed on the altar, unlike other offerings that were partly eaten (Leviticus 1:3,9). What does the totality of this offering communicate about the devotion it pictured?
The word 'olah' comes from a root meaning 'to go up' or ascend, describing how the offering rises as smoke to God. What does this image of ascending suggest about the purpose of worship?

Psalm 45:6-7 declares of the king, 'Your throne, O God, is forever,' language later applied to Messiah. How does Gabriel's announcement to Mary in Luke 1:32-33, that her son will reign over the house of Jacob forever, echo this royal, eternal throne imagery?
The burnt offering models total, not partial, devotion. Where in your life are you offering God only a portion when wholehearted surrender is called for?

Zechariah is struck mute for doubting Gabriel's message, while Mary questions yet responds, 'let it be to me according to your word' (Luke 1:38). What distinguishes their two responses, and what does that suggest about honest questioning versus unbelief?

theme: Sin and Peace Offerings Meet in Bethlehem

questions.text: Luke 2:8 notes the shepherds were in the fields near Bethlehem, keeping watch by night, a region traditionally associated with raising lambs for Temple offerings. What resonance might there be between shepherds tending sacrificial lambs and their being first to hear of the Messiah's birth?

The peace or fellowship offering is called 'shelamim,' from the same root as 'shalom.' What does naming an offering after wholeness suggest about its purpose, distinct from the sin offering (chatat) covered in the same chapters?

Simeon declares over the infant Yeshua that he is God's salvation, 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles and glory to Your people Israel' (Luke 2:32). How does this dual purpose echo the two purposes served by Leviticus 3-4's offerings - restoring peace and atoning for sin?

Leviticus 4 distinguishes offering requirements depending on whether a priest, the congregation, a leader, or an individual sinned. Where do you carry responsibility this week that others might overlook, precisely because of the position you hold?

Simeon and Anna had waited, by tradition, for decades to see God's promised redemption. What sustains hope through such long waiting, and where might you need that kind of patient expectancy right now?

theme: Guilt, Restitution, and a Genealogy Back to Adam

questions.text: Leviticus 6:12-13 commands that the fire on the altar must never go out, requiring the priests' continual attention. What does this ongoing responsibility suggest about sustaining, rather than treating as a one-time event, a relationship with God?

The guilt offering, 'asham,' required restitution plus an additional twenty percent, distinguishing it from the sin offering (chatat) earlier in these chapters. Why might restitution, not sacrifice alone, be necessary to fully resolve guilt?

Luke traces Yeshua's genealogy all the way back to Adam, not merely to Abraham (Luke 3:23-38), presenting him as hope for all humanity. How does this connect to the guilt offering's principle of restitution - Messiah restoring what was broken since Adam's fall?
The guilt offering required tangible restitution, not just verbal apology. Is there a relationship where you owe more than words - where actual repair is needed?

John the Baptist gives crowds, tax collectors, and soldiers each specific, practical instructions for repentance (Luke 3:10-14). What does his concrete ethical counsel suggest about what genuine repentance looks like beyond religious sentiment?

theme: Ordained for Service, Tested in the Wilderness

questions.text: During Aaron and his sons' seven-day ordination, blood was applied to their right ear, thumb, and big toe (Leviticus 8:23-24). What might this placement - ear, hand, foot - communicate about the totality of consecration for service?
The Hebrew for ordination, 'milu'im,' literally means 'filling,' as in filling the hand for service. What does this image of a filled hand suggest about being equipped for ministry?

In the Nazareth synagogue, Yeshua reads from Isaiah and declares, 'today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing' (Luke 4:21). How does this public claiming of anointing parallel Aaron's ordination in Leviticus 8, where consecration was followed by public ministry?
Aaron's consecration touched his ear, hand, and foot - hearing, doing, and walking. Which of these three areas most needs realignment with God's purposes in your life this week?

The Nazareth crowd turns to rage and tries to throw Yeshua off a cliff after he cites God's grace toward the widow of Zarephath and Naaman the Syrian, both outsiders (Luke 4:25-29). What does this reaction reveal about resistance to God's mercy extending beyond expected boundaries?

theme: Strange Fire and Unexpected Grace

questions.text: Nadab and Abihu offer 'strange fire' that Adonai had not commanded, and are instantly consumed (Leviticus 10:1-2). What does the suddenness of this judgment suggest about approaching worship on God's terms rather than one's own initiative?
The phrase describing Nadab and Abihu's offense is 'esh zarah' - strange or unauthorized fire. Why might the text emphasize the fire's foreignness rather than any other detail of their error?

Psalm 49:7-9 declares that no one can ransom another's soul - 'the redemption of their life is costly.' How does this connect to Yeshua's authority to forgive the paralytic's sins before healing him in Luke 5:20-24, an authority the scribes recognized as belonging to God alone?
Aaron responds to his sons' deaths with silence (Leviticus 10:3). Where might restrained, reverent silence be more faithful than explaining away a hard providence you're facing?

Yeshua says new wine must go into new wineskins, not old ones that would burst (Luke 5:37-39). What areas of faith or community life today might need genuinely new 'wineskins' rather than simply patching old structures?

theme: Clean and Unclean, Blessings That Overturn Expectation

questions.text: Leviticus 11:3 gives two signs for permissible land animals: chewing the cud and having a split hoof. Why might God give such concrete, everyday markers rather than a general principle to remember?
The Hebrew terms 'tahor' and 'tamei' - clean and unclean - describe ritual status, not moral failure. How does keeping this distinction clear change how you read the purity laws in Leviticus 11-12?

Psalm 50:14 calls for offering God 'a sacrifice of thanksgiving' rather than mere ritual performance. How does this anticipate Yeshua's Sermon on the Plain, where he blesses the poor, hungry, and weeping (Luke 6:20-23) - inverting expected categories much as clean and unclean laws overturn natural assumptions?
Yeshua commands loving one's enemies and doing good to those who hate us (Luke 6:27-28). Name one concrete, uncomfortable way you can practice this specific command this week.

What purpose might the kashrut laws of Leviticus 11 have served in shaping Israel's distinct identity among the nations, beyond questions of hygiene? How should that purpose inform how Messianic believers relate to these commands today?

theme: Cleansing the Metzora, a Clean Heart Created

questions.text: The ritual for cleansing a healed metzora involves two birds - one killed over fresh water, the other dipped in its blood and released alive into the open field (Leviticus 14:4-7). What might this dual-bird ritual be communicating about death and restored life?

The Hebrew word for the afflicted person, 'metzora,' is linked by later rabbinic wordplay to 'motzi shem ra' - one who spreads slander. What does this connection suggest about how the deeper cause behind this affliction was traditionally understood?

David's cry, 'create in me a clean heart, O God' (Psalm 51:10), finds an echo in Yeshua's words to the sinful woman who anoints his feet: 'your sins are forgiven... your faith has saved you' (Luke 7:48-50). How do both texts point toward a cleansing deeper than ritual alone can provide?
Psalm 51 models honest, specific confession rather than vague acknowledgment of wrongdoing. Is there a sin you've been naming only in general terms that needs the same directness David brings?

In Luke 7:36-50, the sinful woman's lavish devotion contrasts sharply with Simon the Pharisee's calculated hospitality. What does this reveal about the relationship between how forgiven a person feels and how extravagantly they respond in love?

theme: Yom Kippur's Two Goats, a Woman's Reaching Faith

questions.text: On Yom Kippur, one goat is sacrificed as a sin offering while the other, the scapegoat, is sent alive into the wilderness bearing the people's iniquities (Leviticus 16:8-10, 20-22). Why might atonement here require two goats rather than one, each accomplishing a different part of the picture?

The scapegoat is sent 'la-Azazel,' a term whose exact meaning is debated by scholars. Regardless of its precise reference, what does the image of sins being carried away and released communicate about atonement?

A woman who had suffered a flow of blood for twelve years - rendering her ritually unclean under Leviticus 15 - reaches out to touch Yeshua's tzitzit and is instantly healed (Luke 8:43-48). How does this reversal, where his holiness overcomes her impurity, reshape the usual direction of contagion assumed in Leviticus?
The bleeding woman approached Yeshua boldly despite social and ritual barriers. What barrier - shame, exclusion, fear - is keeping you from approaching God boldly this week?

Luke pairs Jairus, a synagogue leader with status, and the unnamed bleeding woman, both seeking Yeshua's help in the same episode (Luke 8:40-56). What does it suggest that Yeshua meets both with equal attention, regardless of social standing?

theme: Set Apart from the Nations, Counting the Cost

questions.text: Leviticus 18:24-28 warns that the land itself 'vomited out' the nations before Israel because of their practices, and warns Israel against the same. What does personifying the land this way suggest about the seriousness of covenant faithfulness tied to place?

Leviticus 17:11 states that 'the life of the flesh is in the blood,' grounding the prohibition against consuming blood in its atoning power. How does this principle deepen your understanding of sacrifice throughout Leviticus?

At the transfiguration, Moses and Elijah speak with Yeshua about his coming 'departure,' literally exodus, which he would accomplish in Jerusalem (Luke 9:31). How does the blood-atonement principle of Leviticus 17:11 illuminate what that departure - his death - would accomplish?

Yeshua tells a would-be follower that no one who puts a hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the Kingdom (Luke 9:62). What is one half-hearted commitment this command is calling you to fully resolve?

James and John ask Yeshua's permission to call down fire on a Samaritan village that rejected him, and are rebuked (Luke 9:54-56). Where do you see the temptation toward zealous retaliation instead of mercy in religious communities today?

theme: Love Your Neighbor, and the Samaritan Who Stopped

questions.text: Leviticus 19:9-10 commands landowners to leave the corners of their fields and the gleanings for the poor and the sojourner. What does this built-in provision reveal about how Torah structures generosity into ordinary economic life rather than leaving it optional?

Leviticus 19:18 commands, 'love your neighbor,' using the word 're'a.' In Luke 10:29, an expert in the law asks Yeshua, 'who is my re'a?' How does the parable of the Good Samaritan expand the expected boundary of that word?

Yeshua directly quotes Leviticus 19:18 as the second great commandment, then illustrates it through the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37), where a Samaritan, not a priest or Levite, shows true neighbor-love. What does this pairing teach about how the commandment was meant to be lived out?
Identify one person this week who is 'inconvenient' to help, as the wounded man was to the priest and Levite, and take a concrete step of care toward them.

Why is a Samaritan, given the deep historical animosity between Jews and Samaritans, cast as the hero of Yeshua's parable rather than a fellow Israelite? What does this choice suggest about who qualifies as 'neighbor'?

theme: Unblemished Offerings, Woes on Hollow Religion

questions.text: Leviticus 21:1-8 restricts priests from certain mourning practices, and Leviticus 22:20-25 requires offerings to be entirely without defect. Why might such a high standard be set specifically for those who represent the people before God?

The Hebrew word 'mum' - blemish or defect - must be absent from any acceptable offering (Leviticus 22:20). How does this standard of physical wholeness function as a picture of the deeper wholehearted devotion God desires?

Yeshua pronounces woes on Pharisees and lawyers for tithing herbs meticulously while neglecting justice and love of God (Luke 11:42-46). How does this critique echo Leviticus's concern that outward correctness must be matched by inward integrity, not hollow performance?
Consider one area of your spiritual life that may be technically correct but not wholehearted. What would genuine devotion in that area look like this week?

Psalm 55:12-14 laments betrayal by a close companion, not an enemy: 'it is you, a man like myself, my familiar friend.' How do you process betrayal that comes from within your own community rather than from outside it?

theme: The Appointed Feasts and the Fool's Bigger Barns

questions.text: Leviticus 23 lists Israel's appointed times in sequence across the year - Pesach, Firstfruits, Shavuot, Yom Teruah, Yom Kippur, and Sukkot. What does organizing the calendar around these recurring, God-ordained rhythms suggest about how Israel's relationship to time was meant to be shaped?
The Hebrew word 'moed,' translated appointed time, shares its root with 'Ohel Moed' - the Tent of Meeting. What connection does this shared root draw between sacred time and sacred space?

The Feast of Firstfruits within Leviticus 23's cycle anticipates resurrection life, and Sukkot points toward future ingathering. How does the rich fool's plan to build bigger barns and take his ease (Luke 12:16-21) reveal a different orientation toward time and provision than the feast cycle teaches?
Rather than hoarding like the rich fool, what is one way you can practice generosity or trust in God's provision with your resources this week?

Leviticus 24:10-16 recounts a man stoned for blaspheming God's Name. What does the weight given here to reverence for God's Name suggest, and how do we hold that seriousness together with the grace extended elsewhere in Scripture?

theme: Jubilee's Release and the Call to Repent in Time

questions.text: Leviticus 25:10 commands that in the fiftieth year, a trumpet should proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants, with land and people released back to their families. Why might God build in a periodic, guaranteed reset rather than leaving justice purely to human generosity?
The Hebrew word for this liberty, 'd'ror,' later inspired the inscription on the Liberty Bell. What does the choice of this word suggest about the kind of freedom the Jubilee was meant to embody?

Yeshua tells a parable of a barren fig tree given one more year before being cut down (Luke 13:6-9). How does this grace period echo the patient, restorative logic built into the Jubilee cycle of Leviticus 25?
Is there a debt, grudge, or possession you are holding onto that a jubilee spirit of release would call you to let go of this week?

Leviticus 26 pairs severe curses for covenant unfaithfulness with God's promise to still remember His covenant even after judgment (26:44-45). How do judgment and enduring covenant faithfulness coexist in this chapter?

theme: Vows and Census, the Banquet Thrown Open

questions.text: Numbers 1 records a detailed, tribe-by-tribe census of Israel's fighting men, but the Levites are deliberately excluded and given a different role (Numbers 1:47-49). What does this distinct treatment suggest about how the community was structured around more than military capacity alone?

The verb behind the census, 'paqad,' means not just 'to count' but also 'to visit' or 'attend to' elsewhere in Scripture. How might this double meaning shape how we understand God's numbering of His people?

In the parable of the great banquet, those originally invited make excuses, so the host sends servants to bring in the poor and those from the highways and hedges (Luke 14:15-24). How does this open invitation contrast with, yet not erase, the specific and ordered calling of Israel's tribes counted in Numbers 1?
Yeshua urges counting the cost of discipleship honestly, like a builder estimating before construction (Luke 14:28-33). What cost have you been avoiding counting in your own commitment to follow him?

The Levites were exempted from Israel's fighting census yet given the weighty, distinct responsibility of caring for the tabernacle. What does this pattern suggest about how a community can hold together different callings without ranking them by status?

theme: Camped Around the Presence, a Father Who Seeks

questions.text: Numbers 2 arranges all twelve tribes in a precise formation on the four sides of the Tabernacle, with the Levites camped innermost around the Tent itself. What does this concentric arrangement, with God's presence at the literal center, suggest about how Israel's identity was structured?
Each tribe camped and marched under its own 'degel' - banner or standard. What might rallying under a visible banner have meant for tribal identity and unity within the larger camp?

In Luke 15, the shepherd searches for one lost sheep, the woman searches for one lost coin, and the father runs to meet his returning son. How does this active seeking mirror the way Israel's entire camp in Numbers 2-3 was structured around drawing near to, not drifting from, God's dwelling place?

Consider whether your current habits and priorities orient you toward God's presence at the center, or toward the margins, like the prodigal son. What is one adjustment that would draw you nearer this week?

Numbers 3:12-13, 40-51 describes the Levites being substituted for Israel's firstborn sons, with redemption money paid for those in excess. How does this substitutionary principle compare with the elder brother's resentment in the prodigal son parable, who seems to miss the logic of grace?

theme: Carrying the Holy Things, Serving One Master

questions.text: The Kohathites were assigned to carry the ark, table, and menorah, but were forbidden to touch these objects directly - the priests had to cover them first (Numbers 4:15,20). What does this careful protocol suggest about the difference between handling something sacred and handling it presumptuously?

The 'sotah' ritual in Numbers 5:11-31 is literally called the law of jealousy ('qin'ah'), addressing a husband's suspicion of his wife's unfaithfulness. Considering its ancient Near Eastern context, how might this ritual have functioned as a protective, God-mediated alternative to unchecked accusation?

Yeshua declares that no one can serve two masters, God and money (Luke 16:13), and tells of the rich man and Lazarus, whose fortunes are reversed in the age to come (Luke 16:19-31). How does the reverence required for handling the Tabernacle's holy furnishings in Numbers 4 parallel the seriousness with which Yeshua says we must handle what is entrusted to us?
What resource, relationship, or responsibility has God entrusted to you that you may be handling too casually, in light of the reverence Numbers 4 calls for?

In the parable of the rich man and Lazarus, Abraham tells the rich man that if his brothers won't listen to Moses and the Prophets, they won't be persuaded even by someone risen from the dead (Luke 16:31). What does this claim suggest about the sufficiency of Scripture already given, versus the desire for dramatic new signs?

theme: Priestly Blessing and a Grateful Leper

questions.text: Numbers 6 lists three marks of the Nazirite vow - abstaining from the fruit of the vine, letting the hair grow uncut, and avoiding all contact with the dead. What do these three practices, taken together, seem to be teaching about consecration to Adonai?

The priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26 asks that Adonai 'make His face shine' on Israel, from the Hebrew verb ya'er. What does it mean for the light of God's presence, not just His words, to rest on a person?

Of the ten men with tzara'at whom Yeshua heals in Luke 17, only the Samaritan comes back shouting praise and falls at His feet in thanks. How does this one man's todah (thanksgiving) put flesh on the blessing Aaron's sons pronounced over Israel generations earlier?
Where has God clearly been gracious to you recently, and have you paused - like the Samaritan leper rather than the other nine - to actually turn back and say so this week?

Nine healed men went on with their lives while one turned back. Discuss what made the Samaritan different, and whether gratitude comes naturally to faith or has to be practiced against the pull of relief and distraction.

theme: A Second Passover and a Persistent Widow

questions.text: Numbers 9 provides a 'second Passover' one month later for those defiled by a corpse or away on a journey. Notice how the law makes room for exception rather than simply excluding those who missed the appointed day.
Two silver trumpets (chatsotzrah) in Numbers 10 signal assembly, war, and festivals alike. What does it mean that the very same instrument sounds both the call to battle and the call to worship?

In Luke 18 the tax collector prays only 'God, be merciful to me, a sinner,' and Yeshua says he went home justified. How does that mercy echo Numbers 9's gracious provision for those who could not come at the appointed time?
Read the persistent widow of Luke 18 alongside Psalm 62's 'my soul waits in silence for God alone.' How might you hold together bold persistence and quiet trust in your own prayer life this week?
