Proverbs 31 closes the book with the eshet chayil, a woman praised for strength, wisdom, and fear of Adonai. Discuss what this portrait affirms about work, dignity, and character, and whether it is more than a domestic checklist.

theme: Craving in the Wilderness, Thirsting for God

questions.text: In Numbers 11 the 'mixed multitude' craves meat and weeps for Egypt's food, while Moses himself despairs under the burden of leading the people. Notice how raw Moses' own complaint to Adonai becomes.

Miriam and Aaron speak against Moses in Numbers 12, yet Moses is called anav - humble - more than anyone on earth. How does anavah contrast with the grumbling (talonim) that fills the surrounding chapters?

David cries in Psalm 63, 'my soul thirsts for You... in a dry and weary land.' How does that longing connect to Zacchaeus in Luke 19 climbing a tree because he was determined to see Yeshua despite the crowd?
Proverbs 1 warns against being enticed by sinners and calls fear of Adonai the beginning of knowledge. Where this week might you need to resist a crowd pulling you toward complaint rather than trust?

Seventy elders receive the Spirit and prophesy, and Eldad and Medad prophesy in the camp though absent from the Tent; Moses says, 'would that all Adonai's people were prophets.' What does this suggest about jealousy over gifting versus welcoming the Spirit's work in unexpected people?

theme: Ten Spies' Fear versus Two Voices of Faith

questions.text: Twelve spies see the same land, the same giants, and the same fruit, yet ten and two draw opposite conclusions. Notice exactly what Caleb says in Numbers 13:30 - 'we should go up and take possession, for we are well able.'
The name Yehoshua (Joshua) means 'Adonai saves.' Why might Moses give Hoshea this new name just before sending him to scout the very land he will one day lead Israel to conquer?

In Luke 20 the religious leaders reject Yeshua as the rejected cornerstone in His parable of the tenants. How does this rejection compare to Israel's rejection of the good land its own scouts confirmed was good?

Proverbs 2 promises wisdom will guard those who treasure Adonai's commands. What 'giants' in your circumstances tempt you toward the fear of the ten spies rather than the confidence of Caleb and Joshua?

An entire generation is barred from the land for unbelief while their children inherit the promise decades later. Discuss what this reveals about consequences that outlast repentance, and how grace and discipline hold together in Numbers 14.

theme: Tzitzit, Korah's Rebellion, and Enduring Faith

questions.text: Numbers 15 commands tzitzit with a blue cord on garment corners 'so you remember all the commandments of Adonai.' Notice how a physical object is meant to interrupt everyday forgetfulness.
Korah's rebellion in Numbers 16 argues 'kol ha-edah kedoshim' - all the congregation is holy. What does Korah get right about Israel's calling, and what does he distort about order within it?

Yeshua warns in Luke 21 that the Temple's stones will be thrown down, 'not one stone left upon another.' How does that warning echo the ground opening beneath Korah's rebellion - both scenes showing that no structure survives without submission to Adonai's actual instruction?
Proverbs 3 urges 'trust in Adonai with all your heart, lean not on your own understanding.' In what area are you tempted, like Korah, to trust your own sense of fairness over what God has appointed?

Aaron runs into the middle of a plague with incense to stop death mid-stride. Discuss what this says about intercession that acts quickly and physically, and how it might shape how believers respond to crises in their own community.

theme: A Budding Staff and the Cup of the New Covenant

questions.text: Among twelve staffs collected from each tribe's leader, Aaron's alone buds, blossoms, and produces ripe almonds overnight in Numbers 17. Notice that it is kept before the Testimony as an ongoing sign, not just a onetime proof.

The almond tree in Hebrew is shaked, from a root meaning 'to watch' or 'be alert,' since it blossoms earliest in spring. Why might this particular tree fit a sign meant to end Israel's complaints about who was truly chosen?

At the Passover table in Luke 22, Yeshua takes the cup and says, 'this cup is the new covenant in My blood.' How does this new covenant cup echo the priesthood confirmed by a budding staff, both signs given so people would rest in what God had already appointed rather than keep striving?

Proverbs 4 says, 'guard your heart, for from it flow the springs of life.' After reading Peter's confident boast and predicted denial in Luke 22, what guard does your own heart need this week against overconfidence?

Discuss why Adonai chose a sign as unusual as an overnight-blossoming staff rather than simply reaffirming Aaron's authority by decree. What does this suggest about God sometimes settling disputes with evidence rather than argument?

theme: The Red Heifer, Moses' Failure, and the Cross

questions.text: In Numbers 20 Moses is told to speak to the rock, but in his frustration he strikes it twice instead. Notice the small but decisive gap between the command given and the action he actually took.

The ashes of the parah adumah - red heifer - in Numbers 19 purify a person defiled by contact with death. Why might a ritual dealing with death require an animal without spot or blemish that had never been yoked to work?

Moses' generation loses entry to the land over a moment of striking rather than speaking, while in Luke 23 Yeshua is struck and pierced on the tree and cries, 'Father, forgive them.' Compare these two very different responses to being wronged.
Miriam and then Aaron both die in this section, and Moses must lead on without them. What loss or transition are you navigating right now, and what does obedience through grief look like for you?
Discuss why Adonai's judgment on Moses seems severe for what looks like a small act of frustration. What does this suggest about the weight of a leader's example before the people he represents?

theme: The Bronze Serpent, Balaam's Donkey, and the Empty Tomb

questions.text: When Israel complains again in Numbers 21, Adonai sends venomous snakes, yet the remedy He commands is to look at a bronze image of the very thing killing them. Notice the strangeness of this instruction.
Balaam's donkey 'sees' the angel of Adonai three times before Balaam does. What is the irony that a hired prophet, sent to see and pronounce, is spiritually blinder than his own animal?

On the road to Emmaus in Luke 24, Yeshua opens the Scriptures 'beginning with Moses and all the Prophets' to explain Himself. The bronze serpent lifted up in Numbers 21 is exactly this kind of foreshadowing, later cited by Yeshua Himself as a picture of His being lifted up.

Proverbs 6 lists things Adonai hates, including 'a heart that devises wicked plans.' Balaam's mouth eventually blessed Israel while his heart still schemed against them; where might your outward words and inward intentions be misaligned?

Discuss why Adonai lets Balaam go with Balak's officials in Numbers 22 even while being angry that he went. What does this tension reveal about the difference between what God permits and what God desires?

theme: A Star from Jacob and the Word Made Flesh

questions.text: Balaam repeatedly opens his mouth to curse Israel and blessing comes out instead. Notice his own protest in Numbers 23:8 - 'how can I curse whom God has not cursed?'
Numbers 24:17 speaks of a kokhav - star - that will come forth from Jacob and a scepter that will rise from Israel. This became one of the most quoted messianic texts in Jewish history.

John 1 opens, 'in the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' How does this eternal Word taking on flesh connect to Balaam's distant-future star, both pointing forward to a ruler not yet visible in Israel's own day?

Psalm 69's suffering psalmist cries, 'zeal for Your house has consumed me,' a line John later applies to Yeshua clearing the Temple. What would zeal for God's house look like lived out in your own life this week?

Discuss why Balaam, a pagan diviner hired specifically to curse, becomes the mouthpiece for some of Torah's clearest long-range prophecy about Israel's future king. What does this suggest about Adonai's sovereignty over even reluctant or compromised messengers?

theme: Zeal at Baal Peor, Zeal at the Temple

questions.text: Pinchas acts alone and decisively to stop the plague at Baal Peor in Numbers 25, and Adonai grants him a 'covenant of peace' and an eternal priesthood as a result. Notice that his zeal is rewarded, not merely tolerated.

The second census in Numbers 26 counts a new generation, almost none of whom were counted in Numbers 1. What does the Hebrew concept of paqad - to number, to visit, to attend to - suggest about God's care in counting His people by name?

At the wedding in Cana, John 2 records Yeshua's first sign, turning water to wine, followed by His cleansing the Temple with the words 'zeal for Your house has consumed me.' How does His zeal echo the zeal of Pinchas, both acting to protect worship due to Adonai alone?
Proverbs 8 pictures Wisdom present with God before the world began, rejoicing in His creation. How might inviting Wisdom into an ordinary decision this week change how you approach it?

Discuss the moral weight of Pinchas' action - some later Jewish tradition celebrates his zeal while others caution against vigilante violence. What boundaries should govern righteous anger in a community today?

theme: Daughters of Zelophehad and Being Born from Above

questions.text: The five daughters of Zelophehad approach Moses directly to request an inheritance despite having no brother, and Adonai tells Moses, 'the daughters of Zelophehad speak rightly.' Notice that Torah itself is amended in response to their petition.

Moses commissions Joshua by laying his hands on him and giving him some of his hod - majesty or authority - in Numbers 27. What does this transfer signal about succession being God's initiative rather than merely Moses' personal choice?

Nicodemus comes to Yeshua by night in John 3 and is told he must be 'born again' or 'born from above' to see the Kingdom of God. How does this necessity of a fresh beginning connect to the new generation numbered in Numbers 26 and given fresh land rights in Numbers 27 - both showing that entering the promise requires more than birthright alone?
Proverbs 9 contrasts Wisdom's invitation to her table with Folly's. What specific 'table' are you being invited to sit at this week, and which voice is calling you there?

Discuss the daughters of Zelophehad's boldness in bringing a legal grievance before Moses, Eleazar, the leaders, and the whole congregation. What does their example suggest about advocating for justice within, rather than outside, established structures?

theme: Feast Offerings, Vows, and Living Water

questions.text: Numbers 29 details offerings for Yom Teruah, Yom Kippur, and Sukkot. Notice how the number of bulls offered during Sukkot decreases day by day, a detail later tradition connects to the seventy nations.

Numbers 30 addresses a neder - vow - especially a woman's vow that a father or husband may uphold or annul on the day he hears it. What weight does Torah place on spoken commitment through the phrase 'he shall not break his word'?

Yeshua tells the Samaritan woman at the well in John 4 that He offers 'living water' so she will never thirst again. How does this connect to the joyful water offerings later associated with Sukkot reflected in Numbers 29, a theme John 7 shows Yeshua directly addressing?

Psalm 72 prays for a king whose reign brings justice to the poor and blessing to all nations. What would it look like this week to pray specifically for just leadership in your own community or nation?

Discuss the treatment of women's vows under a father's or husband's authority in Numbers 30 alongside the directness with which Yeshua discusses theology with the Samaritan woman in John 4. What does the contrast suggest about how Yeshua related to women relative to the culture around Him?

theme: War with Midian and the Man at Bethesda

questions.text: In Numbers 31, Balaam is killed among the Midianites, revealed as the one who counseled the seduction at Baal Peor. Notice how this closes a loop opened back in Numbers 22-25 with a name that had seemed, for a time, to have blessed Israel.

Reuben and Gad ask to settle east of the Jordan in Numbers 32, and Moses rebukes them, asking 'will your brothers go to war while you sit here?' What is at stake in the Hebrew concept of nachalah - inheritance - as they weigh their own comfort against their brothers' portion?

Yeshua heals a man ill for thirty-eight years at the pool of Bethesda in John 5, on the Sabbath, saying 'My Father is working until now, and I am working.' How does this connect to Psalm 73's resolution that only entering God's presence, not circumstances, brings clarity about what truly matters?

Asaph in Psalm 73 confesses he nearly stumbled envying the wicked's prosperity until he 'went into the sanctuary of God.' What perspective do you need to regain this week by turning toward God's presence rather than comparing your situation to others'?

Discuss the compromise in Numbers 32 - Reuben and Gad may settle east of the Jordan if they first help conquer the land west of it. What does this negotiated solution suggest about balancing personal preference with communal obligation?

theme: The Wilderness Journey Recounted and the Bread of Life

questions.text: Numbers 33 lists forty-two stations of Israel's journey from Egypt to the plains of Moab. Notice how meticulously each stopping place is recorded even though most appear nowhere else in the Torah.

Adonai commands Israel in Numbers 33 to 'dispossess' - horish - the inhabitants of the land and destroy their pagan high places and images. What does horish imply about a complete, rather than partial, removal of competing worship?

Yeshua feeds five thousand and then teaches, 'I am the bread of life... your fathers ate manna in the wilderness and died,' in John 6. How does the literal wilderness journey of Numbers 33, sustained daily by manna, connect to Yeshua's claim to be the true and lasting bread that journey only foreshadowed?

Proverbs 12 says 'the righteous care for the needs of their animals' and praises truthful lips over lying ones. What small area of integrity or care for those under your responsibility needs attention this week?

Many disciples turn away from Yeshua in John 6, saying 'this is a hard teaching,' while the twelve stay, saying 'to whom shall we go.' Discuss what makes some of Yeshua's teachings hard to accept today, and what keeps a person from walking away when they are.

theme: Cities of Refuge and Living Water at Sukkot

questions.text: Numbers 35 establishes six cities of refuge for one who kills another accidentally, restraining the 'avenger of blood' from pursuing the manslayer once inside. Notice the careful legal distinction drawn between intent and accident.

The manslayer must remain in the city of refuge 'until the death of the high priest' (Numbers 35:25). What does this Hebrew provision suggest about one specific death releasing a person from ongoing consequence?

At the Feast of Sukkot in John 7, Yeshua cries out that anyone thirsty should come to Him and 'rivers of living water' will flow from within them. How does the refuge a fugitive finds by fleeing to a city of asylum connect to the refuge Yeshua offers anyone who comes to Him amid the crowds debating who He is?

The daughters of Zelophehad marry within their own tribe in Numbers 36 to preserve the land inheritance boundaries. What boundary or commitment in your own life protects something valuable that a careless choice could otherwise lose?

Discuss why Torah ties the manslayer's release specifically to the death of the high priest rather than to a fixed number of years. What might this teach about how one person's death or service can mark a turning point for someone else's captivity?

theme: Remembering Kadesh Barnea and the Light of the World

questions.text: Moses opens Deuteronomy by retelling the spies' story and the refusal to enter the land at Kadesh Barnea. Notice that he now adds his own exclusion from the land 'because of you,' a detail phrased differently than in Numbers.

Moses recalls Adonai saying, 'do not fear or be in dread of them' (Deuteronomy 1:29), right before listing all He had already done. Explore the Hebrew word yare - fear or dread - and how remembering past faithfulness is presented as its antidote.

In John 8 Yeshua declares 'I am the light of the world' and later 'before Abraham was, I am,' directly invoking the divine Name. How does this bold self-disclosure connect to Moses' retelling in Deuteronomy 1-2 of a God who goes ahead of Israel 'in the pillar of fire by night to show you the way'?

Proverbs 14 says 'there is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death.' Where might you need Moses' approach this week - deliberately reviewing where God has already proven faithful before deciding how to respond to a current fear?

The Pharisees bring a woman caught in adultery to Yeshua in John 8, and He says, 'let him who is without sin cast the first stone.' Discuss how this scene handles both real guilt and the demand for mercy, and where that tension sits uneasily today.

theme: Denied Entry, Seeing and Being Seen

questions.text: Moses recounts pleading to cross into the land, only to be told 'enough, speak no more to Me of this,' yet shown the land from Pisgah's summit. Notice the finality of the refusal alongside the grace of being allowed to see it.

Deuteronomy 4 repeatedly uses shamar - keep, guard, observe - regarding the statutes and warns against making a pesel, a carved image. How is 'keeping' the commandments framed here as protecting Israel's unique encounter with Adonai at Horeb?

In John 9 Yeshua heals a man born blind, whose growing spiritual sight contrasts with the Pharisees' willful blindness. How does this connect to Moses' warning in Deuteronomy 4:9 - 'watch yourselves closely... lest you forget the things your eyes have seen'?

Psalm 77 moves from sleepless anguish - 'will the Lord reject forever?' - to deliberate remembrance of God's past wonders. What past evidence of God's faithfulness could you deliberately recall this week when current circumstances feel discouraging?

The healed man in John 9 is cast out of the synagogue for testifying about Yeshua while his own parents distance themselves out of fear. Discuss the cost of honest testimony in the text, and what parallels exist for believers facing social cost today.

theme: The Shema and the Good Shepherd

questions.text: Deuteronomy 5 repeats the Ten Words with small differences from Exodus 20, including the reason given for Shabbat. Notice that here it is tied to remembering slavery in Egypt rather than to creation.

The Shema in Deuteronomy 6:4 declares, 'Sh'ma Yisrael, Adonai Eloheinu, Adonai echad.' Explore the range of meaning in echad - one, united - and why this confession anchors Jewish identity and prayer to this day.

Yeshua says in John 10, 'I and the Father are one,' provoking the crowd to charge Him with blasphemy. How does this claim connect directly to the Shema's declaration of Adonai's oneness that every faithful Israelite recites?

The V'ahavta in Deuteronomy 6 commands teaching Torah diligently to children 'when you sit... walk... lie down... rise up.' What small, repeated moment in your daily rhythm could become an intentional place to pass on faith to someone younger this week?

Psalm 78 recounts generations of Israel forgetting God's works despite seeing them firsthand, ending with the choice of David and Zion. Discuss why the psalm frames national memory, or forgetting, as central to faithfulness, and what institutions might serve that role today.

theme: Chosen Not for Greatness, Life from Death

questions.text: Deuteronomy 7 explicitly denies Israel was chosen for its size or strength, 'for you were the fewest of all peoples,' attributing the choice entirely to Adonai's love and His oath to the patriarchs. Notice how this undercuts any basis for national pride.

Deuteronomy 8 says Adonai let Israel hunger then fed them manna 'that He might humble you and test you, to know what was in your heart.' Explore the Hebrew anah - to humble or afflict - paired with nasah - to test - showing hardship framed as instruction rather than mere punishment.

Yeshua raises Lazarus in John 11, declaring 'I am the resurrection and the life,' after deliberately delaying His arrival. How does this delay, which seems to contradict love but ultimately serves a greater purpose, connect to Deuteronomy 8's manna given only after hunger - both showing God's timing working through apparent absence?

Deuteronomy 8 warns against saying 'my power and the strength of my hand have gained me this wealth' once prosperity comes. What current success or comfort in your life needs a deliberate reminder this week of where it actually came from?

Yeshua weeps at Lazarus' tomb even knowing He is about to raise him. Discuss what this reveals about grief coexisting with faith and foreknowledge, and whether sorrow in the moment is at odds with trusting God's larger plan.

theme: The Golden Calf Remembered, a Grain of Wheat

questions.text: Moses reminds Israel in Deuteronomy 9 that it was not their righteousness but the wickedness of the nations and the oath to the patriarchs that secured the land, recalling how he threw down the tablets himself in anger at the golden calf. Notice how bluntly he calls Israel 'stiff-necked.'
Deuteronomy 10 commands, 'circumcise the foreskin of your heart.' Explore how this Hebrew image applies the physical covenant sign of brit milah to inward devotion.

Yeshua says in John 12, 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit,' just before the plot to kill Him intensifies. How does this necessary death-that-brings-life connect to Moses' costly intercession in Deuteronomy 9, where he fasted forty days pleading that Israel not be destroyed?

Deuteronomy 10 says Adonai 'loves the ger - the stranger - giving him food and clothing,' commanding Israel to do likewise since they were strangers in Egypt. Who is a stranger in your own community this week that this command calls you to notice and care for?

Psalm 80 repeats the refrain 'restore us, O God, let Your face shine, that we may be saved,' describing Israel as a vine brought from Egypt and now broken down. Discuss what corporate repentance and restoration might look like for a community today, beyond merely individual repentance.

theme: Blessing on Gerizim, a Basin and a Towel

questions.text: Deuteronomy 11 promises blessing on Mount Gerizim and curse on Mount Ebal, and Deuteronomy 12 commands worship only 'at the place Adonai your God will choose.' Notice the deliberate limits placed on where and how worship happens.
Deuteronomy 12 repeats 'you shall not do so to Adonai your God' regarding pagan worship practices - lo ta'aseh khen. Why might the text be as concerned with how Israel worships as with whom?

At the Last Supper in John 13, Yeshua takes a basin and towel and washes His disciples' feet, saying, 'I have given you an example.' How does this deliberate redefinition of devotion connect to Deuteronomy 12's insistence that worship must follow God's pattern, not human custom or comfort?
Psalm 81 pictures Adonai saying, 'open your mouth wide and I will fill it,' lamenting that Israel would not listen. What has God offered that you have been slow to receive or ask for this week?

Yeshua washes even Judas' feet, knowing he will betray Him within hours. Discuss what this act suggests about serving people who will disappoint or wound you, and whether such service requires or transcends reconciliation.

theme: False Prophets, Release Years, and the Way to the Father

questions.text: Deuteronomy 13 warns that even a prophet who performs a genuine sign but leads Israel toward other gods must not be followed. Notice that the test given is the message's faithfulness to Adonai already revealed, not the power of the sign.

Deuteronomy 15 institutes the shmita, the sabbatical release year, canceling debts and freeing Hebrew slaves every seventh year. What does shmita reveal about built-in limits on accumulating power or wealth over others?

Yeshua tells His troubled disciples in John 14, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through Me,' promising the Ruach ha-Kodesh as Helper. How does this singular way connect to Deuteronomy 13's insistence that truth is defined by faithfulness to the God already known, not by impressive signs alone?

Deuteronomy 15 commands generosity toward the poor 'without a grudging heart, because for this Adonai your God will bless you.' Is there a specific person or situation this week where you're tempted toward reluctant rather than open-handed generosity?

Psalm 82 pictures God rebuking corrupt judges for failing to defend the weak and fatherless, a verse Yeshua later cites in John 10. Discuss what standard this psalm sets for those who hold any form of authority, and how it applies to leadership today.

theme: Pilgrim Festivals, a King's Limits, the True Vine

questions.text: Deuteronomy 16 commands three pilgrimage festivals - Pesach, Shavuot, and Sukkot - each requiring appearance before Adonai, and Deuteronomy 17 restricts a future king from multiplying horses, wives, or silver. Notice how both chapters guard against concentrating power or forgetting dependence on Adonai.
Deuteronomy 16:20 commands 'tzedek, tzedek tirdof' - justice, justice you shall pursue. Why might the word for justice be doubled, and what does that repetition emphasize about the pursuit itself?

Yeshua tells His disciples in John 15, 'I am the true vine... abide in Me, and I in you,' using imagery of Israel long pictured as a vine. How does His call to abide connect to the festival calendar's rhythm of returning again and again to Adonai's chosen place?

Deuteronomy 17 requires a king to write his own copy of the Torah and read it 'all the days of his life' so his heart is not lifted above his fellow Israelites. What regular practice keeps your own heart in check against pride this week?

Psalm 83 names a specific coalition of hostile nations conspiring against Israel. Discuss how this plea for God to act against real, named enemies sits alongside Yeshua's command in John 15 to expect the world's hatred, and what posture believers should take toward opposition.

theme: A Prophet Like Moses, the Coming Helper

questions.text: Deuteronomy 18 promises 'Adonai your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers,' immediately after forbidding mediums, sorcerers, and diviners. Notice how the true source of guidance is set against the false ones just condemned.
Deuteronomy 19 requires 'two or three witnesses' to establish any matter. Why might single-witness testimony have been considered insufficient even when likely true?

Yeshua tells His disciples in John 16 that it is to their advantage that He go away so the Ruach ha-Kodesh, the Helper, can come. How does this promised presence connect to Deuteronomy 18's promise of a coming prophet like Moses, both pointing Israel toward one still to come?

Psalm 84 says, 'a day in Your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere,' picturing even a sparrow finding a home near the altar. What would it look like this week to treat time in God's presence as more valuable than a thousand hours spent elsewhere?

Torah takes great care to prevent false accusation in Deuteronomy 19, while Yeshua warns in John 16 that persecutors will believe they are serving God by opposing His followers. Discuss what this suggests about the danger of sincere but wrongly directed zeal.

theme: Rules of War, a Curse on the Tree, Yeshua's Prayer

questions.text: Deuteronomy 20 exempts a newly married man, a new homeowner, or a new vineyard planter from going to war, and Deuteronomy 21 requires a specific ritual even for an unsolved murder. Notice how deeply Torah cares about both individual life stages and corporate responsibility for bloodshed.

Deuteronomy 21:23 states that a body hung on a tree is under God's curse and must not remain overnight. This verse is later cited in the B'rit Chadashah regarding Yeshua's crucifixion - explore the weight of that phrase in its original setting.

Yeshua prays His high priestly prayer in John 17 hours before He Himself will be hung on a tree. How does Deuteronomy 21:23's curse language connect to what Sha'ul later says about Messiah becoming 'a curse for us,' bearing what the law described rather than being exempt from it?

Proverbs 23 warns against befriending those given to excess and urges, 'give me your heart, my son, and let your eyes delight in my ways.' What specific desire or indulgence needs realigning with that appeal this week?

For an unsolved murder in Deuteronomy 21, the elders of the nearest town wash their hands over a heifer and declare their innocence. Discuss what this ceremony suggests about communal responsibility for justice, even for crimes committed by unknown hands.

theme: Mixed Kinds, Camp Holiness, and Betrayal in the Garden

questions.text: Deuteronomy 22 forbids mixing certain kinds and requires a parapet on a new roof to prevent bloodguilt from a fall. Notice how these seemingly unrelated rules share a concern for maintaining order and protecting the vulnerable.

Deuteronomy 23 commands the camp remain holy, 'for Adonai your God walks in the midst of your camp.' What does this image of God walking among the people imply about why practical cleanliness mattered?

Yeshua is betrayed with a kiss and arrested in a garden in John 18, then denied three times by Peter around a charcoal fire. How does Peter's failure, despite his earlier boldness, connect to Deuteronomy 23's concern for keeping the camp where Adonai dwells free of what would drive Him away?
Psalm 86 prays, 'teach me Your way, Adonai... unite my heart to fear Your name.' What would it mean this week to ask specifically for an undivided heart rather than simply more willpower?

Peter denies Yeshua three times just as Yeshua predicted, yet is later restored. Discuss what this suggests about the difference between failure and final rejection, and how communities should treat those who fail publicly under pressure.

theme: Gleaning for the Poor, It Is Finished

questions.text: Deuteronomy 24 commands leaving gleanings in the field, olive tree, and vineyard for the poor, the stranger, the orphan, and the widow, 'remembering that you were a slave in Egypt.' Notice how personal memory of hardship is repeatedly cited as the reason for the command.

Deuteronomy 25 commands levirate marriage, where a brother raises up a shem - name - for his deceased brother by marrying his widow. Why might preserving a family's name matter enough to require this obligation?

Yeshua declares, 'it is finished,' from the cross in John 19, and Scripture is fulfilled that not one of His bones would be broken. How does this precise fulfillment connect to Deuteronomy 25's concern for justice down to exact details, like accurate weights and measures?

Deuteronomy 24 forbids taking a widow's garment in pledge and requires paying a hired worker's wages before sundown. What specific practice of fairness toward someone economically vulnerable could you put into practice this week?

Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus bury Yeshua's body with dignity and costly spices in John 19, taking a public risk to do so. Discuss what motivates people to act with sudden courage at moments others shrink back, and whether such late courage still counts for something.

theme: Firstfruits Confession and the Empty Tomb

questions.text: Deuteronomy 26 gives a liturgy for offering firstfruits, beginning 'a wandering Aramean was my father' and recounting the descent to Egypt, slavery, and deliverance. Notice how the worshiper is required to speak this history aloud personally, not simply present a basket.

Deuteronomy 27 commands the Torah be written on large stones plastered and set up at Mount Ebal, along with curses recited aloud. Why might the text have needed to be publicly displayed and read rather than kept only in scrolls or memory?

Mary Magdalene encounters the risen Yeshua at the empty tomb in John 20 and calls Him 'Rabboni,' while Thomas later confesses, 'My Lord and my God.' How do these deeply personal spoken confessions connect to the personal, spoken testimony required in Deuteronomy 26's firstfruits liturgy?
Psalm 88 is unusual among the psalms for ending without resolution or praise, only darkness. Where might you need permission to pray honestly through pain without a tidy resolution this week?

Discuss why Deuteronomy 26 requires the worshiper to personally recite the nation's history rather than simply hearing a priest recount it on their behalf. What might be lost when personal, spoken testimony is replaced by passive listening?

theme: Blessings, Curses, and Feed My Sheep

questions.text: Deuteronomy 28 lists blessings for obedience followed by a far longer, more detailed list of curses for disobedience. Notice the sheer proportional weight given to the curses, and consider why the warnings receive so much more space.

Deuteronomy 29 speaks of 'the secret things belong to Adonai our God, but the things revealed belong to us and our children forever.' What does this distinction suggest about the proper limits of human curiosity and confidence?

Yeshua asks Peter three times, 'do you love Me,' beside the Sea of Galilee in John 21, restoring him after his three denials, then commissions him to 'feed My sheep.' How does this restoration connect to Psalm 89's lament over the apparent failure of God's covenant promises to David, both showing human failure does not have the final word over God's covenant faithfulness?
Proverbs 27 says, 'iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another.' Who in your life is meant to sharpen you this week, and are you making yourself available to that kind of honest friendship?

Psalm 89 moves from celebrating God's covenant with David to bewildered lament, asking, 'Lord, where is Your steadfast love of old which You swore to David?' Discuss how a person or community should hold together confident memory of God's promises with honest confusion when circumstances seem to contradict them.

theme: Choose Life, Wait for the Promise

questions.text: Deuteronomy 30 says the commandment 'is not too hard for you, nor is it far off... it is in your mouth and in your heart, so that you can do it.' Notice how accessible, not distant, Moses insists Torah is right before Israel enters the land.

Moses sets before Israel 'chayim u-mavet, ha-brachah v'ha-klalah' - life and death, the blessing and the curse - in Deuteronomy 30:19, urging 'choose life.' Why might the choice be framed in such absolute, binary terms rather than as a spectrum?

Before ascending in Acts 1, Yeshua tells His talmidim to wait in Jerusalem for the promised Ruach ha-Kodesh and that they will be His witnesses 'to the ends of the earth.' How does this waiting for empowerment connect to Deuteronomy 31's charge to Joshua, 'be strong and courageous... Adonai your God goes with you' - both moments of leadership transition resting on God's presence rather than human readiness alone?

Psalm 90 prays, 'teach us to number our days, that we may get a heart of wisdom.' What would change about a decision or habit this week if you measured it against the shortness of life rather than against immediate comfort?

Sha'ul later cites Deuteronomy 30's 'the word is near you' language in Romans 10 in relation to faith in Messiah. Discuss whether reading it that way honors or overrides the passage's original context addressed to Israel about Torah, and how both readings might be held together.

theme: The Song of Moses, Moses' Blessing, and Shavuot Fire

questions.text: The Song of Moses in Deuteronomy 32 calls Adonai 'the Rock' repeatedly, whose 'work is perfect, for all His ways are just.' Notice how this song was written specifically to be a witness against Israel in future generations, sung even before its warnings came true.

In Deuteronomy 33, Moses blesses each tribe by name before his death, echoing Jacob's blessing in Genesis 49. What does it mean that Moses, barred from entering the land himself, spends his final recorded words blessing the future of a people he will not personally see settled?

On Shavuot in Acts 2, the Ruach ha-Kodesh falls with the sound of a mighty wind and tongues of fire, and Peter declares the promise fulfilled from the prophet Joel. How does this outpouring, exactly fifty days after Passover on the very festival tied to the giving of Torah at Sinai, connect to the Song of Moses' anticipation in Deuteronomy 32 that Adonai would ultimately act to vindicate His people and land?

Psalm 91 promises that the one who dwells 'in the secret place of the Most High' will find refuge under His wings. What practice of abiding in God's presence, rather than merely asking for protection, could shape how you face a specific fear this week?

Three thousand people respond to Peter's Shavuot sermon in Acts 2 and devote themselves to teaching, fellowship, breaking bread, and prayer, holding property in common. Discuss what elements of that early community's practice are timeless versus specific to their moment, and what a modern community might faithfully adapt today.

theme: Moses' Death and the Promised Prophet Like Him

questions.text: Deuteronomy 34:7 notes that at 120 years old Moses' eye was not dim, nor his vigor abated - what does this detail suggest about the nature of his death compared to the natural decline of old age?
Adonai and the people repeat 'chazak v'ematz' (be strong and courageous) to Joshua three times in chapter 1 - what does this repetition tell you about Joshua's inner state as he takes command?

Deuteronomy 34:10 says no prophet like Moses had yet arisen in Israel; in Acts 3:22-23 Peter applies exactly this prophecy from Deuteronomy 18:15 to Yeshua before the Temple crowd. How does this shape your understanding of Yeshua as a prophet in the Mosaic line?
Joshua 1:8 ties courage directly to meditating on Torah day and night. What single practice could you adopt this week to let Scripture shape your daily courage the way it shaped Joshua's?
Torah warns against adding to Moses' words, yet Peter says a 'prophet like Moses' was still to come. Discuss how these two ideas hold together without one canceling the other.

theme: Rahab's Scarlet Cord and the Jordan Crossing

questions.text: In Joshua 2, Rahab hides the spies among stalks of flax on her roof and negotiates specific terms for her family's safety - what does her bargaining reveal about her understanding of Israel's God?
The Hebrew word for the 'cord' Rahab ties in her window (tikvah) is the same word elsewhere translated 'hope' - how does that double meaning color the scene in Joshua 2:18?

Rahab's scarlet cord marks her household for rescue from judgment, much as Acts 4:12 declares there is salvation in no one else but the name of Yeshua - what connects a physical sign of deliverance to trust in a name?

Rahab risked everything on a report she had only heard about Adonai's power (Joshua 2:9-11). What report about God are you being asked to act on by faith this week, even without having seen it yourself?
Rahab lies to protect the spies, yet is honored in Scripture for her faith. How should believers wrestle with the tension between her deception and her commendation?

theme: Memorial Stones, Gilgal, and the Divine Commander

questions.text: Joshua 4 has twelve stones taken from the Jordan's dry riverbed set up specifically so that children would later ask what they mean - why build a question into the memorial rather than just an explanation?

'Gilgal' (Joshua 5:9) is tied to a Hebrew root for 'rolling,' since Adonai there rolled away the reproach of Egypt through circumcision - what does this wordplay say about the new generation's identity?

The Commander of Adonai's army appears to Joshua with sword drawn, refusing to say whose side He is on (Joshua 5:13-15) - compare this to Acts 5, where an angel of Adonai frees the apostles: both scenes show Heaven's own agenda breaking into human plans.
Joshua removes his sandals on holy ground before he ever fights a battle. What would it look like this week to pause in reverence before acting, rather than rushing straight into the next task?

Gamaliel counsels the Sanhedrin in Acts 5 that a movement not from God will fail on its own. Discuss whether 'wait and see' is always wise, using both Gamaliel's case and Israel's urgent entry into Canaan as contrasts.

theme: Jericho's Fall and Achan's Hidden Sin

questions.text: Joshua 6 describes seven priests blowing shofars as Israel circles Jericho seven days, with silence commanded until the final shout - what does that discipline of silence add to a scene of impending victory?
The Hebrew term 'cherem' (devoted to destruction) governs Jericho's plunder in Joshua 6-7 - how does Achan's violation of cherem in chapter 7 explain Israel's shocking defeat at Ai?

Psalm 95:7-8 warns, 'today, if you hear His voice, do not harden your hearts,' a line later applied in the B'rit Chadashah to entering God's rest through Yeshua. How does Israel's failure at Ai versus its obedience at Jericho illustrate that same warning?
Achan's confession traces sin from seeing, to coveting, to taking, to hiding (Joshua 7:21). Where in your own life this week might you interrupt that sequence earlier, before it takes root?

Achan's sin brought defeat and punishment on the whole camp, including his family. Discuss the discomfort of corporate consequence for individual sin, alongside Acts 6's very different model of shared communal responsibility.

theme: Torah Read at Ebal and Stephen's Sweeping Speech

questions.text: Joshua 8:30-35 has Joshua read all the words of the Torah to the whole assembly, including women, children, and the resident foreigner - why might this inclusion matter at this covenant-renewal moment?
Psalm 96:1 opens 'sing to Adonai a new song' (shir chadash), a phrase that recurs at pivotal moments of fresh deliverance in the Psalms - what 'new' thing might this psalm be marking?

Stephen's speech in Acts 7 retells Israel's covenant history from Abraham through Moses, much as the Torah reading at Ebal recounted the nation's story - how does Stephen use that history to confront his hearers, and how does that compare to Ebal's blessings and curses?

The Gibeonites of Joshua 9 secure a treaty through deception rather than asking honestly. What would it look like this week to seek God's guidance directly rather than manufacturing your own workaround?
Israel keeps its oath to the Gibeonites even though it was obtained by trickery (Joshua 9:19-20). Discuss what this teaches about the weight of a promise once made, even a flawed one.

theme: The Sun Stands Still and Isaiah 53 Explained

questions.text: Joshua 10:12-13 records the sun and moon standing still over Gibeon at Joshua's word so Israel could finish the battle in daylight - what does his boldness in asking this reveal about his relationship with Adonai?
In Acts 8:32-33 the Ethiopian official is reading Isaiah 53's image of a lamb led to slaughter - why might this specific image be the one that opens the door for Philip's explanation of Yeshua?

Philip 'began from this Scripture,' Isaiah 53, to tell the eunuch the good news about Yeshua - one of the clearest direct links between Tanakh prophecy and Yeshua's identity. Which details of Isaiah 53 point most clearly to Him?
The eunuch was reading Scripture in his chariot, seeking understanding, when God sent Philip to meet him there. What ordinary moment of seeking this week might become the place God meets you?

A Gentile eunuch, once excluded from full assembly access by Torah, becomes one of the first non-Israelites recorded receiving the good news and immersion. Discuss how this scene handles inclusion without discarding Israel's distinct covenant identity.

theme: Kings Defeated, Land Divided, Sha'ul Transformed

questions.text: Joshua 12 lists thirty-one kings defeated on both sides of the Jordan, while chapter 13 immediately admits 'very much land remains to be possessed' - what tension does this create about the conquest's completeness?

Saul's Hebrew name means 'asked for' - fitting for a man who, after Acts 9's Damascus road encounter, becomes a 'chosen instrument' set apart for a new purpose. How does his name take on new significance after this chapter?

Acts 9:3-6 describes light from heaven overwhelming Sha'ul and the risen Yeshua confronting him directly - much as the unpossessed land in Joshua 13 shows conquest is an unfolding process, so Sha'ul's transformation is only beginning here.
Ananias hesitates to visit Sha'ul given his violent reputation, yet obeys (Acts 9:13-17). What reluctant obedience is God asking of you this week toward someone you would rather avoid?
Joshua 13 lists territories Israel never fully possessed. Discuss what it means to live faithfully in the tension between promises received and promises not yet fully realized.

theme: Caleb's Wholehearted Inheritance and Cornelius' House

questions.text: At eighty-five, Caleb declares he is as strong as he was forty-five years earlier and asks for the very hill country of the giants, Hebron - what does this reveal about his priorities in old age (Joshua 14:10-12)?
Caleb's story is defined by Numbers 14:24's description of him as one who 'followed Adonai fully' - how does that phrase define his whole life across Joshua 14-15?

Caleb, a Kenizzite by descent yet fully inheriting land within Judah, foreshadows how Acts 10's vision to Peter opens covenant blessing to Cornelius and his household - not replacing Israel, but grafting outsiders into what Adonai is doing among His people.

Caleb's daughter Achsah does not stay silent about her need, but boldly asks for springs of water to go with her dry land (Joshua 15:18-19). What need have you been silent about that you should bring boldly before Adonai this week?
Peter says in Acts 10:34-35 that God shows no partiality. Discuss how this truth sits alongside Israel's ongoing distinct calling as a chosen people - are these in tension, or do they work together?

theme: Zelophehad's Daughters and the Name 'Messianic'

questions.text: Joshua 17:3-6 revisits the daughters of Zelophehad, now receiving their inheritance among Manasseh's clans just as Moses had ruled generations earlier - why might it matter that this precedent is honored under a new leader?

Acts 11:26 records that the talmidim were first called 'Christianoi' at Antioch, from the Greek Christos, itself a translation of the Hebrew Mashiach - what does it mean that this label began as an outsider's description rather than the believers' own name?

Just as Zelophehad's daughters had to press for their rightful place in Israel's inheritance, Acts 11 shows Peter defending Gentile inclusion in Jerusalem, and Antioch becoming the first hub where Jew and Gentile worship together under the name of Messiah.

Ephraim and Manasseh complain their land is too small, and Joshua answers that the forested hill country must be cleared by their own effort (Joshua 17:14-18). Where might God be asking you to clear ground rather than wait for an easier portion?
Zelophehad's daughters had to advocate for a right Torah itself already granted them. Discuss the difference between a promise existing on paper and a promise being lived out in practice.

theme: Shiloh's Tabernacle and Wisdom's Ancient Voice

questions.text: Joshua 18:1 notes the tent of meeting is finally set up at Shiloh once the land is subdued, and lots are cast to divide the rest of the territory - why might Shiloh, rather than a more prominent city, become the center of worship?

Proverbs 8:22-31 personifies Chokmah (Wisdom) as present with Adonai before the hills, working beside Him like a master craftsman (amon) - what does this image communicate about wisdom's relationship to creation itself?

B'rit Chadashah writers echo Proverbs 8's picture of Wisdom present at creation when describing Yeshua's role in bringing all things into being - how does reading Proverbs 8 alongside John's Gospel deepen your sense of who Yeshua is?

Joshua receives his own inheritance, Timnath-serah, only after seeing to everyone else's allotment first (Joshua 19:49-50). What would it look like this week to put others' needs ahead of your own before seeking your due?

Acts 12 shows Ya'akov martyred while Peter is miraculously delivered from the same danger. Discuss how believers can hold together God's sovereignty and the reality that deliverance isn't promised equally to everyone.

theme: Cities of Refuge and a Light to the Nations

questions.text: Joshua 20 establishes six cities of refuge where someone who killed unintentionally could flee the avenger of blood and receive a fair hearing - what does requiring a trial before the elders, rather than immediate refuge, tell you about the balance struck here?
The Hebrew 'miklat' (refuge) in Joshua 20 shares a root meaning 'to gather in, take in' - how does this nuance shape your picture of what these cities offered?

Acts 13:47 has Sha'ul and Barnabas quote Isaiah 49:6, 'I have made you a light to the nations,' to justify preaching to Gentiles at Pisidian Antioch - connect this to the cities of refuge, open to the resident foreigner as much as the native Israelite (Joshua 20:9).

Joshua 22's Transjordan tribes build an altar of witness that nearly triggers civil war before its purpose is clarified through honest conversation. What conflict this week could be defused simply by asking rather than assuming the worst?

The city of refuge protected the unintentional killer but not the deliberate murderer. Discuss what this distinction suggests about how Torah weighs intent, and how it might inform how we judge others' failures today.

theme: Covenant Renewal at Shechem and a Living Choice

questions.text: Joshua 24:2 startlingly reveals that Abraham's own father Terah once served other gods beyond the Euphrates - why might Joshua begin his covenant renewal speech by admitting this uncomfortable family history?

Shechem, the site of this covenant renewal, is also where Abraham first built an altar in the land and where Joseph's bones are finally buried (Joshua 24:32) - how does this location's layered history deepen the weight of Joshua's challenge?

Joshua 24:15's 'choose this day whom you will serve' echoes in Acts 14:15, where Sha'ul and Barnabas urge the crowds at Lystra to turn from vain things to the living God rather than worship them as Zeus and Hermes.

Joshua sets up a stone as witness against the people should they break their word (Joshua 24:27). What visible reminder could you put in place this week to hold yourself accountable to a commitment you've made to God?

The people insist twice, 'we will serve Adonai,' yet Judges immediately shows how quickly that resolve collapsed. Discuss why sincere commitments so often fail to hold, and what might make a covenant renewal genuinely lasting.

theme: Bochim's Weeping and the Jerusalem Council

questions.text: Judges 1 catalogs tribe after tribe failing to fully drive out the Canaanites, ending with Dan pushed back into the hill country by Amorites - what pattern do you notice as the text moves through the tribes one by one?

'Bochim,' meaning 'weepers,' names the place where the angel of Adonai rebukes Israel for making covenants with the Canaanites (Judges 2:1-5) - why might the text preserve this place-name as a permanent marker of that grief?

The Jerusalem council of Acts 15 wrestles with how Gentile believers relate to Torah, noting Moses is read in the synagogues every Sabbath even as Gentiles are welcomed - contrast this careful, Spirit-led deliberation with Judges 2's picture of a generation that simply did not know Adonai.

Judges 2:10 warns of a generation arising that did not know Adonai or the work He had done for Israel. What have you learned from God's past faithfulness that you should intentionally pass on this week?

Acts 15 shows apostles and elders debating publicly before reaching a Spirit-guided consensus. Discuss what this process models for how communities today should handle sharp disagreements over faith and practice.

theme: Deborah's Courage and the Philippian Jailer

questions.text: Judges 4:4-5 introduces Deborah as a prophetess already judging Israel under a palm tree before Barak is even called - what does her established authority suggest about her role compared to earlier judges?

'Devorah' means 'bee' in Hebrew, an image combining productive sweetness with a formidable sting - how does that dual image fit both her prophetic encouragement and her sharp words to Barak (Judges 4:8-9)?

Yael's decisive act of hospitality-turned-deliverance (Judges 4:17-21) and the earthquake that frees Paul and Silas in Acts 16:25-26 both show Adonai delivering His people through unexpected means - what do both teach about where rescue can come from?
The Philippian jailer urgently asks, 'what must I do to be saved?' the moment he understands his danger (Acts 16:30). What is one honest question you've been avoiding asking God directly this week?

The Song of Deborah curses the town of Meroz for failing to join the fight while praising Yael, a non-Israelite, for her decisive action (Judges 5:23). Discuss what this suggests about accountability for those who stay neutral when courage is called for.

theme: Gideon's Fleece and the Bereans' Careful Search

questions.text: Gideon is found threshing wheat inside a winepress to hide it from Midianite raiders when the angel of Adonai greets him as 'mighty warrior' (Judges 6:11-12) - what irony do you notice between how he is addressed and how he is actually behaving?

'Gideon' (Gid'on) derives from a root meaning 'to hew' or 'cut down' - fittingly, his first act of faith is tearing down his father's altar to Baal by night (Judges 6:25-27). How does his name anticipate his mission?

Acts 17:11 praises the Bereans for examining the Scriptures daily to test Sha'ul's teaching - compare their careful verification to Gideon's fleece (Judges 6:36-40), where he too seeks confirmation before acting. How do both show that testing God's word can serve faith rather than oppose it?

Gideon needed two separate confirmations before trusting his call. What decision facing you this week might benefit from seeking godly counsel or confirmation rather than acting on first impulse alone?

Sha'ul, at the Areopagus in Acts 17:22-31, engages Athenian philosophy and quotes their own poets to build a bridge to the gospel. Discuss when engaging outside ideas on their own terms serves the truth, and when it risks compromising it.

theme: Three Hundred Torches and Power in Weakness

questions.text: Adonai deliberately shrinks Gideon's army from thirty-two thousand to three hundred through two tests (Judges 7:2-7) - why does the text explicitly give the reason: lest Israel boast that its own hand saved it?

Shofars and torches hidden inside jars form the entire arsenal of Gideon's three hundred (Judges 7:16) - what does the choice of such unconventional weapons say about the nature of the coming victory?

Gideon's victory depends entirely on Adonai's power working through deliberate human weakness, a theme that resurfaces throughout Sha'ul's Corinthian ministry beginning in Acts 18 - where else in Scripture do you see God favoring small, weak instruments over impressive ones?

The night raid's cry was 'a sword for Adonai and for Gideon' - action and prayer combined rather than one substituting for the other (Judges 7:18-20). Where this week do you need to pair concrete action with dependence on God?

Gideon later builds a golden ephod that becomes 'a snare' to Israel even after refusing to be their king (Judges 8:22-27). Discuss how good intentions and genuine humility can still produce something that leads people astray.

theme: Jotham's Fable and Ephesus' Burning Scrolls

questions.text: Jotham's fable has the trees offer kingship to the olive, fig, and vine before the thornbush accepts, warning that fire will come out from it (Judges 9:7-15) - what point is Jotham making about Abimelech's fitness to rule?
The thornbush in Jotham's fable is called 'atad' in Hebrew, a bramble known for fencing rather than shade or fruit - how does this precise detail sharpen the fable's insult?

Acts 19:19 records new believers in Ephesus publicly burning scrolls of sorcery worth a fortune - a dramatic break from the past, much like Israel's repeated cycle in Judges 9-10 of turning to foreign gods and then tearing down what it had built. What does costly, visible repentance look like in both scenes?

Adonai's biting response to Israel's cry for help is, 'go and cry out to the gods you have chosen' (Judges 10:14). What chosen substitutes might you need to release honestly before God this week rather than expecting rescue around them?

The Ephesian riot in Acts 19:23-41 is driven partly by economic threat to the silversmiths' idol trade. Discuss how financial self-interest can disguise itself as religious or cultural conviction, both then and now.

theme: Jephthah's Vow and Sha'ul's Careful Farewell

questions.text: Jephthah, an outcast driven out by his half-brothers, is the very man Gilead's elders beg to lead them against Ammon (Judges 11:1-11) - what does this reversal suggest about who Adonai chooses to raise up as a deliverer?

'Shibboleth,' the test word of Judges 12:5-6, distinguishes Ephraimite from Gileadite speech by whether the 'sh' sound could be pronounced - how does a single sound becoming a matter of life and death strike you?

Sha'ul's farewell in Acts 20:26-28 insists he is innocent of anyone's blood because he did not shrink from declaring the whole purpose of God - contrast this transparent, careful shepherding with Jephthah's rash, unexamined vow in Judges 11:30-31.

Jephthah's tragedy stems from making a vow without thinking through its cost (Judges 11:35). What promise should you weigh more carefully this week, rather than speaking first and reckoning with consequences later?

Judges 11 leaves Jephthah's daughter's fate morally troubling and much debated among interpreters. Discuss how you personally read this passage, and what it teaches about the danger of binding God to human bargains.

theme: Samson's Nazirite Birth and David's Greater Lord

questions.text: The angel of Adonai appears first to Manoah's unnamed wife, and she is the one who correctly reasons that Adonai would not have shown them these things if He meant to kill them (Judges 13:22-23) - what does her insight reveal about her character?

'Nazir' (Nazirite, one set apart) describes Samson before birth (Judges 13:5) and describes the vow Sha'ul joins in completing in Acts 21:23-26 - how does the same practice appear in radically different contexts across these readings?

Psalm 110:1, 'Adonai says to my Lord, sit at My right hand,' is among the most quoted psalms in the B'rit Chadashah, applied to Yeshua as David's Lord and priest forever after the order of Malki-Tzedek. How does this psalm's structure argue for someone greater than David himself?

Sha'ul willingly takes on a Jewish purification vow in Jerusalem to show his continued respect for Torah observance among Jewish believers (Acts 21:20-26). What might it look like this week to honor a practice out of love for others, even if not strictly required of you?

Samson's riddle at his wedding feast turns into a source of betrayal and violence rather than celebration (Judges 14). Discuss what this episode teaches about the risks of testing relationships through games, wagers, or manipulation.

theme: Samson's Sun-Named Fall, Sha'ul's Bold Defense

questions.text: Samson's name likely relates to 'shemesh' (sun), yet his story ends with his eyes gouged out, grinding blindly in a Philistine prison (Judges 16:21) - what irony do you see in a man named for light ending in total darkness?

Proverbs 18:19 observes that 'a brother offended is harder to win than a strong city' - how does this proverb illuminate the pattern of broken trust running through Samson's relationships in Judges 15-16?

Sha'ul's defense in Acts 22:1-21 publicly retells his transformation from persecutor to messenger of Yeshua before a hostile Jerusalem crowd - contrast his willing vulnerability and submission to God's call with Samson's self-willed, self-destructive strength.

Samson repeatedly gives Delilah partial truths before finally surrendering the whole secret of his strength (Judges 16:6-17). Where are you tempted this week to give a partial, guarded honesty rather than full trust with God or others?

Samson kills more Philistines in his death, pulling down the temple of Dagon, than in his entire life (Judges 16:30). Discuss whether this final act redeems his story, and what it says about God's ability to use flawed, compromised people.

theme: No King in Israel, Sha'ul Before the Sanhedrin

questions.text: Micah steals silver from his own mother, then returns it after her curse, and she dedicates it to make a carved image 'for Adonai' (Judges 17:1-4) - what does this reveal about how easily idolatry can masquerade as devotion?

Judges 17:6 introduces the refrain 'everyone did what was right in his own eyes,' repeated at the book's very end (21:25) - how does this framing phrase color everything that happens between these two chapters?

Sha'ul divides the Sanhedrin in Acts 23:6-9 by declaring his belief in the resurrection of the dead, a genuinely Pharisaic hope rooted in Tanakh - connect this hope to the disorder of Judges 17-18, where a nation without clear direction drifts into private religion and violence.

The Danites in Judges 18 take Micah's idols and priest by force rather than seeking Adonai's actual guidance for their inheritance. What shortcut might you be tempted to take this week instead of patiently seeking God's direction?

Sha'ul's use of the Pharisee-Sadducee conflict to protect himself in Acts 23 raises the question of political savvy versus manipulation. Discuss where you would draw the line between wise strategy and self-serving maneuvering.

theme: Gibeah's Atrocity and Corrupt Justice Under Felix

questions.text: The horrifying events of Judges 19 take place in Gibeah, later the hometown of Israel's first king Saul - what significance might this location carry for readers who already know Gibeah's later royal association?

Psalm 113:7 says Adonai 'raises the poor from the dust... to seat him with princes' - how does this picture of divine reversal contrast with the abandonment suffered by the powerless woman in Judges 19?

Felix, in Acts 24:24-26, listens to Sha'ul discuss righteousness and self-control and is afraid, yet delays justice hoping for a bribe - compare his corrupt handling of a clear moral case to the collapse of justice across Judges 19-20, and consider what both say about humanity's need for a truly righteous Judge.

All Israel unites in outrage yet still causes devastating loss of life on both sides seeking justice for one atrocity (Judges 20). Where this week might righteous anger need to be paired with wisdom about how you respond, not just whether you respond?
Felix keeps Sha'ul imprisoned two years, unwilling to decide justly (Acts 24:27). Discuss how delay itself can function as a form of injustice, and where you see that pattern today.

theme: No King, Then Samuel, and a King's Heart

questions.text: Judges closes with 'there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes,' and 1 Samuel 1 immediately opens with childless Hannah weeping and vowing at Shiloh - what do you make of placing these two moments side by side?
'Hannah' means 'grace' or 'favor' - how does her name anticipate both her prayer for a child and the way she later dedicates that child back to Adonai (1 Samuel 1:27-28)?

Proverbs 21:1 declares 'the king's heart is a stream of water in Adonai's hand' - fitting, given Judges just ended lamenting the absence of a king, and Samuel is born as the very prophet who will anoint Israel's first kings, setting the stage for David's line and ultimately Messiah.

Hannah pours out her deep grief directly to Adonai in prayer rather than to people, even when Eli misunderstands her (1 Samuel 1:12-16). What burden are you carrying that needs honest, unfiltered prayer this week rather than just venting to others?

Sha'ul appeals to Caesar in Acts 25:11-12, a Roman citizen's legal right, while still trusting Adonai's larger purposes. Discuss how believers today should think about using legitimate legal or civic rights while trusting God's sovereignty over outcomes.

theme: Hannah's Song and Samuel's Listening Heart

questions.text: Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas take meat by force from worshipers at the tent of meeting (1 Samuel 2:12-17) even as young Samuel grows 'in favor with Adonai and with men' nearby - what contrast is the text drawing between these two paths?

Young Samuel's response to the voice in the night, 'Hineni' (Here I am, 1 Samuel 3:4), echoes Abraham's and Moses' own responses to God's call - what does this shared word suggest about the posture required to hear from Adonai?

Hannah's song celebrates Adonai who raises the poor from the dust and exalts the lowly (1 Samuel 2:1-10), themes clearly echoed centuries later in Miriam's song at Yeshua's coming. Read alongside Acts 26, where Sha'ul insists his resurrection hope is nothing but what the prophets and Moses said, how do both texts anchor future hope in past promise?

Samuel has to be taught by Eli how to recognize Adonai's voice before he can respond rightly (1 Samuel 3:8-9). What practice this week might help you better recognize God's voice amid the noise of daily life?

Eli knows of his sons' sin and rebukes them mildly but fails to restrain them, bringing judgment on his whole household (1 Samuel 2:22-25; 3:13). Discuss the weight of a leader's responsibility to act, not merely to disapprove.

theme: Ichabod's Departed Glory, Protection at Sea

questions.text: Phinehas's wife, dying in childbirth after hearing of the Ark's capture and her husband's and father-in-law's deaths, names her son Ichabod, saying 'the glory has departed from Israel' (1 Samuel 4:21-22) - why does she focus on the Ark's loss above the personal tragedies around her?
'Ichabod' combines a Hebrew term for 'no' or 'where' with 'kavod' (glory, weight, honor) - how does this compound name capture the theological crisis of chapter 4?

Dagon's idol falls facedown and breaks before the captured Ark in Ashdod (1 Samuel 5:1-5), showing Adonai's supremacy even in exile - compare this to Acts 27, where an angel assures Sha'ul that God will preserve everyone aboard the storm-tossed ship, the same sovereign protection extending into Gentile, foreign territory.

Israel treated the Ark almost like a talisman in chapter 4, expecting it to guarantee victory apart from genuine faithfulness. What religious object or routine might you be tempted to lean on this week instead of a genuine relationship with God?

The Philistines suffer plagues wherever the Ark goes, yet keep moving it rather than returning it immediately (1 Samuel 5). Discuss what this stubbornness illustrates about how slowly people sometimes respond to unmistakable evidence of God's power.

theme: Ebenezer's Stone and the Gospel Reaching Rome

questions.text: The Philistines send the Ark back on a cart pulled by nursing cows separated from their calves, testing whether the cows will instinctively return home or head toward Israel against nature (1 Samuel 6:7-12) - what does this test reveal about their own uncertainty regarding Israel's God?

Samuel names a stone 'Ebenezer,' meaning 'stone of help,' declaring 'until now Adonai has helped us' (1 Samuel 7:12) - why might marking a specific moment of help matter more than a general statement of gratitude?

Psalm 117, the shortest psalm, calls all nations and all peoples to praise Adonai - a fitting companion to Acts 28, where Sha'ul finally reaches Rome and preaches Yeshua's kingdom with all boldness and without hindrance to the ends of the known world.

Samuel calls Israel to first put away the foreign gods before leading them into battle against the Philistines (1 Samuel 7:3-4). What would genuine, specific repentance, not just a general prayer, look like for you this week?

Acts 28 ends with the gospel preached openly in Rome, yet with mixed reception among Jewish leaders there, some persuaded and some not. Discuss how you make sense of a divided response to the same clear message, then and now.

theme: Israel Demands a King; the Rejected Cornerstone

questions.text: The elders demand a king 'to judge us like all the nations,' and Adonai tells Samuel the people have rejected Him, not Samuel, as their king (1 Samuel 8:5-20) - what does Adonai's response reveal about how He interprets Israel's request?

Psalm 118:22 declares 'the stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone,' using a Hebrew wordplay between 'even' (stone) and 'ben' (son) - how does this image of rejection-then-exaltation echo across Scripture?

Romans 1:16 states the gospel is the power of God for salvation 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek,' directly affirming Israel's priority even as the good news expands outward - connect this to Psalm 118:22, later quoted by Yeshua Himself about His own rejection and exaltation.

Samuel warns Israel in detail what a king like the nations will cost them (1 Samuel 8:11-18), yet they insist anyway. What desire are you pursuing this week despite having been warned honestly about its cost?

Romans 1:18-32 describes humanity exchanging the truth of God for a lie and being given over to its own desires. Discuss how this warning about idolatry connects to Israel's demand for a king like the nations instead of trusting Adonai's kingship.

theme: Torah in the Heart and True Circumcision

questions.text: Saul is found prophesying among a band of ecstatic prophets after his anointing, prompting the proverb 'is Saul also among the prophets?' (1 Samuel 10:10-12) - what does this unexpected transformation suggest about how Adonai equips someone for a new calling?

Psalm 119 is built as a Hebrew acrostic using near-synonyms for Torah - chukim, mishpatim, mitzvot, edot - throughout its 176 verses. Why might the psalmist need so many different words to describe his love for God's instruction?

Romans 2:28-29 declares that true circumcision 'is that of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter' - read alongside Psalm 119:11, 'Your word I have hidden in my heart,' how do both texts point to the same truth that Torah was always meant to be internalized, not merely external?

Saul rescues Jabesh-gilead by decisively cutting oxen into pieces and sending them throughout Israel as an urgent summons (1 Samuel 11:7). What situation this week calls for that kind of decisive action rather than delay?

Romans 2 insists Jew and Gentile alike will be judged by the same standard, 'for God shows no partiality,' while Scripture elsewhere affirms real advantages to being entrusted with Torah. Discuss how impartial judgment and real covenantal privilege fit together.

theme: Samuel's Farewell and Righteousness for All

questions.text: Samuel calls down thunder and rain during the dry wheat harvest as a sign confirming Israel's sin in demanding a king (1 Samuel 12:16-18), and the people respond in fear, asking Samuel to pray for them - what does their fear reveal about how seriously they now grasp his warning?

Psalm 120:5 laments 'Woe is me, that I sojourn in Meshech, that I dwell among the tents of Kedar,' remote and hostile territories used poetically - what does this image of exile-like distress capture about the songs of ascent it opens?

Romans 3:29 asks pointedly, 'is He the God of Jews only? Is He not also of Gentiles?' - a question that resonates with Samuel's insistence in 1 Samuel 12 that Israel's king still answers to Adonai's kingship over all people, not a tribal deity confined to one nation.

Saul grows impatient waiting for Samuel and unlawfully offers the burnt offering himself, costing him his dynasty (1 Samuel 13:8-14). What situation this week is tempting you to act prematurely rather than wait on God's timing?

Romans 3:23-24 insists all have sinned and are justified as a gift by His grace, a universal diagnosis and universal remedy. Discuss how this squares with 1 Samuel 13's specific, individual accountability for Saul's disobedience.

theme: Jonathan's Boldness and Abraham's Counted Faith

questions.text: Jonathan tells his armor-bearer that 'nothing can hinder Adonai from saving by many or by few' before the two of them alone attack a Philistine garrison (1 Samuel 14:6) - what does his reasoning reveal about where he places his confidence before the odds are known?

Samuel tells Saul 'to obey is better than sacrifice' (1 Samuel 15:22), using the Hebrew 'shama' (to hear, listen, obey) - how does this single word capture the real issue behind Saul's failure to fully destroy Amalek?

Romans 4:3 quotes Genesis 15:6, 'Abraham believed Adonai, and it was credited to him as righteousness,' noting this happened before his circumcision, making him father of all who believe - connect this to Samuel's point that obedience from the heart matters more than the outward form of sacrifice.

Saul's rash oath nearly costs Jonathan his life over honey eaten in ignorance during battle (1 Samuel 14:24-45). Where might strict rule-keeping this week need to yield to the deeper purpose behind the rule?

Saul spares king Agag and the best Amalekite flocks, claiming this was to sacrifice to Adonai, yet Samuel calls it rebellion (1 Samuel 15:15-21). Discuss how good-sounding religious justifications can sometimes mask plain disobedience, and how you can tell the difference.

theme: David Anointed, Goliath Falls, Grace Abounds

questions.text: Adonai tells Samuel not to judge by Eliab's height or looks, for 'man looks at the outward appearance, but Adonai looks at the heart' (16:7). What does it say about Israel's coming king that he is found tending sheep when Samuel arrives?
Samuel 'mashach' (anoints) David with oil in 16:13 -- the very verb behind the title 'Mashiach.' What does it mean that David is a mashiach long before he ever wears a crown?

David the anointed shepherd stands alone for Israel against Goliath's taunt against 'the armies of the living God' (17:26,45). How does his single combat picture the greater reversal Romans 5 describes, where one Man's obedience undoes what one man's trespass brought?

David refuses Saul's armor, trusting instead the sling and stones he has already proven in caring for sheep (17:38-40). What 'armor' are you tempted to borrow this week that isn't suited to how God has actually equipped you?

Saul's jealousy erupts after the women sing 'Saul has slain his thousands, David his ten thousands' (18:7-9), while Jonathan instead binds himself to David in covenant love (18:1,3). What makes comparison so corrosive, and covenant loyalty so redemptive?

theme: Covenant Friendship and Freedom from Sin's Slavery

questions.text: Michal lowers David through a window and places a household idol in his bed to fool Saul's men (19:12-16). What does this detail about an idol sitting in the royal household reveal about Saul's own spiritual state?

Jonathan and David renew their bond with an arrow-signal at the stone Ezel, asking for 'chesed' -- covenant loyalty -- to be shown between their houses forever (20:14-17). What does this word add to a friendship built on more than affection?

Jonathan lays down his own claim to the throne out of love for David (20:14-17,31). How does this self-emptying loyalty anticipate the union with Messiah's death and life that Romans 6 describes through baptism?
Romans 6 asks, 'shall we go on sinning that grace may increase?' What specific old pattern do you need to consider dead this week rather than indulged under the cover of grace?

David lies to Ahimelech and flees for his life while Saul's rage escalates unchecked. Discuss the tension between complete honesty and self-preservation under real threat, using David's choices here as a case study.

theme: Bread of the Presence and the Law's Limits

questions.text: Ahimelech gives the fleeing David the consecrated showbread and Goliath's sword kept behind the ephod (21:1-9). Why would a priest hand over holy bread and a trophy weapon to a fugitive?

Doeg the Edomite, Saul's chief herdsman, becomes the informer who triggers the slaughter of Nob's priests (22:9-19). What does his position of trust turned into betrayal suggest about the danger of misused access?

Yeshua later cites David eating the showbread to defend mercy over rigid legalism. How does Romans 7's statement that the law is 'holy, righteous, and good' yet powerless to conquer sin illuminate why this exception mattered?
Eighty-five priests die because Saul's fear curdles into violence (22:18-19). How does unchecked fear in leaders still endanger the innocent today, and what guards your own reactions against it?
David later admits his lie to Ahimelech set the massacre in motion (22:22). How should we weigh unintended consequences of our choices, even ones that felt necessary for survival at the time?

theme: Sparing Saul and Life in the Ruach

questions.text: Jonathan finds David at Horesh and 'strengthened his hand in God' rather than in the throne he stood to inherit (23:16). What does this scene reveal about friendship that serves another's calling over one's own interest?

In the cave at En Gedi, David cuts off a corner ('kanaph,' the same word used for a robe's tzitzit corner) of Saul's robe (24:4-5). Why might even this small act against 'Adonai's anointed' strike David's conscience so sharply?

David refuses to harm 'the LORD's mashiach' even with the chance and apparent right to do so (24:6-7). How does this restraint anticipate Romans 8's assurance that nothing can separate us from a love that is given, never seized by force?
Romans 8 says those led by the Ruach ha-Kodesh are children of God who cry 'Abba, Father.' Where this week could you trade self-vindication for trust in God's timing, as David did in that cave?
David spares Saul twice yet remains a hunted fugitive for years afterward. What does this teach about the cost of righteousness even when God's promises to us are already certain?

theme: Abigail's Wisdom and Sha'ul's Anguish for Israel

questions.text: Abigail intercepts David with provisions and a plea that averts bloodguilt before her husband even learns of the danger (25:18-35). What specific qualities let her avert disaster where Nabal's foolishness nearly caused it?

Abigail tells David, 'his name means Fool, and folly is with him' (25:25) -- Nabal's name itself, 'naval,' means senseless fool. How does this wordplay function as character judgment within the story?

David again spares Saul's life at Hachilah, taking only the spear and water jug as proof, refusing to 'stretch out his hand against Adonai's mashiach' (26:9-12). How does Sha'ul's grief in Romans 9 over kinsmen who reject the true Anointed One echo this same reverence for God's chosen instrument, even when misunderstood?
