Abigail acts decisively and humbly to avert violence and restore peace. What situation this week calls you to intervene wisely rather than wait passively for it to resolve itself?

Sha'ul says he could wish himself 'cursed and cut off from Messiah' for the sake of his kinsmen (9:3). Discuss how such intense identification with one's own people should shape how believers today regard the Jewish people and Israel's ongoing calling.

theme: David's Deception, Saul's Despair, Faith by Hearing

questions.text: David deceives Achish of Gath about which peoples he is actually raiding while sheltering among the Philistines at Ziklag (27:8-12). How does this episode complicate our picture of David as a man after God's own heart?

Desperate, Saul seeks a medium, a 'ba'alat ov,' at En Dor after Adonai no longer answers him 'by dreams, Urim, or prophets' (28:6-7). What does his turn to forbidden divination reveal about the silence he has brought on himself?

Romans 10 declares 'faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word about Messiah' (10:17). How does Saul's tragic recourse to necromancy once God stopped speaking contrast with the near, accessible 'word of faith' Sha'ul describes?
Psalm 127 insists 'unless Adonai builds the house, the builders labor in vain.' What striving in your life needs to be entrusted to God's building rather than your own effort this week?

Saul's slide from anointed king to consulting a medium raises hard questions about when God 'gives someone over' to their own choices. How do we hold together divine sovereignty and human responsibility in his downfall?

theme: Recovering All and the Unbreakable Root

questions.text: The Philistine commanders refuse to let David fight alongside them, fearing he might turn on them mid-battle (29:3-5). How does this providential exclusion rescue David from an otherwise impossible moral position?

After Ziklag is burned, David 'strengthened himself in Adonai his God' before inquiring through the ephod (30:6). What does this reflexive act of self-strengthening rooted in God, rather than circumstance, model for us?

David rules that spoil be shared equally between those who fought and those who stayed with the supplies (30:24-25). How does this anticipate Romans 11's picture of wild branches sharing together in the richness of Israel's own root?
Where do you need to 'strengthen yourself in Adonai' the way David did before taking any action this week, rather than acting from panic like his grieving men first did?

Romans 11 warns Gentile believers against arrogance toward the 'natural branches,' insisting 'God has not rejected His people.' Discuss what this means practically for how the church should regard Israel and Jewish identity today.

theme: Saul's Fall, David's Lament, a Living Sacrifice

questions.text: Saul falls on his own sword to avoid Philistine abuse, and the men of Jabesh-gilead risk themselves to retrieve his body for burial (31:4,11-13). Why might loyalty to Saul persist here even after everything that has happened?

David's lament orders the 'song of the bow' taught to Judah, repeating 'eikh naphlu gibborim' -- 'how the mighty have fallen' (1:17-27). What does this refrain accomplish structurally across the poem?

David mourns even the king who hunted him for years, refusing to celebrate his rival's death. How does this anticipate Romans 12's call to 'bless those who persecute you' and 'overcome evil with good' (12:14,21)?

Romans 12 calls believers to present their bodies as a 'living sacrifice.' What would it look like this week to meet someone who has wronged you with grief rather than vindication, as David did at news of Saul's death?

David executes the Amalekite messenger for claiming to have killed 'Adonai's anointed,' even though the man expected a reward (1:14-16). What does this reveal about David's view of the sanctity of God's anointed, and what might it mean for how leaders are honored today?

theme: A Kingdom Divided, Waiting for Adonai

questions.text: David is anointed king over Judah at Hebron while Abner installs Ish-bosheth over the rest of Israel, and their camps clash in a deadly contest of young men at Gibeon (2:12-17). What does this fratricidal conflict cost both houses?

After Joab kills Abner in revenge for Asahel, David declares himself and his kingdom 'naqi' -- innocent, clear -- 'before Adonai forever' (3:28). What weight does this word of bloodguilt carry in David's public declaration?

David waits years for the throne already promised to him rather than seizing it by force, even publicly mourning his rival Abner (3:31-34). How does this patient trust echo Psalm 130's 'I wait for Adonai, my soul waits, and in His word I put my hope'?

Romans 13 calls believers to respect governing authority and to 'put on the Lord Yeshua the Messiah.' Where this week might patience with unresolved conflict be the more faithful path, as with David's restraint here?

Joab's private vengeance against Abner undermines David's public diplomacy. Discuss the tension between personal grievance and communal good, and how leaders today navigate followers who act outside sanctioned authority.

theme: Jerusalem Taken, a Weaned Soul at Rest

questions.text: David captures Jerusalem from the Jebusites, reportedly through the water shaft, and makes it 'the city of David' (5:6-9). Why is this previously unconquered stronghold so strategically and symbolically significant for a newly united kingdom?
Psalm 131 pictures the soul 'like a weaned child with its mother' -- 'gamul' (131:2). What does the image of a weaned, rather than nursing, child add to this picture of contented trust?

The elders anoint David king over all Israel, recalling Adonai's word that David would 'shepherd My people' (5:1-3). How does this shepherd-king language reach forward to Messiah, Son of David, as the ultimate shepherd?

Romans 14 addresses disputable matters like food and set-apart days, urging believers not to judge one another but to act from conviction before God. What practice of your own do you hold with humility rather than as a weapon against others?

Romans 14 insists 'the kingdom of God is not eating and drinking.' In a Messianic Jewish context where dietary and calendar practices carry real weight, how do we honor conscience and Torah-observance without turning disputable matters into tests of fellowship?

theme: The Ark Comes Home, an Everlasting House Promised

questions.text: Uzzah reaches out to steady the ark and dies; only three months later, after leaving it with Obed-Edom, does David bring it up with rejoicing and dancing (6:6-15). What does the gap between the failed and the successful attempt teach about approaching holy things on God's terms?

In Nathan's oracle, Adonai promises David a 'bayit' -- house -- meaning both dynasty and Temple, pledging his throne will stand 'olam,' forever (7:11-16). What tension and richness does this wordplay on 'house' carry?

The everlasting Davidic covenant undergirds Messiah's eternal throne. How does Romans 15's statement that Messiah became 'a servant to the circumcised to confirm the promises made to the fathers' (15:8) root Yeshua's mission directly in this covenant?
David wants to build God a house, but is told God will build him one instead. What plans of your own this week might need to be surrendered to God's better, unasked-for provision?

Michal despises David for dancing before the ark 'undignified' in the people's eyes (6:16,20-23). Discuss the tension between wholehearted worship and social propriety -- whose instinct resonates more with you, David's or Michal's?

theme: Kindness for Jonathan's Sake, Unity Like Oil

questions.text: David seeks out Jonathan's crippled son Mephibosheth 'that I may show him kindness for Jonathan's sake,' restoring Saul's land and granting him a permanent seat at the king's table (9:1,7,13). What makes this act so unusual for an ancient Near Eastern king toward a rival dynasty's heir?

David's kindness to Mephibosheth is called 'chesed Elohim' -- the kindness of God (9:3). What does attaching 'chesed' directly to God's own character suggest about the source and scope of David's mercy here?

David welcomes a broken descendant of his rival into permanent covenant favor purely on account of a prior promise, not merit. How does this anticipate the grace by which Messiah welcomes the undeserving into God's household?

Psalm 133 celebrates how good it is 'when brothers dwell together in unity,' likened to oil running down Aaron's beard. What relationship in your life needs the deliberate, costly kindness David showed Mephibosheth this week?

Romans 16 closes with warnings about those who 'cause divisions' right alongside warm greetings to a wide diversity of coworkers. Discuss how a community can hold both vigilance against division and radical hospitality at once.

theme: Uriah's Loyalty, David's Sin, the Cross's Folly

questions.text: Uriah the Hittite refuses to go home to his wife while the ark and Israel's army camp in the field, sleeping at the palace door instead (11:9-11). What does his loyalty expose about David's own failure by contrast?

Hanun's men shave half the beards of David's envoys and cut their garments at the buttocks, a deliberate act of 'kalam' -- shame, humiliation (10:4-5). Why would this specific insult provoke full-scale war?

David's sin with Bathsheba and the murder of the loyal Uriah occur in the very line that will produce Messiah through Solomon. How does 1 Corinthians 1's claim that God chooses 'what is foolish' and 'what is weak' to shame the strong resonate with grace working through such a compromised royal line?

1 Corinthians 1 warns against boasting in human wisdom or status. Where might unchecked comfort -- David remaining in Jerusalem instead of going to battle (11:1) -- be quietly setting you up for compromise this week?

Uriah, a Hittite convert, appears more faithful to Israel's covenant obligations than the king himself, and dies for David's cover-up. Discuss what this says about how outsiders can embody covenant faithfulness better than those born into it.

theme: Nathan's Parable, Tamar's Grief, Spirit-Given Wisdom

questions.text: Nathan tells David a parable of a rich man stealing a poor man's only ewe lamb, and David condemns himself before Nathan says 'you are the man' (12:1-7). Why is an indirect story more effective here than a direct accusation?

David's confession is simply 'chatati' -- I have sinned (12:13) -- met at once by Nathan's word that Adonai has 'put away' his sin. What does the brevity of both confession and pardon suggest about genuine teshuvah?

1 Corinthians 2 says believers speak 'God's wisdom in a mystery, the hidden wisdom,' revealed by the Ruach and not the wisdom of this age's rulers. How does Nathan's Spirit-given insight into David's hidden sin model this same wisdom that exposes what human eyes alone cannot see?

Tamar is violated by her half-brother Amnon and left 'desolate' in Absalom's house (13:14-20). How should this account shape the church's responsibility to protect and actually believe victims within families and communities?

David disciplines neither Amnon for the assault nor, at first, Absalom for avenging it two years later. Discuss how a parent's or leader's failure to address wrongdoing decisively can compound into greater tragedy.

theme: Absalom's Revolt and the Only True Foundation

questions.text: Absalom positions himself at the city gate each morning telling petitioners, 'if only I were judge,' and so 'stole the hearts of the men of Israel' (15:2-6). What specific tactics does he use to build a rival following?

The wise woman of Tekoa argues that 'we must all die... but God devises means so that the banished one is not cast out from him' (14:14). What does her use of 'nadach' -- banished, driven away -- suggest about exile and restoration?

1 Corinthians 3 insists 'no one can lay a foundation other than the one already laid, which is Yeshua the Messiah.' How does Absalom's self-built foundation of popularity contrast with the only foundation Sha'ul says will survive testing?

Psalm 136's refrain, 'His lovingkindness endures forever,' repeats through every line. What recurring truth about God's chesed do you need to deliberately rehearse this week, the way this psalm structurally repeats it?

David flees Jerusalem rather than fight his own son, weeping as he climbs the Mount of Olives (15:30). Discuss what David's refusal to defend his throne by force reveals about costly, humble leadership.

theme: Shimei's Curse, Hushai's Counsel, Weeping by Babylon

questions.text: Shimei curses David and hurls stones as he flees, and David forbids Abishai from killing him, saying 'perhaps Adonai will look on my affliction' (16:5-12). What does David's restraint reveal about his theology of suffering?

Ahithophel's counsel is described as being 'as if one consulted the word of God' (16:23). What makes his eventual defeat by Hushai's counsel, and subsequent suicide, so striking against that description?

Psalm 137 laments, 'how can we sing Adonai's song in a foreign land' beside Babylon's rivers. How might this exile-longing connect with 1 Corinthians 4's picture of the apostles suffering as 'the scum of the world' while still remaining faithful stewards?

1 Corinthians 4 warns against inflated self-importance, asking 'what do you have that you did not receive?' Where might Absalom's self-promotion or Ahithophel's self-important counsel caution against your own hidden pride this week?

Ahithophel, once David's trusted counselor, defects to Absalom and hangs himself when his advice is rejected (17:23). Discuss what this tragic figure teaches about wounded loyalty and tying one's identity entirely to being heeded.

theme: O Absalom My Son, Cleaning Out the Leaven

questions.text: Absalom is caught suspended in a great oak's branches, and Joab kills him against David's explicit order to 'deal gently' with him (18:9-15). Why might Joab have felt justified overriding the king's direct command?
David's lament repeats 'beni' -- my son -- five times over Absalom (18:33). How does this repetition convey grief that overrides political relief at the rebellion's end?

1 Corinthians 5 calls believers to 'clean out the old leaven' of tolerated sin, invoking Passover imagery: 'Messiah our Passover has been sacrificed.' How does David's long failure to deal with sin in his own house illustrate the danger Sha'ul warns against with such urgency?

Joab has to rebuke David for grieving so publicly that he shames the soldiers who just saved his kingdom (19:5-7). Where this week might legitimate grief need to be balanced against responsibility to those depending on you?

David publicly forgives Shimei and restores Mephibosheth's status amid disputed loyalty claims, welcoming back a fractured kingdom (19:16-30). Discuss whether reconciliation without full clarity or justice is wise or risky.

theme: Sheba's Revolt, Rizpah's Vigil, Fully Known

questions.text: A wise woman of Abel Beth-maacah negotiates with Joab to end Sheba's revolt without further bloodshed to her city (20:16-22). What does her intervention reveal about wisdom exercised outside official structures of power?

Rizpah spreads sackcloth on a rock and guards the exposed bodies of Saul's sons 'from the beginning of harvest until the rain fell' (21:10). What does her extended, silent vigil communicate about dignity owed even to the condemned?

Psalm 139 declares, 'You have searched me and known me... such knowledge is too wonderful for me' (139:1,6). How does this total, intimate divine knowledge anticipate 1 Corinthians 6's claim that believers' bodies are 'a temple of the Ruach ha-Kodesh' who dwells within us?

1 Corinthians 6 asks, 'do you not know that the saints will judge the world?' yet believers were suing each other before pagan courts. What dispute in your life this week might be better resolved within community wisdom rather than adversarial escalation?

Rizpah's quiet protest moves David to finally give Saul's family proper burial. Discuss what her example says about the power of one person's persistent, non-violent witness to correct an ignored wrong.

theme: David's Song of Deliverance, the Mighty Men's Water

questions.text: Three of David's mighty men break through Philistine lines to bring him water from the well at Bethlehem, and David refuses to drink it, pouring it out to Adonai instead (23:15-17). Why would he treat water fetched at such risk as too costly to simply drink?

David's song calls Adonai his 'sela' -- rock, crag -- repeatedly (22:2-3), a term for the very wilderness refuges he knew intimately as a fugitive. How does this concrete, geographic metaphor deepen the meaning of divine protection?

David's 'last words' oracle (23:1-7) describes a future ruler who governs 'in the fear of God' like 'the light of morning.' How does this messianic oracle from the 'sweet singer of Israel' reach forward to the greater Son of David?

1 Corinthians 7 counsels believers to remain content in the situation to which they were called. What circumstance are you currently in that this passage invites you to steward faithfully rather than restlessly escape?

David pours out the water his men risked their lives for rather than drinking it, treating their sacrifice as too sacred to casually consume. What postures of honoring others' sacrifice might be missing in how we receive what people give us today?

theme: The Threshing Floor Bought, Love that Builds

questions.text: David insists on paying full price for Araunah's threshing floor rather than accepting it as a gift, saying, 'I will not offer... that which costs me nothing' (24:24). Why does this principle matter for what becomes the future Temple site?

David prays, 'let my prayer be counted as incense before You' -- 'ketoret' (Psalm 141:2), drawing directly on Tabernacle worship imagery. What does comparing prayer to the incense offering suggest about how prayer functions?

Araunah's threshing floor becomes, by tradition, the future Temple Mount, purchased at David's own cost after his census sin. How does this costly restored relationship echo 1 Corinthians 8's principle that love, not mere rights or knowledge, should shape our conduct toward others?

1 Corinthians 8 warns that exercising a 'right' can wound a weaker brother's conscience. What freedom of yours might need to be voluntarily limited this week out of love rather than insisted upon out of correctness?

Adonijah crowns himself king while David still lives, and Solomon is anointed only through Nathan and Bathsheba's intervention (1 Kings 1:5-40). Discuss what this succession crisis teaches about assumed entitlement to leadership versus divinely appointed calling.

theme: Solomon's Wisdom Requested and Received

questions.text: At Gibeon, Solomon asks not for riches or long life but for 'an understanding heart to judge Your people, to discern between good and evil' (3:9). Why might this specific request please Adonai more than any other?

Solomon's request for a 'lev shome'a' -- literally a hearing heart (3:9) -- becomes the basis for his famous judgment between two women disputing a living child (3:16-28). What does this Hebrew phrase suggest wisdom fundamentally is?

Solomon's wisdom becomes so renowned that rulers travel to hear him, later echoed in the 'something greater than Solomon' language applied to Yeshua. How does 1 Corinthians 9's picture of Sha'ul becoming 'all things to all people' reflect the responsibility that comes with God-given wisdom or influence?

Under Solomon, 'every man lived under his own vine and fig tree' in peace (4:25). What would cultivating that kind of settled contentment, rather than restless striving, look like in your life this week?

Solomon executes Adonijah, Joab, and Shimei to secure his throne (2:13-46). Discuss the moral tension between establishing justice and political stability and the ruthlessness with which Solomon begins his reign.

theme: Building the Temple in Silence, Seeking Guidance

questions.text: The Temple's stones are cut and finished at the quarry so that 'no hammer, chisel, or any iron tool was heard in the house while it was being built' (6:7). What significance might this silence at the sacred building site carry?

Solomon's alliance with Hiram of Tyre for cedar is sealed with 'karat brit' -- the two of them 'made a covenant' (5:12) -- covenant language applied to what is essentially a trade agreement. What does this suggest about how Israel related to surrounding nations?

1 Corinthians 10 recalls that Israel's wilderness generation 'drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and the rock was Messiah.' How does the Temple, built as the place for God's Name and Presence to dwell (6:11-13), anticipate the deeper truth that Messiah himself is where God dwells with His people?

Psalm 143 pleads, 'teach me to do Your will... let Your good Spirit lead me' (143:10). What decision this week calls for that kind of dependent, moment-by-moment guidance rather than self-directed planning?

1 Corinthians 10 warns believers not to repeat Israel's wilderness failures while still affirming shared spiritual experience -- 'all ate the same spiritual food.' Discuss what it means to learn from Israel's history without dismissing or idealizing it.

theme: The Glory Fills the House, Remembering Rightly

questions.text: When the ark enters the finished Temple, 'the cloud filled the house of Adonai' so thickly that 'the priests could not stand to minister' (8:10-11). What does this echo of the Tabernacle's dedication in Exodus signal about continuity in God's presence?

Solomon's dedication prayer repeatedly asks Adonai to 'shama' -- hear, heed -- 'when they pray toward this place' (8:29-30,35). Why does the prayer emphasize hearing rather than simply requesting blessing?

Solomon acknowledges that even the highest heavens 'cannot contain' God, let alone this house (8:27). How does this point toward 1 Corinthians 11's instructions on the Lord's Supper, where believers proclaim Messiah's presence 'until He comes' without any physical temple?

1 Corinthians 11 warns against approaching the Lord's Supper 'in an unworthy manner' without self-examination. Solomon's whole prayer assumes that same humble posture before God's presence -- what in your own approach to worship needs it this week?

Solomon's prayer anticipates Israel's future exile and pleads that even then, prayer toward Jerusalem and this Temple would be heard (8:46-50). Discuss what this reveals about hope sustained even through anticipated failure and judgment.

theme: A Conditional Blessing and the Queen's Wonder

questions.text: Adonai appears to Solomon a second time, warning that if Israel turns to other gods, the Temple just built could become 'a heap of ruins' and 'a byword' (9:6-9). How does this warning temper the celebration of the previous chapters?

The Queen of Sheba, after witnessing Solomon's wisdom and wealth, says 'there was no more breath (ruach) left in her' (10:5). What does this striking idiom communicate about the scale of what she saw?

Yeshua later says, 'something greater than Solomon is here,' referencing the Queen of Sheba's journey to witness wisdom. How does 1 Corinthians 12's teaching that varied gifts serve 'one body' reflect a wisdom greater than any single ruler's, distributed across the whole community of Messiah?

Psalm 145 says Adonai is 'gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and great in lovingkindness' (145:8). What specific attribute of God from this psalm do you most need to rehearse and trust this week?

Solomon's wealth and foreign alliances set up the very compromises that unravel his kingdom in the next chapter. Discuss how blessing and prosperity, even when directly from God, can carry the seeds of future compromise.

theme: Solomon's Turned Heart and the Greatest Gift

questions.text: Solomon's foreign wives 'turned his heart after other gods,' leading him to build high places for Ashtoreth, Chemosh, and Milcom (11:4-8). How does this explain the kingdom's fracture as much a spiritual failure as a political one?

Rehoboam threatens the people with 'scorpions' instead of his father's 'whips' (12:11,14). What does this proverbial escalation reveal about his approach to leadership compared to the wise counsel he rejected?

The kingdom's tragic split under Jeroboam and Rehoboam foreshadows Israel's long history of division, healed only in the greater Son of David. How does 1 Corinthians 13's description of love that 'does not seek its own' stand against Rehoboam's self-serving harshness?
1 Corinthians 13 insists that gifts and knowledge without love amount to 'nothing.' Where this week might you be tempted to prioritize being right or powerful over loving patiently?

Jeroboam sets up golden calves at Bethel and Dan so the people won't return to worship in Jerusalem (12:26-30). Discuss how political convenience can corrupt worship, and where similar temptations might exist in religious communities today.

theme: The Man of God Deceived, Order in Worship

questions.text: The unnamed man of God from Judah correctly prophesies against Jeroboam's altar, then is deceived by an old prophet's false claim of new revelation, leading to his death by a lion (13:1-24). What does this tragedy warn about testing claimed revelation, even from a fellow prophet?

Though blind with age, Ahijah recognizes Jeroboam's disguised wife by divine revelation and pronounces the word he was told to speak against the coming judgment on Jeroboam's house (14:5-6). What does his physical blindness paired with clear spiritual perception suggest?

1 Corinthians 14 insists that everything in worship gatherings be done 'decently and in order' for the building up of the body. How does the chaos and deception surrounding true and false prophecy in 1 Kings 13 underscore the discernment Sha'ul calls for regarding prophetic words?

Psalm 147 says Adonai 'heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds,' while also numbering the stars (147:3-4). What broken area of your life this week needs you to trust the God who tends both the vast and the intimate?

The man of God obeys Adonai's specific command until an apparent prophetic word contradicts it, and dies for the inconsistency. Discuss what standard we should use for testing a 'word from the Lord' against Scripture's already-revealed instruction today.

theme: Asa's Reforms, Omri's Rise, Resurrection Hope

questions.text: King Asa removes his own grandmother Maacah from her position as queen mother for making an idol, burning it at the Kidron valley (15:13). What does this costly, personal reform reveal about the seriousness of Asa's commitment?

Omri 'bought the hill of Shomron' and built the city that would become Israel's capital, named after its previous owner Shemer (16:24). What does establishing this new capital signal about the northern kingdom's growing instability?

1 Corinthians 15 declares, 'if Messiah has not been raised, your faith is futile,' describing resurrection as the guarantee of victory over death. How does the chaotic, short-lived succession of northern kings in this chapter contrast with the permanence Sha'ul promises through resurrection life in Messiah?

1 Corinthians 15 calls believers to 'always excel in the work of the Lord, since your labor in Him is not in vain.' What discouragement about the fruit of your efforts might this promise address this week?

The northern kingdom cycles through multiple dynasties via assassination within a few chapters. Discuss what this instability suggests about a kingdom founded on political convenience rather than covenant faithfulness.

theme: Elijah at Carmel, Standing Firm

questions.text: Elijah tells the widow of Zarephath that her jar of flour and jug of oil will not run out, and they don't, 'according to the word of Adonai spoken through Elijah' (17:14-16). What does this miracle, performed for a non-Israelite widow, reveal about the scope of Adonai's care?

Elijah challenges Israel to stop 'pasach' -- limping, wavering -- between two opinions, choosing either Baal or Adonai (18:21). What does this vivid verb, elsewhere describing a physical limp, suggest about half-hearted religious loyalty?

Elijah repairs the broken-down altar of Adonai with twelve stones representing the twelve tribes before calling down fire (18:30-32). How does this restoring of unified worship for a divided nation anticipate the ultimate reconciliation of God's people that Messiah brings?

1 Corinthians 16 closes with the charge to 'be on the alert, stand firm in the faith, be courageous, be strong.' What area of wavering conviction does Elijah's Carmel showdown challenge you to resolve decisively this week?

Elijah settles publicly whether Adonai or Baal is God, fire falls, yet he still flees in fear from Jezebel immediately afterward. Discuss why even dramatic spiritual victories don't automatically resolve fear or doubt.

theme: A Still Small Voice, the God of All Comfort

questions.text: Adonai is not in the wind, earthquake, or fire at Horeb, but in a 'still small voice' (19:11-12). Why might this manner of appearing matter for how Elijah, and we, expect to hear from God?

The Hebrew phrase describing Adonai's approach to Elijah, 'qol demamah daqqah' -- a voice, or sound, of thin silence -- resists easy translation. How does its very ambiguity teach something about encountering God beyond the spectacular?

2 Corinthians 1 calls God 'the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our affliction.' How does this connect to Adonai's gentle provision for Elijah's exhaustion and despair -- food, rest, the quiet voice, renewed commission -- rather than rebuke?

Elijah, despite his Carmel victory, collapses into suicidal despair and isolation under Jezebel's threat (19:4). What does God's response of physical care before spiritual correction suggest about caring for someone, or yourself, in burnout?

Psalm 150, the Psalter's final psalm, calls 'everything that has breath' to praise Adonai with a cascade of instruments. Discuss what it means that the book's answer to all its laments ends in unrestrained praise rather than resolved argument.

theme: Naboth's Vineyard, Micaiah's Truth, Two Paths

questions.text: Naboth refuses to sell his ancestral vineyard to Ahab, citing 'the inheritance of my fathers' (21:3), and is subsequently framed and stoned through Jezebel's scheme. What does his refusal reveal about Torah's protection of ancestral land?

Micaiah alone, among four hundred court prophets, delivers Adonai's true word, describing a 'ruach sheker' -- a lying spirit -- sent to entice Ahab's false prophets (22:19-23). What does this vivid vision suggest about false consensus forming even among those who claim to speak for God?

Psalm 1 contrasts the blessed man who delights in Torah, 'like a tree planted by streams of water,' with the wicked, who are 'like chaff.' How does Micaiah's lone faithfulness against overwhelming false consensus embody this righteous man, and anticipate Yeshua's own lone faithfulness against religious consensus?

2 Corinthians 2 calls believers to forgive and comfort a repentant offender rather than let excessive sorrow overwhelm him. Where might unforgiveness or Ahab-like resentment over the vineyard need to give way to actual reconciliation this week?

Ahab imprisons Micaiah for telling the truth and dies exactly as prophesied, disguised in battle (22:26-28,34-38). Discuss what this teaches about the cost faithful truth-tellers often pay, and how communities should treat unwelcome prophetic voices.

theme: Elijah's Ascension and Unveiled Glory

questions.text: Elisha refuses to leave Elijah's side through Gilgal, Bethel, Jericho, and the Jordan, insisting three times, 'as Adonai lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you' (2:2-6). What does his persistence secure for him at the Jordan and beyond?

Elisha asks for a 'pi shnayim' -- a double portion, the customary firstborn inheritance share -- of Elijah's spirit (2:9). Why is this a bold yet fitting request from a successor rather than an equal?

2 Corinthians 3 describes believers beholding the Lord's glory 'with unveiled face' and being transformed, contrasting Moses' veiled, fading glory. How does Elijah's fiery ascension and Elisha's unveiled vision of it (2:11-12) parallel this movement from partial to fuller glimpses of God's glory?

Psalm 2 warns rulers to 'serve Adonai with reverence' while also promising Him as the ultimate inheritance of the nations. What area of your life needs the reverent trembling this psalm commends rather than casual presumption?

2 Corinthians 3 says a veil remains over hearts when the Torah is read apart from Messiah, 'but it is removed in Messiah.' Discuss how to hold this text carefully and respectfully, honoring Torah's ongoing validity and Jewish covenant life while still testifying to what Sha'ul describes as clarified sight in Messiah.

theme: Elisha's Miracles: Water, Oil, New Life

questions.text: In 2 Kings 3 the allied kings dig trenches in a dry wadi with no wind or rain in sight, and by morning they are full of water; what does their willingness to prepare before seeing anything reveal about faith?

In 2 Kings 4:26 Gehazi is sent to ask the Shunammite woman "hashalom lakh" ("is it well with you?") three times right after her son has died — what does this show about how "shalom" carries more weight than surface calm?

2 Corinthians 4:7 says we carry treasure in "jars of clay" — how does that image echo the widow's ordinary jars overflowing with oil, both showing God's power displayed through humble, unremarkable containers?
What "empty jars" (limited time, resources, or ability) do you need to bring to God this week, trusting Him to fill them rather than waiting until you feel adequately supplied?

The Shunammite woman answers "shalom" ("all is well") to Gehazi even though her son had just died in her arms — is her response evasion, composure, or a form of faith, and how should we read grief that doesn't immediately unravel?

theme: Naaman's Healing and New Creation

questions.text: Naaman storms off in rage when told simply to wash seven times in the Jordan, and it takes his own servants' persuasion to bring him back — what does his initial reaction reveal about how we expect God to work?

The condition afflicting Naaman is called "tzara'at," often translated "leprosy" but covering a range of skin conditions requiring ritual purification — how does knowing this reshape the story's focus on restoration and access to community?
2 Corinthians 5:17 declares that anyone in Messiah is "a new creation" — how does Naaman's flesh becoming like a young boy's after simple obedience foreshadow the total renewal Sha'ul describes?
Where might pride be tempting you to reject a simple, unimpressive instruction from God in favor of a grander gesture you'd feel better about performing?

Naaman asks permission to bow in the temple of Rimmon when supporting his master, and Elisha simply says "go in shalom" — what does this exchange suggest about navigating faith within a compromised workplace or culture?

theme: Four Lepers Discover Good News to Share

questions.text: The four men with tzara'at outside Samaria's gate reason "why sit here until we die?" and stumble into the abandoned Aramean camp — notice how they first eat and hide plunder before deciding, "we are not doing right; this is a day of good news."

Proverbs 31:10 opens with "eshet chayil," often rendered "woman of noble character" — how does exploring "chayil" (strength, valor, capability) expand the picture beyond quiet virtue to active strength?

2 Corinthians 6:14 warns against being unequally yoked, while 2 Kings 8 shows the Shunammite woman's testimony to the king restoring her land — how do both passages call God's people to align themselves rightly rather than compromise for convenience?
The lepers' initial instinct is to keep the good news to themselves — what "good news" have you stumbled onto recently that you've been slow to share?

What obligates the four lepers — outcasts with nothing to gain socially — to go back and report the news at personal risk, and what does this suggest about who often carries urgent truth to a community?

theme: Jehu's Zeal and the Test of Godly Sorrow

questions.text: A watchman recognizes Jehu from a distance simply by his reckless chariot driving "like a madman" — what does this small detail communicate about Jehu's character before he even arrives?

Jehu invites Jehonadab to "come see my kin'ah (zeal) for Adonai" — how does this word for zeal, used elsewhere for jealous covenant devotion, both justify and complicate his violent purge of Baal worship?

2 Corinthians 7:10 distinguishes godly sorrow that produces repentance from worldly sorrow — how does Jehu's outward zeal against Baal, without inward transformation, illustrate the difference Sha'ul describes?
Where in your own life is zeal for what's right outpacing genuine, inward change of heart?

God commends Jehu's purge of Ahab's house and Baal worship, yet Hosea 1:4 later condemns "the bloodshed of Jezreel" against Jehu's own house — how do we hold together an act being divinely sanctioned and later morally indicted?

theme: Joash Hidden, the Temple Restored, Grace Given

questions.text: Jehosheba hides the infant Joash in the Temple for six years while Athaliah reigns, and later the priests set up a chest with a hole bored in its lid to collect funds for Temple repairs — what do these two details reveal about patient, unglamorous faithfulness?
The name Joash (Yehoash) means something like "Adonai has given" or "Adonai is fire" — how does this name fit a boy whose survival itself was a near-impossible gift?

2 Corinthians 8:1-5 describes Macedonian believers giving beyond their ability out of joy — how does this compare to the people's willing contributions toward repairing "the House of Adonai" under Joash?
What would it look like to give toward something you care about not from surplus, but the way the Macedonians and Joash's generation gave — sacrificially and joyfully?

Athaliah nearly wipes out the entire Davidic royal line, and only one infant survives in hiding — what does this near-annihilation and providential rescue suggest about how fragile-looking, yet ultimately unbreakable, God's promises to David proved to be?

theme: Elisha's Final Arrows and Cheerful Giving

questions.text: Dying Elisha tells King Jehoash to strike arrows on the ground; Jehoash strikes only three times and Elisha grows angry, prophesying only limited victories over Aram as a result — what does the king's half-hearted response cost him?
Elisha calls the arrow shot eastward the "arrow of victory" (teshu'ah) over Aram — how does hearing the root shared with "yeshu'ah" (salvation) deepen the weight of this small prophetic act?

2 Corinthians 9:6-7 says God loves a cheerful giver who "sows bountifully" — how does Jehoash's stopping short at three strikes illustrate the opposite of sowing bountifully, holding back when more was available?
Where are you settling for a partial effort — three strikes instead of five or six — when God has invited fuller trust or generosity?

Why does such a small, seemingly arbitrary act (the number of arrow strikes) carry lasting national consequences, and what does that suggest about how our half-measures in prayer or obedience ripple further than we expect?

theme: Ahaz's Compromise and Weapons Not of Flesh

questions.text: King Ahaz shuts the doors of the Temple, sacrifices his own son "in the fire," and has a new altar built to a Damascus design, replacing the bronze altar Solomon made — what pattern of escalating compromise do these choices trace?

Ahaz's new altar (mizbe'ach) is copied directly from one he saw in Damascus while paying homage to Assyria's king — what does borrowing worship's very furniture from a pagan power say about where his true allegiance lay?

2 Corinthians 10:4 insists "the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh" — how does Ahaz's appeal to Assyria for military rescue, rather than trust in Adonai, model exactly the fleshly strategy Sha'ul warns against?
Under pressure this week, where are you tempted to reach for a worldly alliance or strategy instead of a spiritual one?

What does it mean that a king could "pass his son through the fire" and still be described as ruling in Jerusalem, the city of the Temple — how does the text want us to feel the shock of that contradiction, and how does compromise so often creep in through small, incremental steps?

theme: Samaria Falls; Hezekiah Trusts Adonai Alone

questions.text: 2 Kings 17 explains Israel's exile as the result of generations "walking in the customs of the nations," while 2 Kings 18 shows Hezekiah rebelling against Assyria and trusting Adonai despite the Rabshakeh's public taunts at Jerusalem's wall — what contrast is the narrator drawing between these two kingdoms' endings?

2 Kings 18:5 says Hezekiah "batach" (trusted) in Adonai so that none among the kings of Judah before or after was like him — what does this repeated word for trust suggest was uniquely decisive about his reign?

2 Corinthians 11 finds Sha'ul boasting in weakness and suffering for the sake of the gospel rather than capitulating to false apostles — how does this parallel Hezekiah standing firm under mockery and overwhelming odds rather than surrendering to intimidation?
Where is mockery or pressure tempting you to compromise a conviction you know is right?

The northern kingdom's fall is attributed to accumulated, generational idolatry rather than any single event — what does this teach about how corporate and long-term patterns of unfaithfulness, not just isolated acts, bring consequences on a community?

theme: Hezekiah's Prayer, Healing, and Sufficient Grace

questions.text: Hezekiah literally spreads Sennacherib's threatening letter out before Adonai in the Temple before praying, and that night the angel of Adonai strikes down 185,000 in the Assyrian camp — what does the act of physically laying out the problem before God suggest about prayer?

The sign given to Hezekiah involves the shadow moving backward on the "ma'alot Achaz," the stairway or dial of Ahaz — what does this concrete, visible sign add to the promise of fifteen more years of life?

2 Corinthians 12:9's "My grace is sufficient for you, for My power is perfected in weakness" mirrors Hezekiah's total helplessness before an empire and before death, met only by God's sovereign intervention rather than human strength — how do both passages relocate strength in weakness?
What overwhelming letter, diagnosis, or threat do you need to "spread out before Adonai" honestly this week rather than carrying alone?

Hezekiah's prayer for more life results in the birth of Manasseh, who becomes Judah's most wicked king — what does this ironic outcome teach about the complexity of answered prayer and the limits of our ability to foresee consequences?

theme: Manasseh's Evil and Josiah's Rediscovered Torah

questions.text: Manasseh rebuilds the high places his father Hezekiah destroyed and sets a carved image in the Temple itself, while decades later young King Josiah orders repairs to that same Temple and Hilkiah discovers the scroll of the Torah — what does the physical space of the Temple track across these two reigns?

The "Sefer HaTorah" (scroll of the Torah) found during renovation prompts Josiah to tear his clothes in grief — what does his physical reaction reveal about how unfamiliar God's own words had become to the royal court?

2 Corinthians 13:5 urges believers to "examine yourselves, whether you are in the faith" — how does Josiah's grief and immediate reform upon hearing the Torah read aloud model exactly this kind of honest self-examination in light of God's word?
What would it look like to let a fresh, unguarded encounter with Scripture this week expose an area of complacency rather than confirming what you already assumed?

Why is the prophetess Huldah, rather than a well-known male prophet, the one consulted to authenticate the newly found scroll, and what does her role suggest about women's place in Israel's prophetic tradition?

theme: Josiah's Reform, Jerusalem's Fall, Gospel from Heaven

questions.text: Josiah celebrates a Passover unlike any kept since the days of the judges (23:22), yet the narrative moves swiftly to his death at Megiddo and then Jerusalem's destruction and Temple burning under Babylon — what emotional whiplash does this compressed sequence create for the reader?
The final chapters describe the deportation to Babylon using the term "golah" (exile) — how does this recurring word frame Israel's story going forward as a people displaced from the land?

Galatians 1:11-12 has Sha'ul insist his gospel came directly by revelation of Yeshua, not from human tradition — how does this echo Josiah's reform being rooted in rediscovering God's own written word rather than inherited custom?
Where do you need to anchor a conviction in God's revealed word rather than familiar habit or tradition, even if it proves costly?

2 Kings 23:26 states that even Josiah's sweeping reforms couldn't turn away the judgment already decreed because of Manasseh — what does this teach about the limits of individual righteousness to reverse corporate consequences, and how does that sit alongside the hope of grace?

theme: Zion's Indictment and Swords into Plowshares

questions.text: Isaiah 1 opens with Adonai's lawsuit against Judah, describing the nation as a body "sick from head to foot," while Isaiah 2 pictures nations streaming to Zion to learn Torah and beating their swords into plowshares — what emotional arc moves the reader from indictment to hope?

Isaiah 1:21,27 pairs "mishpat" (justice) with "tzedakah" (righteousness) as what Jerusalem has lost and must recover — how do these twin terms together define covenant faithfulness beyond mere ritual correctness?

Galatians 2:16 insists a person is justified through faith in Messiah Yeshua, not merely by works of the law — how does this connect to Isaiah 1:11-17's charge that sacrifice without justice and mercy is detestable to Adonai?
Where might your religious activity be outpacing your actual practice of justice and mercy in daily relationships?

Isaiah 2:2-4 envisions nations streaming to Zion specifically to learn Torah — how should this future hope shape how Messianic Jews and Gentile believers together anticipate world peace, rather than treating it as a purely spiritual or purely political image?

theme: Jerusalem Judged and the Branch of Adonai

questions.text: Isaiah 3 details the haughty daughters of Zion stripped of their finery and the removal of Judah's leaders, while chapter 4 closes with hope — a cloud by day and fire by night over Zion, echoing the wilderness pillar — what does this juxtaposition of judgment and covenant memory accomplish?

Isaiah 4:2 promises "Tzemach Adonai," the Branch of the LORD, will be beautiful and glorious for Israel's survivors — how does this messianic title, later developed by Jeremiah and Zechariah, function here as a seed of hope amid judgment?

Galatians 3:13-14 says Messiah redeemed us from the law's curse so the blessing of Abraham might come to the nations — how does this anticipate the same restoration Isaiah's "Branch" figure promises for the remnant?
What would true adornment before God look like for you this week, in contrast to the external vanity condemned in Isaiah 3?

Galatians 3 argues the promise to Abraham preceded the Torah by 430 years, with the Torah functioning as a "guardian" until Messiah came — does this diminish Torah's ongoing significance, or clarify its purpose, and how should Messianic believers hold both truths together?

theme: Isaiah's Vision and Adoption as God's Sons

questions.text: Isaiah 5 sings of a carefully tended vineyard that yields only wild grapes, while chapter 6 records Isaiah's vision of Adonai's throne, seraphim calling to one another, and a live coal touching his lips — what shift happens between the nation's failure and the prophet's own cleansing?
The seraphim cry "Kadosh, kadosh, kadosh" (Holy, holy, holy) in 6:3 — what does this rare triple repetition in Hebrew, used as a superlative, communicate about the nature of Adonai's holiness?

Galatians 4:4-6 describes God sending His Son so that we might receive adoption as sons — how does this pair with Isaiah 6, where atoning grace (the coal purifying Isaiah's lips) comes before the call to service, rather than after it?
Where in your life is God's careful cultivation meeting disappointing "wild grapes," and what would it look like to invite Him to address the root rather than just the fruit?

Isaiah responds "Here am I, send me" immediately after hearing that his message will only harden the people's hearts (6:9-10) — what does costly obedience in the face of foreseen difficulty look like, and how does Galatians 4's Hagar-Sarah allegory address freedom versus slavery in faith?

theme: Immanuel Promised, Freedom in the Spirit

questions.text: Ahaz refuses to ask for a sign under a show of false humility, so Adonai gives one anyway, while in chapter 8 Isaiah names his own son Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz as a sign against Aram and Israel — what does the contrast between a reluctant king and an obedient prophet reveal?
The promised child in 7:14 is named "Immanu El," God with us — what weight does this compound name carry as a sign to a fearful king refusing to trust Adonai's presence?

Galatians 5:1 declares that Messiah has set us free, followed by the fruit of the Spirit — how does Immanuel's promise of God's abiding presence anticipate the freedom Yeshua brings, in contrast to Ahaz's fear-driven reliance on an Assyrian alliance?
Where is fear tempting you toward a worldly solution instead of trusting God's promised presence?

Isaiah 7:14 uses the word "almah" (young woman), which some later connect to "virgin" through the Septuagint's rendering — how should the immediate 8th-century sign to Ahaz and its later application to Yeshua's birth be held together responsibly?

theme: A Son Is Given; Burdens Are Shared

questions.text: Isaiah 9:6-7 lists throne names for a coming child, while chapter 10 calls Assyria "the rod of My anger" that God will later judge for its own arrogance — what tension does the text hold between using a nation as an instrument and still holding it accountable?
Among the throne names in 9:6 is "Sar Shalom," Prince of Peace — how does "sar," meaning ruler or commander, shape the kind of peace this coming king brings?

These throne names form one of the clearest Tanakh anticipations of Yeshua's identity, and Galatians 6:2's call to "bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Messiah" extends the government resting on His shoulders (9:6) into how His followers now carry each other.
What is one specific burden you could actively help carry for someone this week, in keeping with Galatians 6:2?

Isaiah 10 portrays Assyria as both God's chosen instrument of judgment and morally culpable for its own pride — how do we hold together divine sovereignty and human or national accountability without letting either cancel out the other?

theme: The Shoot of Jesse, Chosen Before Creation

questions.text: Isaiah 11 pictures a shoot growing from the stump of Jesse on whom the Spirit of Adonai rests, alongside the wolf dwelling with the lamb, while chapter 12 is a short song declaring "God is my salvation" — how does the mood shift from prophetic vision to personal praise?
Isaiah 12:2 declares, "Behold, God is my yeshu'ati (salvation)" — how does hearing the root shared with the name Yeshua deepen this verse's resonance?

Ephesians 1:4 says God "chose us in Him before the foundation of the world" — how does this pair with the image of a shoot emerging from Jesse's ancient stump, a Davidic king planned long before his visible appearance?
What would it look like to live this week from the thankful confidence of 12:2 — "I will trust and not be afraid" — rather than from anxiety?

Isaiah 11's picture of the wolf dwelling with the lamb and the leopard lying down with the goat — is this meant literally, as a future restored creation, or figuratively, as transformed human relationships, and how does that choice shape Messianic hope for the age to come?

theme: Babylon's Fall, Made Alive with Messiah

questions.text: Isaiah 13 compares Babylon's coming overthrow to Sodom and Gomorrah's, and chapter 14 includes a taunt-song against the king of Babylon, describing a figure called "Helel ben Shachar" (day star, son of the dawn) cast down from heaven — what emotions does this taunt-song evoke about pride brought low?

"Helel ben Shachar" in 14:12 is aimed specifically at a boastful Babylonian king — how does understanding its immediate historical target inform (without necessarily excluding) later applications of the image to a cosmic figure of pride?

Ephesians 2:1-5 describes being "dead in trespasses" yet "made us alive together with Messiah" — how does this total reversal of fortune contrast with the total downfall pictured for Babylon's proud king brought low to Sheol?
What prideful "ascent" or self-exaltation in your own life needs to be surrendered in light of the grace Ephesians 2 describes?

Many later readers apply Isaiah 14:12-15 to a fallen angelic being rather than only the king of Babylon — what is the textual basis for that reading, and how should we handle a passage that may carry more than one legitimate layer of meaning?

theme: Moab Mourns, Gentiles Welcomed as Heirs

questions.text: Isaiah 15-16 mourns over Moab's cities — Dibon, Nebo, Kir-Hareseth — with imagery of fleeing refugees, yet also includes a plea for Moab's outcasts to find refuge in the shadow of Zion (16:3-4) — what unexpected note does this plea strike within a judgment oracle?
This prophecy is headed "massa" (burden/oracle) — how does this word's double sense, a spoken pronouncement and a heavy weight, fit the emotional tone of this lament over a historic rival nation?

Ephesians 3:6 declares the Gentiles "fellow heirs" through the Good News — how does this resonate with 16:5's surprising hope of a throne established "in mercy" offering refuge even to Moab, historically one of Israel's adversaries?
Where could you extend compassion toward a historic "outsider" or adversary this week, mirroring the plea for Moab's refugees to find shelter?
Isaiah pronounces severe judgment on Moab while simultaneously offering refuge (16:3-5) — what does this teach about how judgment oracles in Scripture can still carry seeds of mercy within them?

theme: Forsaken Cry Becomes One New Body

questions.text: Isaiah 17:10 pairs Damascus's downfall with Israel/Ephraim's related decline for "forgetting the God of your salvation," while Psalm 22 moves from anguished abandonment to a declaration of praise among the nations — what movement from despair to hope runs through both readings?

Psalm 22 opens, "Eli, Eli, lamah azavtani" (My God, my God, why have you forsaken me) — words Yeshua later cries from the cross — how does the psalm's turn toward praise by its end reshape how we hear this opening cry?

Psalm 22's precise imagery — pierced hands and feet, divided garments, cast lots — is fulfilled at Yeshua's crucifixion, and Ephesians 4:4-6's call to unity as "one body, one Spirit... one Lord" flows directly from the reconciling suffering this psalm anticipates.
How can honest lament, as modeled in Psalm 22's opening verses, coexist with ultimate trust in your own prayer life this week, rather than suppressing pain until it passes?

Why does Psalm 22 shift so dramatically from abandonment to praise and a prophecy of worldwide worship (22:27-28), and what does this arc teach about processing suffering within community rather than in isolation?

theme: Adonai My Shepherd Through Every Valley

questions.text: In Isaiah 20, Isaiah walks barefoot and stripped for three years as a living sign against trusting Egypt and Cush, while Psalm 23 pictures Adonai as a shepherd leading beside still waters and through the valley of the shadow of death — what does each passage cost its central figure?
Psalm 23:4 uses "tzalmavet" (shadow of death) — how does this compound Hebrew word shape the imagery of walking through, rather than around, deep darkness?

Yeshua later calls Himself the good shepherd, fulfilling the shepherd-King hope embedded in Psalm 23, while Ephesians 5:8 says "once you were darkness, but now you are light in the Master" — how do both echo safe walking through dark valleys under the Shepherd's care?
In what current "valley" season do you need to specifically trust that the Shepherd is with you, per Psalm 23:4?

Isaiah walks naked and barefoot for three years as a costly, humiliating prophetic sign-act — what does this reveal about the personal price some prophets bore to embody God's message, and are there echoes of this kind of self-emptying in Messiah?

theme: Valley of Vision and the Armor of God

questions.text: Isaiah 22 rebukes Jerusalem, called "the valley of vision," for reveling ("let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die," 22:13) instead of repenting during siege, and contrasts unfaithful steward Shebna with faithful Eliakim, given "the key of the House of David" (22:22) — what does this contrast teach about leadership under pressure?
Eliakim receives "the key of the house of David" (22:22) — how does tracing this image forward to Revelation 3:7, where it is applied to Yeshua, deepen its significance here?

Ephesians 6:11-17's armor of God pictures spiritual readiness — how does this contrast with Jerusalem's careless revelry under threat in Isaiah 22, both calling for vigilant preparedness rather than complacency?
Is ease or distraction ("eat and drink") replacing vigilance in a specific area of your spiritual life right now?

What does the replacement of unfaithful Shebna with faithful Eliakim, entrusted with "the key of David," teach about stewarding God-given authority well, and how does this connect to Yeshua holding that same key in Revelation?

theme: Earth's Reckoning; To Live Is Messiah

questions.text: Isaiah 23 laments Tyre's commercial collapse ("wail, ships of Tarshish"), while chapter 24 widens to cosmic scope, declaring "the earth reels like a drunkard" under a broken covenant — what shift in scale happens between these two chapters?

Isaiah 24:5 speaks of the "brit olam" (everlasting covenant) being broken by the earth's inhabitants — what covenant is likely in view here, and how does its universal scope extend beyond Israel alone?

Philippians 1:21's "for me, to live is Messiah and to die is gain" reframes Sha'ul's imprisonment much the way Isaiah 24 reframes cosmic collapse — both find hope beyond present devastation in a larger divine purpose.
How can you reframe a current personal loss or upheaval with the eternal perspective Sha'ul models while writing from prison?

Isaiah 24 describes a broken "everlasting covenant" affecting the whole earth, not just Israel — how does this universal scope shape our understanding of judgment and hope extending beyond Israel's own borders?

theme: Death Swallowed Up; Messiah's Humble Descent

questions.text: Isaiah 25:8 promises Adonai will "swallow up death forever" and wipe away every tear, while 26:3 promises "perfect peace" for the mind that stays fixed on Him — what relationship do these two promises, one cosmic and one personal, have to each other?
Isaiah 26:3 doubles the word for emphasis: "shalom shalom" (perfect peace) — how does this Hebrew intensification technique shape the depth of peace being promised?

Philippians 2:6-11's hymn of Messiah emptying Himself, becoming obedient to death, then being exalted so every knee bows, is the means by which Isaiah 25:8's promise becomes reality — a verse 1 Corinthians 15:54 later quotes directly regarding resurrection.
What would it look like to keep your mind "stayed" on God this week as the pathway to the shalom shalom promised in 26:3?

How does Philippians 2's call to have "the same mind" as Messiah's self-emptying humility challenge cultural instincts toward self-promotion, and what would costly humility look like practically this week?

theme: The Tested Cornerstone and Counting All Loss

questions.text: Isaiah 27:1 depicts Adonai slaying Leviathan, "the fleeing serpent," while Isaiah 28 rebukes the "drunkards of Ephraim" yet promises a "tested stone, a precious cornerstone" laid in Zion (28:16) — what does pairing cosmic imagery with local political corruption in back-to-back chapters accomplish?

Isaiah 28:16 describes an "even bochan, pinat yikrat" (a tested stone, a precious cornerstone) — how does tracing this phrase's later quotation in Romans 9:33 and 1 Peter 2:6 illuminate its significance?

Philippians 3:7-8 has Sha'ul count his impressive credentials as loss compared to knowing Messiah — how does this parallel Isaiah 28:16's cornerstone offering stability that the "refuge of lies" (28:15,17) can never match?
What personal credential or security might need to be recounted as loss for the sake of deeper trust in Messiah, as Sha'ul models?

Why does Isaiah picture Adonai defeating cosmic chaos (Leviathan) alongside very local, civic corruption (drunken leaders of Ephraim) within the same short span of chapters, and what does that pairing suggest about the scope of God's justice?

theme: Ariel Besieged; Peace That Guards the Heart

questions.text: Isaiah 29 calls Jerusalem "Ariel" under siege, and Isaiah 30:15 states Judah's strength would be "in quietness and trust," but the people refuse, preferring to flee to Egypt on horses — what does this refusal reveal about how people respond to fear?
"Ariel" (29:1-2), a poetic name for Jerusalem meaning "lion of God" or "hearth of God," is used ironically as the city under siege — what layered wordplay is Isaiah drawing on here?

Philippians 4:6-7's "peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts" is precisely the "quietness and trust" Judah refused in 30:15 — Sha'ul, writing from prison, embodies the calm dependence Isaiah's audience rejected in panic.
Where is the instinct to "run to Egypt" — a self-reliant fix — tempting you instead of resting in quiet trust with a current anxiety?

Isaiah 30:15's straightforward offer, "in returning and rest you shall be saved," is met with outright refusal — what makes quiet trust so difficult in the face of real danger, and how do we cultivate it corporately, not just individually?

theme: Egypt's Flesh versus the Poured-Out Spirit

questions.text: Isaiah 31:3 warns that Egypt's horses are "flesh, not spirit," while chapter 32 anticipates a righteous king reigning and the Spirit poured out "from on high" turning wilderness into fruitful field — how does this movement from misplaced trust to true hope unfold?
Isaiah 31:3 contrasts "basar" (flesh) with "ruach" (spirit) to describe Egypt's horsemen versus Adonai's help — what does this word pair reveal about the nature of true versus false security?

Colossians 1:15 calls Messiah "the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation" — how does this describe the very righteous king Isaiah 32:1 anticipates, with the Spirit poured out (32:15) matching the transformation Messiah's supremacy brings?
Where is reliance on visible, human strength crowding out dependence on the Spirit in your life right now?

Isaiah 32:9-20 addresses "complacent women" enjoying ease before judgment — what does this specific rhetorical choice reveal about the passage's original audience and its message about false security?

theme: The King's Beauty and Fullness in Messiah

questions.text: Isaiah 33 cries out against "the destroyer" and then promises Zion's eyes will see "the King in His beauty," while chapter 34 pronounces total judgment on Edom, likened to a great sacrifice at Bozrah — what contrast between beauty and devastation runs through these two chapters?
Isaiah 34:8 calls Edom's judgment "yom nakam," a day of vengeance — how does this specific term shape the intensity of the oracle against a historic rival nation?

Colossians 2:9-10 says "in Him all the fullness of Deity dwells bodily, and you have been made complete in Him" — how does this correspond to Isaiah 33:17's vision of beholding "the King in His beauty," both pointing to a fullness found only in the Messiah-King?
Where might you be seeking completeness in "philosophy and empty deceit" (Colossians 2:8) rather than in Messiah?

Isaiah 34 pictures Edom's judgment in graphic terms — soil turned to burning pitch, a sword "bathed in heaven" — how should we understand such intense oracles against specific historic nations within the broader canon of Scripture?

theme: Highway of Holiness, Minds Set Above

questions.text: Isaiah 35 promises the desert will "blossom like the crocus" along a "Highway of Holiness" for the redeemed, but chapter 36 immediately narrates the Rabshakeh mocking Hezekiah at Jerusalem's wall — what does this jarring transition from serene hope to present crisis accomplish?
Isaiah 35:8 names the "Derech HaKodesh," the Highway of Holiness, reserved for the redeemed alone — what does this image of a purified processional road suggest about who travels it and how?

Isaiah 35:5-6's healing of blind eyes, deaf ears, and lame limbs is directly echoed in Yeshua's ministry and His answer to Yochanan's disciples (Matthew 11), while Colossians 3:1-2's "set your mind on things above" invites walking now, by faith, on that highway of holiness.
What is one concrete way to "set your mind on things above" this week amid circumstances resembling Hezekiah's siege-level pressure?

What does the abrupt shift from Isaiah 35's serene future hope to chapter 36's immediate historical crisis teach about holding future promise and present danger together in faith, rather than letting one erase the other?

theme: Hezekiah's Deliverance and Gracious Speech

questions.text: Isaiah 37 records Hezekiah spreading Sennacherib's letter before Adonai and the angel striking down 185,000 Assyrians overnight, while Isaiah 38 records Hezekiah's own psalm-like writing after recovering from illness, reflecting on being brought near the gates of Sheol — what shared posture of dependence links these two deliverances?

Isaiah 38:9 calls Hezekiah's reflection a "michtav," a personal writing or psalm of thanksgiving composed after his illness — what does choosing to write and preserve this reflection suggest about turning a private crisis into public testimony?

Colossians 4:6's call to let speech "always be with grace, seasoned with salt" reflects the same wisdom shaping Hezekiah's grateful "michtav" after deliverance from death — both model turning crisis into testimony that instructs others.
Following Hezekiah's example in chapter 38, consider writing or voicing your own reflection of thanksgiving after a recent deliverance, however small.

Hezekiah's earnest prayer results in fifteen additional years of life being granted, seemingly altering what had already been declared — what does this teach about the relationship between fixed divine purposes and the real efficacy of prayer?

theme: Comfort, Strength, and Waiting on Adonai

questions.text: In Isaiah 39, notice how Hezekiah responds to Isaiah's warning that his treasures and descendants will be carried off to Babylon: what does his answer, 'there will be peace and truth in my days,' reveal about his priorities?

The Hebrew word qavah, 'to wait' or 'hope,' appears in Isaiah 40:31 ('those who wait for Adonai shall renew their strength'); what does qavah's sense of tense, expectant waiting add to the promise of mounting up like eagles?

Isaiah 40:3's 'voice crying in the wilderness, prepare the way of Adonai' is applied to Yochanan the Immerser preparing for Yeshua; how does 1 Thessalonians 1:10's picture of 'waiting for His Son from heaven' pick up that same posture of expectant waiting?
Isaiah 41:10 says 'fear not, for I am with you' to a people about to face exile; what fear in your own life this week needs to hear that same word?

Hezekiah's private compromise in chapter 39 doesn't cause immediate consequences but sets up disaster generations later; how should a chavurah wrestle with decisions that feel low-stakes today but could echo far beyond our own lifetime?

theme: The Servant's Gentleness, Redeemed and Called by Name

questions.text: In Isaiah 42:1-4, the Servant of Adonai is described handling a 'bruised reed' and 'smoldering wick' with restraint rather than force; what specific images convey his gentleness toward the weak?

In Isaiah 43:1, Adonai says 'I have redeemed you' using the Hebrew ga'al, the word for a kinsman-redeemer's duty; how does knowing ga'al implies a family obligation change how you hear 'you are Mine'?

Isaiah 42's Servant who will not break a bruised reed is echoed in Sha'ul's description of himself in 1 Thessalonians 2 as 'gentle among you, like a nursing mother'; what connects the Servant's character to how Sha'ul chose to minister?
Psalm 34:8 invites you to 'taste and see that Adonai is good'; what is one concrete way you can test that invitation this week rather than just agreeing with it intellectually?
Isaiah 43:22-24 says Israel grew 'weary' of Adonai even while going through religious motions; how can a community tell the difference between faithful practice and weary, hollow routine?

theme: Cyrus the Anointed and Every Knee Bowing

questions.text: Isaiah 44:9-20 mocks a man who takes half a tree to warm himself and cook bread, then bows to the other half as a god; what specific details make this satire so pointed?

Isaiah 45:1 calls the pagan king Cyrus Adonai's 'mashiach,' anointed one; what does it mean that this Hebrew title, usually reserved for Israel's kings and priests, is applied to a Gentile ruler here?

Agur's question in Proverbs 30:4, 'what is his name, and what is his son's name?' sits alongside Isaiah 45:23's 'to me every knee shall bow' (quoted of Yeshua in Philippians 2); how do these texts together press toward the mystery of God having a Son?
Agur prays in Proverbs 30:8-9 for neither poverty nor riches, 'lest I be full and deny you, or be poor and steal'; where in your own life is this same prayer for contentment needed right now?

Cyrus never worshiped Adonai yet is called His anointed and shepherd for accomplishing His purposes; what does this suggest about how God can use people or nations outside the covenant community to fulfill His plans for Israel?

theme: Carried by God, Not Carrying Idols

questions.text: Isaiah 46:1-2 pictures Bel and Nebo, Babylon's idols, loaded onto weary animals and carted away, while verses 3-4 describe Adonai carrying Israel from the womb to old age; what is the deliberate contrast being drawn?
Proverbs 31:10 opens the poem to the 'eshet chayil,' the woman of valor or strength; what does chayil, a word also used for warriors and armies, suggest about the kind of strength being praised?

Isaiah 46:4's promise 'I will carry and will save' anticipates the resurrection hope of 1 Thessalonians 4:16-17, where the Lord Himself descends to gather His people; how do both texts show God as the one who bears His people through to the end?
Isaiah 47 shows Babylon's confident boast 'I am, and there is no one besides me' leading to sudden downfall; what unexamined self-sufficiency might Adonai be inviting you to release this week?
1 Thessalonians 4:13 addresses grief over 'those who are asleep'; how should the hope of resurrection shape, rather than eliminate, how a community grieves together?

theme: A Light to the Nations, Children of Light

questions.text: In Isaiah 49:14-16, Zion complains that Adonai has forgotten her, and He answers by saying He has engraved her on the palms of His hands; what makes this image of engraving more permanent than the mother-and-child comparison just before it?

Isaiah 49:6 says the Servant will be a light 'la-goyim,' to the nations, a verse quoted in Acts 13:47 about Sha'ul and Barnabas's mission; what does goyim's scope suggest about the reach of the Servant's calling?

The Servant 'called from the womb' to be a light to the nations in Isaiah 49 connects to 1 Thessalonians 5:5's description of believers as 'children of light' awaiting the Day of the Lord; how do both texts frame identity around light rather than darkness?

Proverbs 1:7 declares 'the fear of Adonai is the beginning of knowledge,' while the chapter also warns against being enticed by companions promising easy gain; what 'easy gain' in your life this week needs the counter-weight of that fear?

1 Thessalonians 5:19-21 warns 'do not quench the Spirit' but also 'test everything, hold fast what is good'; how does a community hold both openness to the Ruach and careful discernment without falling into either extreme?

theme: The Servant's Suffering and the Coming Vindication

questions.text: Isaiah 50:6 describes the Servant giving His back to those who strike Him and not hiding His face 'from disgrace and spitting'; what specific physical details make this description so vivid and personal?

Isaiah 51:9 calls on the 'arm of Adonai' to awake as it did when it 'cut Rahab in pieces'; Rahab here is a poetic name for a chaos monster tied to Egypt, not the woman of Jericho; how does this mythic imagery portray Adonai's power over chaos?

The Servant of Isaiah 50, who sets His face like flint and trusts that 'he who vindicates me is near,' anticipates the vindication 2 Thessalonians 1:6-10 promises will come 'when the Lord Yeshua is revealed from heaven'; how do both texts hold present suffering and future vindication together?
Psalm 38 is a raw confession of guilt and physical distress, 'there is no soundness in my flesh'; what would it look like to bring an unconfessed weight to Adonai with that same honesty this week?

2 Thessalonians 1:6 speaks of God repaying affliction to those who afflict His people; how should believers hold together a longing for justice with the call to love and pray for those who persecute them?

theme: Pierced for Our Transgressions, the Suffering Servant

questions.text: Isaiah 52:7 celebrates 'how beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news'; what is significant about the text praising feet rather than a voice or a face?

Isaiah 53:5 says the Servant was pierced (Hebrew m'chholal) for our transgressions and crushed (m'dukka) for our iniquities; how do these two distinct Hebrew verbs each describe a different dimension of suffering?

Isaiah 53 as a whole, the Servant despised, bearing griefs, 'like a lamb led to slaughter,' making 'many to be accounted righteous,' is central to Messianic hope in Yeshua; how does the chapter's climax in 53:10-12, seeing offspring, prolonging days, dividing spoil, point beyond death to vindication?

Isaiah 53:6 says 'all we like sheep have gone astray, we have turned every one to his own way'; where has 'your own way' quietly taken priority over God's this week, and what would turning back look like?

Jewish and Christian readers have long debated whether the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 refers to Israel corporately, to an individual, or to Messiah specifically; what evidence from the passage itself, singular pronouns, offspring, prolonged days, speaks to each reading, and how might these readings interact rather than exclude each other?

theme: Come, Everyone Who Thirsts, an Open Ear

questions.text: Isaiah 55:1 invites 'everyone who thirsts' to come to the waters and buy wine and milk 'without money and without price'; what is paradoxical about being told to buy something free?

Isaiah 54:10 promises Adonai's 'chesed,' steadfast covenant love, will not depart, alongside a 'covenant of peace'; how does chesed's sense of loyal commitment differ from a merely emotional kind of love?

Psalm 40:6-8, 'you have given me an open ear... I delight to do your will, O my God,' is applied in Hebrews 10 to the Messiah's incarnation; how does Isaiah 55's invitation to 'come' and Psalm 40's picture of glad obedience together describe the shape of a life devoted to Adonai?

2 Thessalonians 3:10 says 'if anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat,' amid a warning against idleness; what practical, unglamorous work is Adonai calling you to steady faithfulness in this week?

Isaiah 55:8-9 says Adonai's thoughts are 'higher than your thoughts'; how should a community respond when Scripture's picture of God's ways genuinely unsettles our expectations rather than confirms them?

theme: A House of Prayer for All Peoples

questions.text: Isaiah 56:3-5 promises foreigners and eunuchs who 'hold fast' Adonai's covenant a place and a name 'better than sons and daughters' in His house; what specific groups does this include that would otherwise be considered excluded?

Isaiah 56:7 calls the Temple 'beit tefillah l'chol ha'amim,' a house of prayer for all peoples, a phrase Yeshua later quotes while overturning tables; what does 'kol ha'amim,' all peoples, reveal about the Temple's intended scope?

Isaiah 56's vision of outsiders gathered into Adonai's house connects with Sha'ul's description of himself in 1 Timothy 1:15-16 as 'the foremost of sinners' shown mercy as a pattern for others; how do both passages picture grace reaching those who seem least likely candidates?

Proverbs 5 warns at length about the seductive path of the forbidden woman whose 'feet go down to death'; what boundary or safeguard might Adonai be prompting you to strengthen this week, even in an area that feels harmless?

Isaiah 56:9-12 indicts Israel's own watchmen as blind and self-serving even while outsiders are welcomed in; how should a community hold leaders accountable without becoming cynical about leadership itself?

theme: The Redeemer Comes to Zion, One Mediator

questions.text: Isaiah 58:6-7 redefines the fast Adonai chooses as loosing the bonds of wickedness, sharing bread with the hungry, and clothing the naked; what specific everyday actions does this list rather than abstract religious terms?

Isaiah 59:20 promises a go'el, redeemer, will come 'to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from transgression,' a verse quoted in Romans 11 about Israel's future; what does go'el's family-redemption sense add to this promise?

1 Timothy 2:5's declaration of 'one mediator between God and men, the man Messiah Yeshua' resonates with Isaiah 59:16's observation that 'there was no one to intercede' until Adonai's 'own arm brought him salvation'; how do both texts highlight the need for and provision of a mediator?
Proverbs 6:6-11 sends the sluggard to observe the ant's diligence; what area of drift or procrastination in your own life needs the ant's example this week?

Isaiah 58 insists that fasting without justice, while still oppressing workers, is worthless to Adonai; how should a modern chavurah examine whether its own spiritual disciplines are paired with tangible care for the vulnerable?

theme: Anointed to Bring Good News to the Poor

questions.text: Isaiah 60:6 pictures camels from Midian and Ephah, and people from Sheba, bringing gold and frankincense; what later Gospel scene does this specific combination of gifts and travelers echo?

Isaiah 61:1 begins 'Ruach Adonai Elohim alai,' the Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me; what does it mean that the Servant's mission, good news, binding the brokenhearted, liberty to captives, flows directly from being anointed by the Ruach?

In Luke 4, Yeshua reads Isaiah 61:1-2 in the Nazareth synagogue and stops mid-verse before 'the day of vengeance of our God'; why might that specific stopping point matter for understanding His first-century mission versus what is still to come?
