1 Timothy 3 sets qualifications for overseers and deacons rooted heavily in character and household management rather than charisma or eloquence; what quiet, unglamorous character quality might Adonai be shaping in you right now for future responsibility?

Isaiah 61:3 calls the restored 'oaks of righteousness, the planting of Adonai, that he may be glorified'; how should a community balance celebrating personal transformation with keeping the glory pointed toward God rather than the individual?

theme: Wisdom, the Master Craftsman Before Creation

questions.text: Isaiah 62:4 promises Jerusalem a new name, Hephzibah, 'My Delight Is in Her,' replacing 'Forsaken'; what does the practice of renaming signal in the Tanakh about identity and destiny?

Proverbs 8:22-31 pictures Wisdom, Chokhmah, present 'beside him, like a master workman' at creation, 'daily his delight'; what does personifying wisdom this way, active before the mountains were formed, suggest about wisdom's relationship to God Himself?

Proverbs 8's Wisdom, present with God before creation and rejoicing in His works, has long been read by Messianic believers as foreshadowing the preexistent Word later described in Yochanan's Gospel and Colossians 1; what specific details in Proverbs 8 make that connection compelling?
Isaiah 63:10 says Israel 'rebelled and grieved his Ruach Kodesh'; what habit or attitude in your life this week might be quietly grieving God's Spirit rather than welcoming Him?

Isaiah 63:1-6 depicts Adonai as a divine warrior treading a winepress of judgment alone; how should believers hold this image of righteous judgment alongside the tenderness described just a few verses later in 63:9, 'in all their affliction he was afflicted'?

theme: Your Throne, O God, Forever and Ever

questions.text: Isaiah 64:8 pictures Israel pleading, 'we are the clay, and you are our potter, we are all the work of your hand'; what posture does this metaphor ask the reader to take toward God's shaping?

Psalm 45:6 declares to the king, 'your throne, O God, is forever and ever,' a verse later applied directly to the Son in Hebrews 1:8; what makes it striking that a royal wedding psalm addresses the king with the divine title Elohim?

Psalm 45's picture of a king 'fairer than the sons of men' whose throne is everlasting anticipates Hebrews 1's use of this psalm for Yeshua; how does reading Psalm 45 as a wedding song for an earthly king still leave room for this greater fulfillment?
Isaiah 65:1 has Adonai say 'I was ready to be sought by those who did not ask for me'; where in your life might God already be reaching toward you before you've thought to seek Him?

Isaiah 65:17 promises 'new heavens and a new earth' where former troubles 'shall not be remembered,' alongside 65:25's picture of wolf and lamb grazing together; how literally should a chavurah expect these images to be fulfilled, and how does that shape how we live now?

theme: Called Before Birth, Watched by an Almond Branch

questions.text: Jeremiah 1:11-12 shows Adonai asking Jeremiah what he sees, and Jeremiah answers 'a branch of an almond tree'; what is the specific wordplay in Hebrew between 'almond' and 'watching' that makes this vision meaningful?

Jeremiah 1:5 states 'before I formed you in the womb I knew you,' using the Hebrew yatsar, the same verb used of a potter shaping clay; what does that verb choice suggest about how intentionally Jeremiah's calling was formed?

Isaiah 66:13's picture of Adonai comforting His people 'as one whom his mother comforts' and Jeremiah's prophetic call both show God intimately forming and nurturing His servants from before birth; how does this anticipate the incarnation, where Yeshua Himself was formed in the womb?
Jeremiah protests 'I am only a youth' and doesn't know how to speak, yet Adonai touches his mouth and commissions him anyway; what excuse of inadequacy might Adonai be answering in you this week?

Isaiah 66:1-2 says Adonai looks to 'him who is humble and contrite in spirit and trembles at my word,' even while stating 'heaven is my throne'; how does a right sense of God's transcendence actually produce humility rather than either arrogance or despair?

theme: Forsaking the Fountain of Living Water

questions.text: Jeremiah 2:13 accuses Israel of two evils: forsaking Adonai, 'the fountain of living waters,' and hewing 'broken cisterns that can hold no water'; what does the image of a cracked cistern add that a simple accusation of unfaithfulness wouldn't?

Jeremiah 2:2 recalls 'the devotion (chesed) of your youth, your love as a bride'; how does describing Israel's early relationship with Adonai in bridal, covenantal terms shape the accusation of unfaithfulness that follows?

Jeremiah's image of Adonai as the fountain of living water resonates with Yeshua's words to the woman at the well and at Sukkot in Yochanan's Gospel about living water; how do both texts diagnose the futility of substitute sources of satisfaction?

2 Timothy 1:6-7 urges Timothy to 'fan into flame' his gift, since 'God gave us a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control'; what gift or calling might need rekindling in you rather than being left dormant?

Jeremiah 3 offers Israel a startling invitation to 'return' even after behaving like an adulterous wife; how should a community talk honestly about both the seriousness of unfaithfulness and the reality of an open door back?

theme: Circumcised Hearts and the Son of David

questions.text: Jeremiah 4:23-26 describes judgment in language echoing creation's undoing, 'the earth was without form and void'; what specific images does Jeremiah use to picture this as a kind of reversal of Genesis?

Jeremiah 4:4 calls Judah to 'circumcise yourselves to Adonai, remove the foreskin of your hearts'; how does applying the covenant sign of brit milah metaphorically to the heart reframe what true covenant faithfulness requires?

2 Timothy 2:8 urges Timothy to 'remember Yeshua Messiah, risen from the dead, the offspring of David'; how does this rootedness in Davidic lineage answer the same longing for a faithful king that Jeremiah's generation, under corrupt leadership, so desperately needed?

Jeremiah 5:1 has Adonai challenge anyone to search the streets of Jerusalem to find just one person who does justice, so He might pardon the city; what would it look like for you to be that one person in your own sphere this week?

2 Timothy 2:15 charges Timothy to be 'a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth'; what does rightly handling Scripture look like in practice, and where have you seen it done poorly?

theme: Den of Robbers, True Worship Restored

questions.text: Jeremiah 6:14 accuses the prophets of healing 'the wound of my people lightly, saying peace, peace, when there is no peace'; what makes false reassurance more dangerous here than open conflict?

Jeremiah 7:11 asks whether the Temple 'has become a den of robbers (m'arat paritsim) in your eyes'; what does the image of a den, a hideout robbers retreat to after crimes rather than where crimes occur, reveal about the charge being made?

Yeshua quotes Jeremiah 7:11 directly while overturning tables in the Temple courts; how does connecting His action to Jeremiah's centuries-old Temple Sermon show continuity in God's concern for the Temple's true purpose rather than a rejection of it?

Jeremiah 6:16 invites, 'ask for the ancient paths, where the good way is, and walk in it, and find rest for your souls'; what ancient path of faithfulness might you need to recover this week rather than chase something new?

2 Timothy 3:16 calls 'all Scripture' theopneustos, God-breathed, useful for teaching, reproof, correction, and training; how should this conviction shape the way a group handles a text like Jeremiah's harsh Temple Sermon rather than softening or avoiding it?

theme: Is There No Balm in Gilead?

questions.text: Jeremiah 8:22 laments, 'is there no balm in Gilead? is there no physician there?'; what does this rhetorical question reveal about the depth of Jeremiah's grief over his people's unhealed wound?

Jeremiah 9:23-24 says one should not boast in wisdom, might, or riches, but rather in 'knowing and understanding' Adonai; how does the Hebrew concept of yada, to know, go beyond mere intellectual knowledge to describe intimate relational knowing?

Jeremiah, the weeping prophet, pours out tears in 9:1 over 'the slain of my people,' foreshadowing Yeshua's own weeping over Jerusalem in Luke 19; what do both moments reveal about God's heart toward a people heading toward judgment?

2 Timothy 4:7, 'I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith,' is Sha'ul's testimony near death; what would it take for you to be able to say something similar at the end of this current season?

Jeremiah 9:23-24, echoed approvingly in 1 Corinthians 1:31, redefines what is worth boasting in; how does a culture that prizes wisdom, strength, or wealth challenge a community trying to root its identity in knowing Adonai instead?

theme: A Clean Heart, a Steadfast Ruach

questions.text: Jeremiah 10:3-5 describes an idol as a scarecrow in a cucumber field that 'cannot speak' and 'has to be carried'; what specific mocking details expose the idol's helplessness?

Psalm 51:10 pleads, 'create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me'; the Hebrew bara for 'create' is the same verb used only of God's action in Genesis 1; what does that word choice say about the kind of renewal David is asking for?

Jeremiah 11:19 has Jeremiah describe himself as 'a gentle lamb led to the slaughter,' unaware of the plot against him; how does this personal lament echo, while remaining distinct from, Isaiah 53's description of the Servant led like a lamb to slaughter?

Psalm 51:17 says 'the sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise'; what would bringing genuine brokenness rather than performance look like in your prayer life this week?

Jeremiah 11:21 reveals that the men of Jeremiah's own hometown, Anathoth, conspired to kill him for his message; how should a community respond when the hardest resistance to a hard truth comes from those closest to us?

theme: The Ruined Waistband and the Blessed Hope

questions.text: Jeremiah 13:1-11 has Jeremiah bury a linen loincloth by the Euphrates and dig it up ruined; what specific detail about the garment's condition drives home the point about Judah's corrupted pride?

Jeremiah 12:5 asks, 'if you have raced with men on foot and they have wearied you, how will you compete with horses?'; what does this rhetorical escalation suggest about the greater trials Jeremiah is being warned to prepare for?

Titus 2:13 describes believers 'waiting for our blessed hope, the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Yeshua the Messiah'; how does this forward-looking hope answer the same weariness and complaint Jeremiah voices in chapter 12 about the wicked prospering?

Proverbs 16:18, 'pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall,' pairs naturally with the ruined waistband of Jeremiah 13; what specific area of pride might Adonai be inviting you to lay down before it costs you something?

Jeremiah 13:23 asks, 'can the Ethiopian change his skin, or the leopard his spots?' about people 'accustomed to evil'; how should a community hold together the reality of deeply ingrained sin patterns with genuine hope for transformation?

theme: Eating God's Words as Joy and Fire

questions.text: Jeremiah 16:1-4 records Adonai forbidding Jeremiah to marry, have children, or even attend funerals or feasts; what does the totality of these restrictions communicate about the severity of the coming judgment?

Jeremiah 15:16 says, 'your words were found, and I ate them, and your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart'; what does eating, akal, God's words as a literal act of internalizing them suggest about how Scripture should function in a life?

Titus 3:5 speaks of being saved 'by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Ruach Hakodesh'; how does this renewal language connect to Jeremiah 15's picture of God's words becoming joy and delight, an internal transformation rather than mere external compliance?

Jeremiah 15:18 candidly asks Adonai, 'will you be to me like a deceitful brook, like waters that fail?'; what would it look like to bring that same raw honesty to God about disappointment this week rather than suppressing it?

Jeremiah 14:14 has Adonai declare that false prophets promising peace and prosperity were never sent by Him; how can a community today discern the difference between genuine comfort from God and comfortable messages people simply want to hear?

theme: The Potter's Clay and a Beloved Brother

questions.text: Jeremiah 18:3-4 describes Jeremiah watching a potter rework a spoiled vessel into 'another vessel, as it seemed good to the potter to do'; what does the potter's response to the flaw, reworking rather than discarding, reveal?

Philemon's letter turns on the name Onesimus, which in Greek means useful or profitable; how does Sha'ul's wordplay in verse 11, 'formerly useless to you, but now indeed useful,' tie the runaway slave's name to his transformation?

Jeremiah 17:9-10 says 'the heart is deceitful above all things... I, Adonai, search the heart'; how does this diagnosis of the human heart's condition set up the need for the kind of inward reworking Adonai as potter offers, and that Sha'ul urges Philemon to extend to Onesimus?

Philemon 17-18 has Sha'ul offer to personally cover any debt Onesimus owes, 'charge that to my account'; where might Adonai be asking you to absorb a cost on someone else's behalf rather than demand repayment?

Sha'ul never tells Philemon to free Onesimus outright but appeals 'on the basis of love' for Philemon to receive him 'no longer as a slave but... as a beloved brother'; why might Sha'ul have chosen persuasion over command, and what does that suggest about how transformation is meant to spread within a community?

theme: Fire in the Bones, God Speaks by His Son

questions.text: Jeremiah 19:10-11 has Jeremiah smash a clay flask in front of the elders at the Valley of Ben Hinnom, saying Adonai will break Jerusalem 'as one breaks a potter's vessel... so that it can never be mended'; why does Jeremiah perform this as a visible, physical sign rather than only speaking the words?

Jeremiah 20:9 describes God's word as 'a burning fire shut up in my bones' that Jeremiah cannot hold in even when he tries; what does this image suggest about the involuntary, consuming nature of his prophetic burden?

Hebrews 1:1-2 opens by contrasting how 'God spoke to our fathers by the prophets,' men like Jeremiah bearing fire in their bones, with how 'in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son'; how does this framing honor the prophets' role while pointing to something greater?

Jeremiah 20:14-18 is a raw lament where Jeremiah curses the day of his birth despite his faithful obedience; what permission does this give you to bring genuine despair to God rather than perform false positivity?

Hebrews 1:3 calls the Son 'the exact imprint of his nature' and quotes several Tanakh passages, Psalm 2, Psalm 45, Psalm 102, to establish His identity; how should a chavurah engage respectfully with the fact that these same texts are read differently in the wider Jewish tradition?

theme: The Curse on Coniah, a Brother Who Was Tested

questions.text: Jeremiah 21:8 has Adonai set before the besieged city 'the way of life and the way of death,' where surrender to Babylon means survival and resistance means death; what makes this such an unexpected and unpopular message for a prophet to deliver?

Jeremiah 22:30 pronounces judgment on King Coniah, Jeconiah: 'write this man down as childless... for none of his offspring shall succeed in sitting on the throne of David'; why would this specific curse on a royal line become significant for later discussions of Messiah's genealogy?

Hebrews 2:17 says Yeshua 'had to be made like his brothers in every respect' so He could become 'a merciful and faithful high priest'; how does this full identification with human weakness contrast with the failed kings of Jeremiah 22 who used power for unrighteousness and dishonest gain?

Jeremiah 22:3 commands the king to 'do justice and righteousness, deliver from the hand of the oppressor the one who has been wronged'; what specific act of justice toward someone vulnerable might be within your reach this week?

The curse on Coniah's line in Jeremiah 22:30 creates a genealogical problem later addressed by Matthew's and Luke's differing genealogies of Yeshua; how does tracing Yeshua's legal versus biological lineage help resolve the tension, and why might this detail matter for confidence in the text's coherence?

theme: The Righteous Branch, Adonai Our Righteousness

questions.text: Jeremiah 24:1-10 presents two baskets of figs, good figs representing the exiles already taken to Babylon, bad figs representing those who remained under Zedekiah; what is surprising about which group Adonai considers good?

Jeremiah 23:6 gives the coming righteous Branch, Tzemach, the name 'Adonai Tzidkenu,' Adonai is our righteousness; what is significant about a human descendant of David bearing a name that includes the divine name itself?

Jeremiah 23:5-6's promise of a righteous Branch from David's line who reigns wisely connects directly to Hebrews 3:3-6's description of Yeshua as a faithful Son 'over God's house'; how do both texts point to a ruler whose faithfulness surpasses that of Israel's previous kings?
Jeremiah 23:1-2 condemns shepherds who 'scatter my flock' through neglect; where in your own circles of responsibility, however small, might faithful shepherding be needed this week?

Hebrews 3:2-6 compares Yeshua's faithfulness to Moses' faithfulness 'in all God's house as a servant' without diminishing Moses; how can a community honor Moses' unique role in Torah and Israel's story while also affirming Yeshua's greater sonship, without pitting the two against each other?

theme: Seventy Years and a Sabbath Rest Remaining

questions.text: Jeremiah 26:20-23 recounts the prophet Uriah, who fled to Egypt in fear but was extradited and killed by King Jehoiakim, contrasted with Jeremiah being spared through Ahikam's protection; what does this pairing show about the unpredictable danger faced by Adonai's prophets?

Jeremiah 25:11 prophesies Judah will 'serve the king of Babylon seventy years'; how does this specific, numbered prophecy, later recalled explicitly in Daniel 9:2, demonstrate Adonai's precision even amid judgment?

Hebrews 4:9 speaks of a Sabbath rest, sabbatismos, still remaining for the people of God; how does this promise of rest still to come echo Jeremiah's own promise of a fixed, appointed time when exile would end and restoration would begin?

Hebrews 4:12 describes God's word as 'living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword'; where might you need to let Scripture examine the thoughts and intentions of your own heart rather than using it only to examine others?

Jeremiah 26 shows elders defending Jeremiah's life by recalling that Hezekiah didn't kill Micah for a similarly harsh prophecy; how does this appeal to precedent and communal memory function as a check on abuse of power, and where do we see similar appeals to history today?

theme: True and False Prophecy, the Broken Yoke

questions.text: Jeremiah 28:10-11 shows the false prophet Hananiah publicly breaking the wooden yoke off Jeremiah's neck and prophesying restoration within two years; what makes this such a dramatic, theatrical act of defiance against Jeremiah's message?

After Hananiah breaks the wooden yoke, Jeremiah 28:13 has Adonai declare an iron yoke will replace it; what does the escalation from wood to iron communicate about the consequences of resisting a true, hard word with a false, easy one?

Hebrews 5:8 says Yeshua 'learned obedience through what he suffered,' and Hebrews 5:14 calls for maturity that can 'distinguish good from evil'; how does the ability to discern true prophecy from false, as tested between Jeremiah and Hananiah, model the spiritual maturity Hebrews calls for?

Jeremiah 28:6 has Jeremiah initially respond to Hananiah's false hope with 'Amen! may Adonai do so' before later correcting the record; what does this teach about how even a faithful person should hold a hopeful message loosely until it is confirmed?

Hananiah dies within the year exactly as Jeremiah predicted, for making the people 'trust in a lie'; how should a community today evaluate teachers or leaders who offer comforting but ultimately false assurances, and what accountability, if any, should follow?

theme: Plans for Hope, an Anchor for the Soul

questions.text: Jeremiah 29:5-7 instructs the exiles to build houses, plant gardens, and 'seek the shalom of the city' where they've been sent, even praying for Babylon's welfare; what is countercultural about telling captives to invest in their captors' flourishing?

Jeremiah 29:11's promise of plans for shalom, not evil, uses the rich Hebrew word encompassing wholeness, peace, and welfare; how does shalom's breadth exceed the narrower promise of simple comfort or ease often read into this popular verse?

Hebrews 6:19 calls hope 'an anchor of the soul, sure and steadfast,' entering 'where Yeshua has gone as a forerunner'; how does this anchored hope answer the same exile-era anxiety Jeremiah addresses when he promises 'a future and a hope'?

Jeremiah 29:7 calls the exiles to seek the welfare of a pagan city, 'for in its shalom you will find your shalom'; what would actively seeking the flourishing of your own surrounding community, even one that doesn't share your faith, look like this week?

Hebrews 6:4-6 contains one of the Bible's most debated warnings about the danger of falling away after being enlightened; how have you seen this passage interpreted, and what pastoral care does a community need in applying it without either presumption or despair?

theme: The New Covenant Written on the Heart

questions.text: Jeremiah 32:6-15 has Jeremiah buy a field at Anathoth from his cousin Hanamel while Jerusalem is under Babylonian siege, sealing the deed in a clay jar for safekeeping; what makes this purchase, made from a besieged city about to fall, such a striking act of hope?

Jeremiah 31:33 promises Adonai will put His Torah 'within them' and write it 'on their hearts'; how does this internal writing of Torah differ from, yet still affirm, the ongoing validity of the Torah given at Sinai?

Hebrews 7's description of Melchizedek's priesthood, without recorded genealogy, preceding and superior to Levi, sets up the case made from Jeremiah 31's new covenant in the following chapter; how does a priesthood outside the Levitical line prepare the way for understanding Yeshua's priestly role?
Jeremiah 32:17 has Jeremiah pray, 'nothing is too hard for you,' even while watching his nation collapse; what circumstance in your life right now needs that same declaration prayed over it?

Jeremiah 31:35-37 insists Israel will remain a nation before Adonai 'as long as the sun, moon, and stars endure'; how should this promise of Israel's perpetual existence shape the way believers read the new covenant of 31:31-34, guarding against any notion that it replaces rather than renews Adonai's commitment to Israel?

theme: An Everlasting Covenant Like Day and Night

questions.text: Jeremiah 34:8-11 describes the people of Jerusalem proclaiming liberty, dror, to their Hebrew slaves and then reneging, re-enslaving them; what does this reversal reveal about the shallowness of their covenant commitment?

Jeremiah 33:20-21 compares the permanence of Adonai's covenant with David and the Levitical priests to His 'covenant with day and night'; what does invoking the fixed order of creation itself suggest about how unbreakable these covenants are meant to be?

Hebrews 8:8-12 quotes Jeremiah 31:31-34 in full regarding the new covenant; how does reading this quotation alongside Jeremiah 33's reaffirmation of the Davidic and Levitical covenants' permanence guard against reading the new covenant as erasing rather than fulfilling what came before?

Jeremiah 34's account of broken promises to freed slaves is a warning against reversing acts of justice once convenient pressure eases; what commitment to someone's good might you be tempted to quietly walk back, and what would faithfulness look like instead?

Hebrews 8:13 calls the first covenant 'obsolete' and 'growing old'; given Jeremiah 33's insistence elsewhere that God's covenants with Israel are as fixed as day and night, how should this language in Hebrews be understood so as not to contradict the Torah's and the Prophets' own testimony to Israel's enduring place?

theme: Faithful Rechabites, a Burned Scroll, Better Blood

questions.text: Notice how the Rechabites refuse Jeremiah's wine in the Temple chamber because of Jonadab's centuries-old command - what does their loyalty to a human father's instruction reveal about Judah's disloyalty to Adonai's instruction through the prophets?

The scroll Baruch writes is a 'megillah' in Hebrew; trace what happens to it in Jeremiah 36 as it is cut with a scribe's knife and cast into the firepot column by column, then rewritten 'with many similar words added' (36:32).

Hebrews 9 contrasts the blood of goats and calves sprinkled in the earthly sanctuary with Messiah's own blood offered once for all - how does this 'better sacrifice' answer the ongoing cycle of covenant-breaking that fills Jeremiah's scroll?
Where in your life this week might you be tempted, like Jehoiakim, to cut away and discard the parts of God's word that convict you rather than let them shape you?
Why does God hold up the Rechabites - not even ethnic Israelites - as a model of faithfulness while judging Judah, and what does that suggest about how Adonai measures covenant loyalty?

theme: Jeremiah in the Cistern, Faith That Doesn't Shrink Back

questions.text: Notice Ebed-Melech the Cushite official's specific care - pulling old rags and worn-out clothes to pad the ropes so they wouldn't hurt Jeremiah's armpits as he's drawn up from the muddy cistern (38:11-12).
Zedekiah repeatedly asks Jeremiah for a 'davar' - a word - from Adonai in secret; consider why the king seeks the word yet lacks the courage to act on it.

Hebrews 10 warns against 'shrinking back' and describes confidence to enter the Most Holy Place through Yeshua's flesh as a 'new and living way' - how does Zedekiah's fear-driven hesitation illustrate the opposite of that confident faith?
Consider one decision this week where fear of what others will think is holding you back from a course you already know Adonai has laid out for you.

Ebed-Melech, a foreign court official, rescues the prophet while Judah's own officials leave him to die - what does this pattern of outsiders acting righteously suggest about where courage and compassion can be found?

theme: Jerusalem Falls, Faith's Long Roll Call

questions.text: Notice the specific mercy shown to Jeremiah amid the city's fall - Nebuzaradan gives him a choice to go to Babylon or stay, and Gedaliah is appointed governor over the remnant left in the land (39:11-14; 40:5-6).

Zedekiah's sons are killed before his eyes, then his own eyes are put out - the last image he carries; consider how this cruel irony centers on his failure to truly 'see' Jeremiah's repeated warnings.

Hebrews 11's roll call of faith celebrates those who 'died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them from afar' - how does this frame the remnant left in a devastated Judah, still awaiting promised restoration?
Faith's heroes in Hebrews 11 acted on promises they would not see fulfilled in their lifetime; what promise are you holding onto this week that requires that same patient trust?
Gedaliah is a righteous, Babylon-appointed governor trying to hold together a broken community - what does his brief, doomed administration suggest about the limits of good leadership under judgment?

theme: Gedaliah's Murder and the Unshakable Kingdom

questions.text: Notice how Ishmael son of Nethaniah, of royal blood, assassinates Gedaliah at a shared meal in Mizpah - even hospitality becomes the setting for betrayal (41:1-3).

Agur's confession in Proverbs 30 that he does not have 'the knowledge of the Holy One' models a humility strikingly absent from the remnant who ask Jeremiah for guidance while already intending to disobey it (42:20).

Hebrews 12 contrasts the terrifying, untouchable Mount Sinai with the accessible Mount Zion, 'the city of the living God' - how does this offer hope beyond the devastation and treachery unfolding around Mizpah and the fleeing remnant?
The remnant asks Jeremiah for God's will while already planning to flee to Egypt regardless of the answer - where might you be asking God for direction while your mind is already made up?
Hebrews 12 describes discipline as proof of sonship; how do we reconcile that idea with the raw devastation of Judah's judgment in Jeremiah 41-42 without minimizing real suffering?

theme: Fleeing to Egypt, a Woman of Valor, Love That Endures

questions.text: Notice the remnant's specific act of defiance in Jeremiah 44 - burning incense to the 'Queen of Heaven' and blaming their earlier troubles on having stopped that worship, rather than on their idolatry (44:17-18).

Proverbs 31's 'eshet chayil' - woman of valor - is prized far above jewels; consider how her strength, wisdom, and fear of Adonai contrast with the remnant's stubborn unfaithfulness in the same day's Jeremiah reading.

Hebrews 13 closes with 'Yeshua the Messiah is the same yesterday, today, and forever' and calls believers to go to Him 'outside the camp' - how does this steadiness answer a people who keep looking for security in the wrong place, whether Egypt or foreign gods?
What would it look like this week to 'let brotherly love continue' and show hospitality to a stranger, as Hebrews 13:1-2 urges?

The remnant explicitly prefers the 'Queen of Heaven' because they remember prosperity under her worship - how should communities today wrestle with nostalgia for practices that felt like blessing but were actually unfaithfulness?

theme: A Word for Baruch, Wisdom's Beginning, Faith Under Trial

questions.text: Notice the tender, personal message to Baruch in Jeremiah 45 - God tells him not to 'seek great things' for himself amid the coming catastrophe, while still promising to spare his life wherever he goes.
Proverbs 1:7 sets 'the fear of Adonai' as the 'reshit' - the beginning - of knowledge; consider how this reshit functions as a foundation rather than merely a chronological starting point.

James 1 describes wisdom as something God 'gives generously to all without finding fault' when we ask in faith - how does this connect to Proverbs 1's picture of wisdom calling aloud in the street, offering herself freely to whoever will listen?

James 1:22 insists we must be 'doers of the word, and not hearers only' - what specific action will you take this week in response to something you've been reading rather than just nodding along to it?

Baruch is told not to seek great things for himself in a moment of national collapse; how should believers today weigh personal ambition against faithfulness during seasons of communal upheaval or crisis?

theme: Judgment on the Nations, Faith Proven by Deeds

questions.text: Notice the vivid image in Jeremiah 48:11 describing Moab as having 'settled on his dregs' like wine never poured from vessel to vessel - a picture of complacency about to be shattered.
James 2:23 calls Abraham 'God's friend' (Greek 'philos'); explore how this friendship language connects to the intimate wisdom Proverbs 2 promises to those who seek it 'as silver.'

James 2 insists faith without works is dead, citing both Abraham's near-sacrifice of Isaac and Rahab the prostitute's hiding of the spies - how do these two very different figures together model the kind of faith Jeremiah's audience lacked?

James 2 warns against showing favoritism to the rich over the poor in the assembly; is there a relationship this week where you're tempted to value someone based on status rather than treating them with equal honor?
Rahab, a Gentile and a prostitute, becomes a byword for justifying faith in James 2 - what does her inclusion suggest about who gets to belong to the story of covenant faithfulness?

theme: Babylon's Coming Fall, Trusting with All Your Heart

questions.text: Notice that Jeremiah 50 begins the long oracle against Babylon itself - the very empire God has used as His instrument of judgment is now told its own reckoning is coming, invaded 'out of the north.'

Proverbs 3:5's command to trust Adonai 'with all your heart' and not lean on 'your own understanding' directly addresses the temptation the nations show throughout Jeremiah 49 - trusting their own strongholds and wisdom.

James 3 describes the tongue as 'a small member' that can set a whole forest ablaze - set beside the sweeping destruction Jeremiah pronounces on Ammon, Edom, and Babylon by God's spoken word, it pictures how much power lies in what is spoken.
James 3:17 describes wisdom from above as 'peaceable, gentle, ready to obey, full of mercy' - which of these qualities most needs to characterize a conversation you'll have this week?

Babylon is God's chosen instrument of judgment in earlier chapters, yet here its own judgment is announced - what does this pattern teach about nations or powers that God uses but does not thereby endorse?

theme: Babylon Falls, Jehoiachin Lifted Up, Friendship with the World

questions.text: Notice how the book of Jeremiah ends not with destruction but a small note of grace - King Jehoiachin, exiled 37 years earlier, is released from prison in Babylon and eats at the king's table for the rest of his life (52:31-34).

Jeremiah 51:6 urges the exiles, 'Flee from the midst of Babylon' (nusu); consider how this literal command to physically leave a doomed city parallels James 4:4's warning that 'friendship with the world is enmity with God.'

The small, quiet mercy shown to Jehoiachin at the close of Jeremiah - a Davidic descendant preserved even in exile - anticipates the line through which Messiah would come, sustained by Adonai even through judgment.
James 4:14 asks, 'What is your life? For you are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes' - how might remembering this reshape one plan or worry you're carrying this week?
Why end an entire book of devastating judgment with a single verse of mercy toward a defeated king - what does that placement suggest about how Adonai's justice and compassion relate to each other?

theme: Ezekiel's Throne Vision and the Righteous King

questions.text: Notice the overwhelming detail in Ezekiel's opening vision - four living creatures each with four faces and four wings, wheels within wheels full of eyes, all beneath a throne of sapphire with a figure like fire and brilliant light (1:4-28).
God repeatedly addresses Ezekiel as 'ben-adam,' son of man - a title of creaturely humility that later becomes Yeshua's own favored self-designation in the Gospels.

Psalm 72, a prayer for Solomon, describes a king whose 'dominion shall be from sea to sea' and who will 'have pity on the poor and needy' - language read by Jewish and Messianic tradition alike as ultimately pointing beyond Solomon to Messiah's reign.
Ezekiel falls on his face at the overwhelming vision of God's glory before he can be commissioned to speak; what posture of humility do you need to recover before God can use you this week?

James 5 rebukes wealthy landowners for withholding wages and living in self-indulgent luxury - what would it look like for a modern community to take seriously James's warning that the cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of Adonai Tzva'ot?

theme: Eating the Scroll, a Living Hope

questions.text: Notice that when Ezekiel eats the scroll filled with 'lamentation, mourning, and woe,' it tastes 'sweet as honey' in his mouth (3:1-3) - even hard words from God can nourish when received as His own.
Ezekiel is appointed a 'tzofeh' - watchman - for the house of Israel (3:17), responsible to warn even when the message is unwelcome, a role of costly faithfulness rather than popularity.

1 Peter 1:10-11 says the prophets themselves searched intently to understand what the Ruach ha-Kodesh was revealing about 'the sufferings of Messiah and the glories to follow' - Ezekiel's bitter-yet-sweet scroll vividly pictures that same mix of suffering and future hope.
Peter calls believers to 'be holy, for I am holy,' echoing Leviticus - what specific habit or attitude needs to change this week to reflect that call?

Ezekiel lies on his side for 390 and then 40 days as a living sign-act of Israel's and Judah's sin - what does God's use of such a physically costly, embodied prophecy suggest about how seriously He wants the message taken, and how might that challenge comfortable faith today?

theme: Judgment on Jerusalem, Living Stones

questions.text: Notice the shocking sign-act that opens chapter 5 - Ezekiel shaves his head and beard, then divides the hair into thirds to burn, strike, and scatter, each portion symbolizing a fate awaiting Jerusalem's people.
Chapter 7 repeats 'ha-ketz ba' - 'the end has come' - with hammering intensity; consider why the text piles up this phrase rather than stating judgment once.

1 Peter 2:4-6 describes Yeshua as the 'living stone' rejected by builders but chosen by God, and believers as 'living stones' built into a spiritual house - a striking contrast to the physical stones of Jerusalem's temple about to be torn down in Ezekiel's day.

1 Peter 2:21 says Messiah suffered 'leaving you an example, so that you should follow in His steps' - where this week could you respond to mistreatment the way He did, entrusting yourself to God rather than retaliating?

Ezekiel 7 says even silver and gold will be thrown into the streets because they cannot deliver in the day of Adonai's wrath - what modern equivalents of 'silver and gold' do people trust for security, and what would it mean to hold them loosely?

theme: Abominations in the Temple, Suffering for Doing Good

questions.text: Notice the escalating abominations Ezekiel is shown inside the temple itself - the image of jealousy, elders worshiping idols in darkness, women weeping for Tammuz, and men bowing to the sun with their backs to the sanctuary (8:5-16).
The man in linen marks a 'tav' - a sign - on the foreheads of those who 'sigh and groan' over Jerusalem's abominations (9:4); those grieved by evil are marked for protection before judgment falls.

1 Peter 3:18 says Messiah 'suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God' - the marked remnant in Ezekiel 9, spared because they grieved sin rather than committed it, prefigures how those united to the Righteous One are spared judgment.
1 Peter 3:15 calls believers to always be ready to give a defense 'with gentleness and respect' for the hope within them - who might you have that conversation with this week?

Only those who 'sigh and groan' over the evil around them are marked for protection in Ezekiel 9 - what does this suggest about complacency versus grief as a moral posture, and how should that shape how we respond to injustice today?

theme: The Glory Departs, Suffering as Stewards of Grace

questions.text: Notice the slow, reluctant movement of God's glory in these chapters - from the cherubim to the threshold (10:18) and finally to the mountain east of the city (11:23), departing Jerusalem in stages rather than all at once.

Amid the judgment, God promises through Ezekiel to give the exiles 'lev echad' and 'ruach chadashah' - one heart and a new spirit, replacing a heart of stone with a heart of flesh (11:19) - renewal language later echoed throughout the B'rit Chadashah's teaching on the Ruach ha-Kodesh.
1 Peter 4:17 says 'judgment begins at the household of God' - exactly what Ezekiel's vision depicts, with glory departing the temple itself before wider judgment on the city and nations proceeds.

Peter urges believers to use whatever gift they've received to 'serve one another, as good stewards of God's varied grace' - what specific gift could you put to use this week for someone else's benefit?
God's glory doesn't vanish suddenly but withdraws step by step, almost hesitantly, from the temple - what does this pacing suggest about how Adonai relates to a people He is disciplining?

theme: Packing for Exile, Shepherds Who Serve Humbly

questions.text: Notice Ezekiel's sign-act in chapter 12 - digging through a wall and carrying his baggage out at twilight with his face covered, acting out exactly what King Zedekiah and the people would soon experience.

Ezekiel 13 condemns false prophets for 'whitewashing' a flimsy wall (tafel, untempered plaster) to make it look solid - papering over coming disaster with false assurances of 'shalom, shalom' when there is no shalom.

1 Peter 5:2-4 describes elders as shepherds who serve 'not for shameful gain' but eagerly, awaiting reward from the 'Chief Shepherd' when He appears - a direct rebuke of the self-serving false prophets Ezekiel confronts.
1 Peter 5:6-7 calls believers to humble themselves and cast all their anxiety on God because He cares for them - what anxiety are you carrying this week that you need to consciously hand over?

Ezekiel's false prophets promise 'shalom' that isn't real; how can communities today discern the difference between leaders who offer genuine comfort grounded in truth and those who simply tell people what they want to hear?

theme: Idols of the Heart, Confirming Our Calling

questions.text: Notice God's striking claim in 14:14 that even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were present in a sinful land, 'they would deliver only themselves' by their righteousness - personal integrity cannot substitute for national repentance.
Ezekiel 14:3 speaks of elders who have set up 'gilulim b'libam' - idols in their hearts - locating the sin not merely in physical statues but in inner devotion.

2 Peter 1:16-18 grounds Peter's authority in having been an eyewitness to Messiah's transfiguration glory on the mountain - a real, historical anchor set against the false, self-invented spirituality Ezekiel 14 condemns among Israel's elders consulting idols in secret.
2 Peter 1:5-7 lays out a chain - add to faith virtue, knowledge, self-control, perseverance, godliness, brotherly affection, love - which link in that chain most needs your attention this week?

Why would God say that Noah, Daniel, and Job's righteousness could save only themselves and not others in the land - what does this suggest about the limits of individual righteousness versus the need for communal repentance?

theme: Jerusalem the Unfaithful Bride, False Teachers Warned

questions.text: Notice the tender opening of Ezekiel 16 - Jerusalem pictured as an abandoned newborn, unwashed and unwanted, whom God finds, cleans, and raises to full beauty before she turns to prostitute herself with other lovers.

The riddle of the great eagles and the vine in chapter 17 uses the image of a 'gephen' - vine - to describe Zedekiah's broken covenant loyalty to Babylon, political betrayal pictured as a vine reaching toward the wrong eagle.

2 Peter 2:22 quotes the proverb 'a dog returns to its own vomit' to describe those who fall back into corruption after knowing the way of righteousness - fitting commentary on Jerusalem's repeated returns to idolatry despite God's covenant kindness in Ezekiel 16.

Peter warns of false teachers who exploit people 'with false words' for personal gain; what would it look like to test the messages and teachers you're following this week against Scripture rather than charisma or promises?
Ezekiel 16 is one of the Bible's most graphic and uncomfortable allegories; how should readers today handle the tension between its intended message and its harsh, sexualized imagery?

theme: The Soul Who Sins, Scoffers and the Day of the Lord

questions.text: Notice how Ezekiel 18 directly rejects the proverb 'the fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge,' insisting instead that 'the soul who sins shall die' - individual moral responsibility replacing inherited blame.
Ezekiel 19 is labeled a 'kinah' - lament - for the princes of Israel, pictured first as a lion cub and then as a vine, a genre of mourning poetry sung here over a still-living but doomed dynasty.

2 Peter 3:9 answers scoffers who ask 'where is the promise of His coming' by insisting Adonai is patient, 'not wishing that any should perish' - a direct echo of Ezekiel 18:23's own question, whether God takes pleasure in the death of the wicked rather than their turning to live.

Peter urges believers to live 'holy and godly' lives while waiting for the day of the Lord, since everything will ultimately be dissolved - what would living with that end in view change about your priorities this week?

Ezekiel 18 insists each person is judged for their own sin, not their parents' - how do we hold that together with the very real, observable ways generational sin and family patterns do shape individuals?

theme: A History of Rebellion, Walking in the Light

questions.text: Notice how Ezekiel 20 recounts Israel's history of rebellion stretching all the way back to Egypt, before Sinai - the pattern of idolatry did not start with Canaan but existed even during the exodus.
Ezekiel 21 personifies Adonai's sword ('cherev') as a weapon 'sharpened and polished' for slaughter, given theatrical, poetic dread as it is described clapping and turning.

1 John 1:5-7 declares 'God is light, and in Him is no darkness at all,' and that walking in the light brings fellowship and cleansing by 'the blood of Yeshua His Son' - a direct contrast to the long history of Israel's hidden idolatry that Ezekiel 20 exposes.
1 John 1:9 promises that if we confess our sins, God is 'faithful and just' to forgive - what sin have you been avoiding naming honestly before Him that you could confess this week?

Ezekiel 20 shows God withholding full judgment repeatedly 'for the sake of My name' among the nations rather than because Israel deserved it - what does this reveal about the motives behind God's patience, and how should that shape our own view of grace?

theme: Two Unfaithful Sisters, Loving Not the World

questions.text: Notice the catalog of specific sins listed in Ezekiel 22:6-12 - bloodshed, contempt for parents, oppression of the sojourner, taking bribes, sexual sin - an indictment showing corruption saturating every level of society.
The names Oholah and Oholibah in chapter 23 represent Samaria and Jerusalem as two sisters whose infidelity plays out through political alliances with Assyria and Babylon pictured as illicit lovers.

1 John 2:15-17 warns, 'Do not love the world or the things in the world,' listing the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life - categories that map closely onto the political and sexual 'lovers' Jerusalem and Samaria pursue instead of covenant faithfulness.

John writes that whoever keeps God's word truly has love for Him 'perfected' in them - what single command of Yeshua's could you deliberately practice this week as an act of love rather than obligation?

Ezekiel 23 uses shockingly explicit imagery to indict Israel's political alliances as spiritual adultery; what does it mean for a nation's trusted alliances or dependencies to become, in effect, idolatry?

theme: The Boiling Pot, Love in Deed and Truth

questions.text: Notice the deeply personal sign-act at the heart of chapter 24 - Ezekiel's wife, 'the delight of his eyes,' dies suddenly, and he is commanded not to mourn publicly, becoming himself a living sign of the coming, unmournable devastation of Jerusalem.
The rusted, boiling pot ('sir,' cauldron) of 24:1-14 pictures Jerusalem's corruption as baked-in rust that no amount of boiling can remove, requiring the pot itself to be emptied and scorched clean.

1 John 3:16-18 insists real love is shown 'in deed and truth,' not merely word - a standard Ezekiel's silent, obedient grief embodies, standing over against the empty, ritual mourning practices Jerusalem is condemned for lacking in substance.

John asks how God's love can abide in anyone who has the world's goods and sees a brother in need yet closes his heart against him - is there a concrete need you could meet this week rather than simply feeling sympathy?

God forbids Ezekiel from mourning his wife's death as a prophetic sign to Israel - how should we think about the cost prophets and leaders sometimes bear personally in order to communicate God's message to a wider community?

theme: Tyre's Fall, Perfect Love Casting Out Fear

questions.text: Notice how Ezekiel 27 dedicates an entire chapter to Tyre's trade network in painstaking detail - merchants, cargo, and materials from Tarshish, Damascus, and Judah - before picturing the great trading ship wrecked and sinking with all its wealth.
Psalm 84:2 describes the psalmist's soul as 'kalta' - yearning, even fainting - for the courts of Adonai, a longing for God's presence set against Tyre's confidence in its own wealth and location.
1 John 4:18 declares that 'perfect love casts out fear,' grounding security not in commercial power or fortified position, as Tyre trusted, but in the love of God made known through sending His Son.
1 John 4:20-21 insists that anyone who claims to love God while hating a brother is a liar - is there a strained relationship this week where love needs to move from claim to action?

Tyre's downfall in Ezekiel 26-27 stems partly from its gloating over Jerusalem's fall as a trading opportunity - what does this suggest about how nations or people can be judged for how they respond to others' suffering?

theme: The King of Tyre in Eden, Faith That Overcomes

questions.text: Notice the startling language in Ezekiel 28:13-15 describing the king of Tyre as having been 'in Eden, the garden of God,' a guardian cherub who was blameless 'until unrighteousness was found' in him.

Pharaoh is called 'the great sea monster' ('tannin,' often translated dragon) lying in the midst of the Nile's streams (29:3) - chaos-serpent imagery applied to Egypt's arrogant claim, 'My Nile is my own; I made it.'

1 John 5:4-5 declares that 'whoever is born of God overcomes the world,' and this victory is 'our faith' - a direct answer to the prideful self-sufficiency of both the king of Tyre and Pharaoh, who each claimed divine status or self-made greatness rather than trusting Adonai.

John writes 'these things I have written... so that you may know that you have eternal life' - what would it look like to live this week with settled confidence rather than anxious uncertainty about your standing with God?

Ezekiel 28's oracle against the king of Tyre is often read on two levels - an earthly ruler and a picture of primordial rebellion - how should we handle biblical passages that seem to operate on both a historical and a cosmic-spiritual level?

theme: Egypt's Fall Foreshadowed, Walking in Truth and Love

questions.text: Notice how Ezekiel 31 uses Assyria's fallen glory - pictured as a towering cedar in Lebanon whose height 'was exalted above all the trees' before it was cut down - as a warning example held up specifically for Pharaoh and Egypt to consider.
Psalm 86:11 prays 'horeni Adonai darkecha' - teach me Your way, Adonai - asking for 'an undivided heart' to fear His name, the same wholehearted devotion 2 John commends in those who 'walk in truth.'

2 John warns believers to watch out for deceivers who deny 'Yeshua the Messiah coming in the flesh,' while Ezekiel 30-31 warns nations against trusting in visible, worldly power rather than the truth of who God is.
2 John instructs believers to love one another by walking according to God's commandments - what is one concrete way you could show that love to someone in your community this week?
Ezekiel 31 uses Assyria's collapse as a cautionary tale for Egypt - what past examples, personal, national, or historical, do we tend to ignore as warnings for our own present pride or complacency?

theme: Descent to Sheol, Faithful Hospitality Commended

questions.text: Notice how Ezekiel 32 pictures Pharaoh and Egypt descending into Sheol to join a grim assembly of previously fallen empires - Assyria, Elam, Meshech-Tubal, Edom - already lying there in disgrace.
Ezekiel 33:7 restates Ezekiel's role as 'tzofeh' - watchman - responsible to warn the wicked; the same chapter records that news finally arrives, 'the city has fallen' (33:21).

3 John commends Gaius for his faithfulness and hospitality toward traveling teachers 'for the sake of the Name' - a small, personal act of covenant loyalty set against the sweeping, impersonal downfall of empires we've been reading about in Ezekiel.
3 John warns against Diotrephes, who 'loves to be first' and refuses to welcome others - is there any way ambition or control could be creeping into how you treat people in community this week?

Ezekiel is told even a faithful watchman isn't responsible for a hearer's choice to reject the warning, only for giving it - how should that principle shape how much responsibility we feel for outcomes when we've spoken truth to someone who doesn't listen?

theme: The True Shepherd Promised, Contending for the Faith

questions.text: Notice the sharp indictment of Israel's shepherds in Ezekiel 34:2-4 - feeding themselves instead of the flock, not strengthening the weak or healing the sick, ruling 'with force and harshness' - before God promises to shepherd the flock Himself.
Ezekiel 34:23 promises God will set up 'one shepherd, My servant David' over the flock - a davidic hope the B'rit Chadashah identifies as fulfilled in Yeshua, the Good Shepherd.

Jude 1 urges believers to 'contend earnestly for the faith once for all delivered,' warning of ungodly people who creep in unnoticed - a New Covenant echo of Ezekiel 34's false shepherds who exploit rather than protect the flock entrusted to them.

Jude describes building yourselves up in 'your most holy faith,' praying in the Ruach ha-Kodesh, and keeping yourselves in God's love - which of these three practices most needs strengthening in your life this week?

Psalm 88 is unique among the Psalms for ending without resolution or praise, closing instead with 'darkness is my closest friend' - what place does unresolved lament have in faith, and how should communities today make room for it?

theme: Dry Bones Live, the Son of Man Among the Lampstands

questions.text: Notice the specific progression of the valley of dry bones vision - bones coming together, then sinews and flesh, then skin, and only at the very end breath entering to make them live (37:7-10), a step-by-step resurrection.

Ezekiel 37 uses 'ruach' - breath, spirit, wind - repeatedly in a few verses, the same word used of God's breath in Genesis 2:7, tying Israel's national resurrection to the language of humanity's original creation.

Revelation 1:12-18 shows Yochanan's vision of the risen Yeshua standing among seven golden lampstands, holding the keys of death and Sheol - the ultimate fulfillment of the life-from-death power Ezekiel's dry bones vision anticipates.

God tells Ezekiel the dry bones represent Israel's despairing cry, 'our bones are dried up, our hope is lost' (37:11) - what area of your life feels like dry bones right now that needs to hear God's promise of restored breath?

Ezekiel 37 also includes the sign of two sticks - Judah and Israel/Joseph - joined into one in God's hand; what does this vision of reunification suggest about how deep divisions might ultimately be healed?

theme: Gog and Magog, Letters to a Church That Lost Its First Love

questions.text: Notice the specific coalition Ezekiel names in chapter 38 - Gog of the land of Magog, along with Persia, Cush, Put, Gomer, and Beth-togarmah - a massive northern-led alliance coming against a peaceful, unwalled Israel.

Ezekiel 39:7 has God declaring, 'I will make My holy name known' among the nations through this deliverance - the ultimate purpose behind the battle is the vindication of God's own name, not merely Israel's rescue.

Revelation 2:4-5 rebukes Ephesus for having 'left your first love,' calling the church to remember and repent - while Ezekiel 39 pictures restoration precisely so both Israel and the nations remember who God is; both hinge on returning to right relationship rather than merely correct behavior.
The message to Ephesus calls for a return to 'the deeds you did at first' - what spiritual practice or devotion from earlier in your walk with God might be worth returning to this week?

Ezekiel's Gog and Magog battle has generated wide-ranging interpretations about timing and identity across Jewish and Christian traditions; how should believers hold prophetic passages like this with both seriousness and humility about the details?

theme: A Temple Measured in Detail, an Open Door

questions.text: Notice the meticulous, almost architectural precision of Ezekiel's guided tour in chapters 40-41 - a man with a measuring reed and linen cord walks him through gates, chambers, and courts down to exact cubit measurements.

Psalm 91:1 describes the one who dwells 'b'tzel Shaddai' - in the shadow of the Almighty - finding refuge, a striking contrast to the exacting, external measurements of Ezekiel's temple vision; one is architecture, the other intimate shelter.

Revelation 3:7-8 has Yeshua telling Philadelphia, 'I have set before you an open door that no one can shut' - a striking counterpoint to Ezekiel's later vision of the temple's east gate being shut permanently because the glory of Adonai entered through it.
Revelation 3:20 pictures Yeshua standing at the door and knocking, asking to be let in and to dine together - where might He be asking for a more welcomed place in your daily rhythm this week?

Ezekiel's temple vision is famously difficult to place historically - has it been built, will it be built, is it symbolic? - how should believers approach prophetic architecture like this without either dismissing it or over-literalizing every detail?

theme: The Glory Returns, Holy Holy Holy

questions.text: Notice the vivid detail of Ezekiel 43:2 - the glory of the God of Israel comes from the east, and 'the earth shone with His glory,' with a sound 'like the sound of many waters,' directly reversing the glory's earlier departure in chapters 10-11.

Proverbs 25:2 says 'it is the glory of God to conceal a matter, but the glory of kings is to search it out' - a fitting proverb for a day whose Tanakh reading is thick with concealed, symbolic architectural detail waiting to be searched out.

Revelation 4:8 has the four living creatures crying 'Holy, holy, holy is Adonai Elohei-Tzva'ot,' directly echoing Isaiah's throne room and paralleling the returning glory and worship centered on God's throne in Ezekiel 43.

Psalm 92, a song for the Sabbath, celebrates that 'it is good to give thanks to Adonai' and to declare His works 'in the morning' and 'in the night' - what rhythm of daily gratitude could you build into this week?

Ezekiel 43 ties the return of God's glory to Israel no longer defiling His holy name with idolatry and royal corpses near the temple (43:7-9) - what does this suggest about the connection between the presence of God and a community's holiness practices?

theme: The Shut Gate, the Lion Who Is the Lamb

questions.text: Notice the specific rule in Ezekiel 44:1-3 - the outer east gate through which God's glory entered is shut permanently, and only 'the prince' may sit in its gateway to eat before Adonai.

Ezekiel 44 distinguishes Levites in general from the 'b'nei Tzadok' - sons of Zadok - who alone may approach to minister at the altar because they 'kept the charge of My sanctuary' when Israel went astray.

Revelation 5:5-6 has the 'Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David' announced as worthy to open the sealed scroll, yet what Yochanan actually sees is 'a Lamb, as though it had been slain' - the paradox of conquering through sacrifice answers Ezekiel's picture of a coming prince who alone has rightful access to God's presence.

The riddle-like proverbs of chapter 26 warn against answering fools according to their folly one moment and not answering them the next (26:4-5) - what discernment do you need this week to know when engagement helps and when it only feeds a bad dynamic?

Revelation 5 pictures all creation joining in worship of the Lamb; how does this universal scope relate to Ezekiel's much more restricted, carefully guarded picture of who may approach God's presence in the restored temple?

theme: The Prince's Gate, the River, and the Cry for Justice

questions.text: Notice in Ezekiel 46 that the prince (nasi) enters the temple's east gate only on Shabbat and Rosh Chodesh and must leave the same way he came in — what does this detail suggest about even a ruler's posture before Adonai?

In Ezekiel 47 the river flowing from beneath the temple threshold deepens the farther it flows, from ankle-deep to a river no one can cross — what does this progression teach about how life flows outward from God's presence?

Ezekiel 48:35 closes the book with the city's new name, Adonai Shammah, 'the LORD Is There' — how does this anticipate Revelation 6's scene, where the martyrs cry out from beneath the altar confident that the enthroned LORD sees and will act?

Ezekiel 47's river brings healing even to the Dead Sea so that fish thrive in it — where in your own life or community do you need to trust that God's presence can make a 'dead' place come alive again?

The souls under the altar in Revelation 6:9-10 cry 'How long, O Sovereign Lord, before You judge?' and are told to wait a little longer — how do we hold trust in God's justice together with the real pain of unanswered 'how long' prayers, as Psalm 94 also wrestles with?

theme: Gomer, the Wilderness, and a New Betrothal

questions.text: Hosea is told to name his children Jezreel, Lo-Ruhamah ('not pitied'), and Lo-Ammi ('not my people') — what does it mean that God asks a prophet to live out a message through his own family?

In Hosea 2:19-20 God says 'I will betroth you (v'erastikh) to Me forever,' using the language of a Jewish marriage covenant — how does this specific covenant term shape the meaning of Israel's restoration?

Hosea 2:14-15 pictures God luring Israel into the wilderness and turning the Valley of Achor ('trouble') into a 'door of hope' — how does this pattern of a redeemed people echo in Revelation 7's vision of the 144,000 from Israel's tribes and the great multitude from every nation?
Hosea 2:23 promises, 'I will say to Lo-Ammi, You are My people' — where do you need to receive God's reversal of shame or rejection in your own story this week?

Why would God command a prophet to marry a woman who would be unfaithful as the very means of revealing His own heart toward an unfaithful people — what does this uncomfortable object lesson teach about how far God goes to communicate His love?

theme: Bought Back, and a Land Without Knowledge

questions.text: In Hosea 3, Hosea must buy Gomer back for fifteen shekels of silver and a homer and a half of barley — what does this price and act of repurchase reveal about redemption?
Hosea 4:1 charges Israel with no da'at Elohim, 'knowledge of God,' in the land — how is da'at here more than head knowledge, but relational faithfulness?

Hosea 3:5 promises that 'afterward the children of Israel will return and seek Adonai their God and David their king' — how does this hope of a coming Davidic king anticipate the worship scenes unfolding as the seven trumpets begin in Revelation 8?
Hosea 4:6 says, 'My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge' — what practical step could you take this week to deepen genuine knowledge of God rather than settling for surface familiarity?

Hosea acts out redemption by literally purchasing his unfaithful wife back — how should this shape how a community responds to someone who has broken trust, and what does it cost the one who forgives?

theme: Torn and Healed, Raised on the Third Day

questions.text: Hosea 5:15 has God saying He will return to His place 'until they acknowledge their guilt and seek My face' — what does God's withdrawal, rather than immediate punishment, teach about how He deals with a stubborn people?

Hosea 6:6 states, 'I desire chesed and not sacrifice, and da'at Elohim more than burnt offerings' — how does chesed, steadfast covenant love, differ from mere ritual, and why would Yeshua later quote this verse twice in Matthew?

Hosea 6:1-2 says, 'He will revive us after two days; on the third day He will raise us up, that we may live before Him' — how might this shape our reading of Yeshua's resurrection 'on the third day,' even amid the terrifying judgments unleashed in Revelation 9?

Hosea 6:4 laments that Israel's devotion 'is like a morning cloud, like dew that goes early away' — what would it look like to cultivate steadier devotion to God this week rather than short-lived bursts of feeling?

God says He desires chesed over sacrifice — in what ways might religious communities today still substitute ritual performance for real mercy and relational faithfulness, and how do we tell the difference in ourselves?

theme: A Cake Not Turned, a Wind Sown

questions.text: Hosea 7:8 pictures Ephraim as 'a cake not turned' — burned on one side, raw on the other — what does this odd baking image suggest about a divided, half-hearted people?

Hosea 8:7 warns, 'They sow the wind, and they will reap the whirlwind (sufah)' — how does this pairing of small cause and devastating consequence apply to Israel's calf-idols and foreign alliances in this chapter?

Hosea 8:14 says Israel 'has forgotten his Maker and built palaces,' trusting fortresses instead of God — how does John's call in Revelation 10 to 'take and eat' the little scroll, even though it turns bitter in the stomach, model the opposite: fully absorbing God's word rather than half-digesting it?
Where in your life have you been 'sowing the wind' — small compromises — that could be growing into a whirlwind of consequence, and what would it take to turn back now?

Hosea 8 condemns Israel for making political alliances with Assyria and Egypt for security instead of trusting Adonai — where might believers today face a similar temptation to trust human power over God, and how do we discern wisdom from unbelief?

theme: A Degenerate Vine and Unplowed Ground

questions.text: Hosea 9:10 recalls God finding Israel 'like grapes in the wilderness' at first, full of promise — what does the contrast with their later corruption at Baal-peor teach about how a good beginning can be squandered?

Hosea 10:1 calls Israel a 'luxuriant vine' (gefen boqeq) that produced fruit only for itself, multiplying altars as it prospered — what does this word picture reveal about the danger of prosperity without gratitude?

Hosea 10:12 calls Israel to 'sow for yourselves righteousness, reap chesed; break up your unplowed ground, for it is time to seek Adonai until He comes and rains righteousness on you' — how does this promise of a coming outpouring resonate with the two witnesses of Revelation 11, killed and raised in view of the nations?
Hosea 10:12's call to 'break up unplowed ground' suggests some soil in us needs fresh tilling before it can receive God's word — what unplowed area of your heart might need that this week?

Hosea repeatedly shows blessing turning into self-sufficiency and idolatry — is prosperity itself a spiritual danger, or only the heart's response to it, and how should a community guard against this without rejecting good gifts from God?

theme: Out of Egypt I Called My Son

questions.text: Hosea 11:1-4 pictures God teaching Ephraim to walk like a toddler, holding him by the arms, drawing him with 'cords of a man, bands of love' — what does this tender parental imagery add to how we picture God's discipline?

Hosea 11:1 says, 'When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called My son' — this verse will later be applied to Yeshua's return from Egypt in Matthew 2; what does it mean that Israel's history and the Messiah's story are woven together this way?

Hosea 12:3-4 recalls Jacob wrestling with the angel at Peniel and prevailing through weeping and pleading — how does this ancestor's struggle for a blessing anticipate the woman and her offspring under dragon-attack in Revelation 12, where the seed ultimately prevails?
Hosea 11:8-9 shows God's heart 'recoiling' in compassion even as He must discipline — how might remembering God's reluctance to punish shape how you extend correction or grace to someone this week?

Hosea 11:1 is written about Israel's exodus from Egypt, yet Matthew quotes it about Yeshua's return from Egypt as a boy — what do you think it means for a prophecy to have both a historical fulfillment in Israel and a deeper fulfillment in the Messiah?

theme: O Death, Where Are Your Plagues? Return and Be Healed

questions.text: Hosea 13:3 compares Israel's fleeting devotion to morning mist, vanishing dew, wind-blown chaff, and smoke from a chimney — what do these four images have in common, and what do they warn against?

Hosea 13:14 declares, 'I will ransom them from the power of Sheol; I will redeem them from death. O death, where are your plagues? O Sheol, where is your destruction?' — how does this verse, later echoed in 1 Corinthians 15, frame death as an enemy already under notice of defeat?

Hosea 14:4-7 closes the book with Israel blossoming like a lily and rooted like Lebanon's cedars, a picture of resurrection life — how does this hope contrast with the beast rising from the sea and demanding worship in Revelation 13?
Hosea 14:1-2 calls Israel to 'return to Adonai... take words with you' — what specific words of confession or turning do you need to bring to God this week rather than vague guilt?

The book of Hosea ends, 'Whoever is wise, let him understand these things' — after fourteen chapters of Israel's unfaithfulness and God's relentless pursuit, what is the single truth about God's character you find hardest to fully believe, and why?

theme: Locusts, Torn Hearts, and a Coming Army

questions.text: Joel 1:4 lists four kinds of locusts devouring in succession — what does this relentless, wave-after-wave devastation communicate about the completeness of the judgment?
Joel 2:13 calls Israel to 'tear your hearts (qir'u levavkhem) and not your garments' — why does God specifically contrast an inward posture with the outward custom of tearing clothes in mourning?

Joel 2:23 promises 'the early rain and latter rain' restoring the years the locusts consumed — how does this agricultural image of restored harvest anticipate the harvest imagery of Revelation 14, where the earth is reaped by the Son of Man?

Joel 2:12-13 calls for return 'with fasting, weeping, and mourning,' describing God as 'gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in chesed' — what would genuine, wholehearted repentance look like for you this week?

Joel describes the locust plague as both a natural disaster and 'the day of the LORD' — how should believers today think about the relationship between calamity, judgment, and God's sovereignty without blaming every disaster on personal sin or ignoring God's hand in history?

theme: I Will Pour Out My Ruach on All Flesh

questions.text: Joel 3:1-2 promises the Ruach poured out on 'sons and daughters,' 'old men and young men,' even 'menservants and maidservants' — what does this remarkably broad list say about who God intends to include in this outpouring?

Joel 4:2 names the 'Valley of Jehoshaphat' (Yehoshafat, meaning 'Adonai judges') as the place of final judgment on the nations — how does the meaning of that name itself become the message of the passage?

Kefa quotes Joel's promise of the Ruach outpoured at Shavuot in Acts 2 — how does that Pentecost fulfillment connect to Revelation 15's 'song of Moses and of the Lamb,' sung by those who conquered the beast, uniting Exodus deliverance with Messiah's final victory?
Joel 4:14 says, 'Multitudes, multitudes in the valley of decision' — what decision is God asking you to make more decisively this week rather than remaining in the crowd of the undecided?

Joel's promise that 'everyone who calls on the name of Adonai will be delivered' sits alongside a vision of harsh judgment on nations gathered against Israel — how do we hold together God's offer of salvation to 'all flesh' with His particular judgment on nations that oppose His people?

theme: For Three Transgressions, and for Four

questions.text: Amos 1-2 repeats 'for three transgressions... and for four' against Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and Judah before turning on Israel — why might Amos structure his oracle so his audience nods along at judgment on their neighbors first?

Amos 2:6-7 condemns Israel for selling 'the righteous for silver, and the needy for a pair of sandals' — what does this specific, almost trivial price reveal about how casually justice was being sold?

Amos's oracles against the nations culminating in judgment on God's own people anticipate Revelation 16's bowls of wrath, poured out with similar comprehensiveness — how do both passages insist that no nation, including the covenant people, stands outside accountability to Adonai?
Amos 2:6-8 pairs idolatry with exploitation of the poor — where might your own comfort be built, even indirectly, on injustice to someone else, and what small correction could you make this week?

Amos, a shepherd from Judah, is sent to prophesy judgment against the northern kingdom of Israel — what does it mean for God to send an 'outsider' to confront His people, and how should communities today respond to prophetic critique from unexpected voices?

theme: Cows of Bashan and a God Who Warns Before He Acts

questions.text: Amos 3:3 asks, 'Do two walk together unless they have agreed?' as the first in a series of cause-and-effect questions — how does this build-up prepare the reader for the conclusion that Adonai does nothing without revealing it to His prophets?
Amos 4:1 mocks wealthy women of Samaria as 'cows of Bashan' who oppress the poor and crush the needy — why would this fertile, well-fed region become shorthand for careless indulgence?

Amos 4:12's warning 'prepare to meet your God' and Amos 3's insistence that judgment never comes without warning both resonate with Revelation 17's unveiling of 'Babylon the Great' — how do both passages show God exposing corrupt systems before they fall?
Amos 4:6-11 lists five judgments Israel ignored, 'yet you did not return to Me' — what warning signs in your own life might you be explaining away instead of heeding?

Amos indicts Samaria's elite for indulgence on the backs of a poor they never notice — how do systemic injustice and personal comfort intertwine today, and what does genuine repentance from that kind of blindness require of a comfortable community?

theme: Let Justice Roll Down, Woe to Those at Ease

questions.text: Amos 5:24 pictures justice 'rolling down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream' — why an image of overwhelming, unstoppable water rather than something gentle?
Amos 6:1 pronounces 'woe' (hoy) on those 'at ease in Zion' who feel secure — how does this prophetic hoy function almost like a funeral cry pronounced in advance, rather than a simple warning?

Amos 6:4-6 condemns those who 'lie on beds of ivory' while ignoring coming ruin, a picture strikingly similar to Revelation 18's Babylon whose merchants mourn their lost luxury — what do both texts say about misplaced security in wealth?

Amos 5:21-24 has God rejecting Israel's festivals and songs because justice was absent from daily life — what would it look like this week to make sure your worship and your treatment of others actually match?

Amos 5:18-20 warns that people were longing for 'the day of the LORD' assuming it would only bring them vindication, when in fact it would expose them too — where might we today assume God's coming judgment will fall only on others?

theme: A Plumb Line and a Basket of Summer Fruit

questions.text: In Amos 7:7-9, God shows Amos a plumb line held against a wall — what does this image of precise, unbending measurement communicate about how Israel's crookedness will be judged?
Amos 8:1-2 plays on the Hebrew words qayits ('summer fruit') and qets ('the end') — how does this wordplay turn an ordinary basket of fruit into an announcement that Israel's end has come?

Amos 7:10-17 shows the priest Amaziah trying to silence Amos at the royal sanctuary of Bethel — how does this conflict between true prophetic word and institutional power anticipate Revelation 19's vision of the Word of God riding out to judge and make war against every false authority?
Amaziah tells Amos to go prophesy elsewhere and stop troubling Bethel's status quo — where might you be tempted to silence an uncomfortable truth rather than let it correct you?

Amos 8:11 warns of 'a famine... of hearing the words of Adonai' — what would it look like for a society, or a person, to be spiritually starving for God's word even while surrounded by information, and how do we recognize that famine in ourselves?

theme: The Fallen Sukkah of David Restored

questions.text: Amos 9:11 promises, 'In that day I will raise up the fallen sukkah of David' — what does calling David's dynasty a fragile, temporary shelter rather than a permanent palace suggest about its condition by Amos's time?
Obadiah condemns Edom, descendants of Esau, for standing aloof and even joining violence 'against your brother Jacob' — why does the word 'brother' (ach) make Edom's betrayal especially bitter?

Amos 9:11-12's promise to restore David's fallen sukkah is quoted by Ya'akov at the Jerusalem council in Acts 15 to explain Gentile inclusion — how does this connect to Revelation 20's vision of the thousand-year reign, where Messiah's kingdom finally fulfills what David's house pointed toward?
Obadiah warns against gloating over a relative's or rival's downfall — is there someone whose misfortune you've quietly enjoyed, and what would genuine care for them look like instead this week?

Obadiah's judgment on Edom is entirely about how one nation treated another in a moment of vulnerability — how much should 'how you treated the vulnerable when they were down' matter in how we evaluate nations, individuals, or even our own past choices?

theme: Fleeing to Tarshish, Prayer from the Deep

questions.text: Jonah flees toward Tarshish, roughly the opposite direction of Nineveh, and the pagan sailors end up more responsive to Adonai than the prophet himself — what does this reversal of roles teach about who truly 'knows' God in the story?

Jonah 2:1-9 records his prayer from within the fish, quoting phrases across many psalms about descending to the depths and Sheol — why would a prayer built from Israel's existing prayer-language, rather than original words, be exactly what Jonah needed in that moment?

Yeshua later cites Jonah's three days and nights in the fish as 'the sign of Jonah' pointing to His own death and resurrection — how might Jonah's rescue from the deep also anticipate Revelation 21's declaration that 'there was no longer any sea' in the new creation?
Jonah's flight shows that we can try to run from a calling we find distasteful — what assignment or conversation might you be avoiding because, like Jonah, you don't want it to succeed?
