Jonah prays a beautiful psalm of thanksgiving from the fish's belly before he ever repents of his disobedience or changes his attitude toward Nineveh — what does this tell us about the difference between crying out to God in distress and actually surrendering our will to Him?

theme: Nineveh Repents, Jonah's Withered Plant

questions.text: Jonah 3:5-9 shows the entire city of Nineveh, from the king down to the animals, responding to a brief sermon with sackcloth and fasting — why might such a massive response to so short a message be striking?

Jonah 4:6 describes a qiqayon plant that God appoints to give Jonah shade, then a worm to destroy it — how does this small, almost comic detail set up God's larger point about Jonah's misplaced compassion?

Jonah 4:2 reveals Jonah fled originally because he knew God is 'gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in chesed, and relenting from calamity' — how does this same divine compassion for a pagan, violent city anticipate Revelation 22's invitation, 'let the one who is thirsty come,' extended to all who will drink the water of life?
God asks Jonah, 'Should I not have compassion on Nineveh?' — what group of people might you, like Jonah, resist wanting God to show mercy toward?

The book of Jonah ends on an open question, with God challenging Jonah's anger rather than resolving it neatly — why might Scripture choose to end this story unresolved, and what does that literary choice ask of the reader?

theme: Lament Over the Cities, Hope of a Breaker

questions.text: Micah 1:10-15 uses wordplay on the names of towns in Judah's Shephelah, like Lachish and Moresheth-gath, to pronounce judgment — what does tailoring the message to specific local geography suggest about how personally these judgments were meant to land?

Micah 2:12-13 promises a 'Breaker' (poretz) who goes up before the people, breaking through the gate with 'their king passing before them, Adonai at their head' — how does this term evoke someone who clears the way for others to follow?

Matthew 1 opens with Yeshua's genealogy rooting Him in David's line, born through unlikely circumstances involving Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba — how does Micah's picture of a coming 'Breaker/king' who leads a scattered remnant find its answer in this Messiah descended through such an unlikely lineage?

Micah 2:1-2 condemns those who 'devise iniquity on their beds' and then act on it in daylight, coveting fields and seizing them — what plans do you turn over in quiet moments that you would be ashamed to have others see?

Matthew's genealogy deliberately includes women with complicated, even scandalous histories in Yeshua's lineage — what does their inclusion suggest about how God works through imperfect, non-idealized family stories rather than only spotless ones?

theme: Swords into Plowshares, Nations Streaming to Zion

questions.text: Micah 3:9-11 condemns Jerusalem's leaders, priests, and prophets for taking bribes while still claiming, 'Is not Adonai among us? No disaster will come upon us' — what is dangerous about religious confidence detached from actual obedience?

Micah 4:3 envisions nations who 'will beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks' — why does turning weapons into farm tools capture the reversal of the Messianic age so vividly?

Micah 4:1-2 pictures many nations streaming up to the mountain of the LORD's house to be taught His ways — how does this anticipate the Gentile Magi from the East in Matthew 2, coming to worship the newborn King in Bethlehem while Jerusalem itself was troubled?

Micah 4:4 promises a day when 'each man will sit under his vine and under his fig tree, and no one will make them afraid' — what fear are you carrying this week that you need to bring under the promise of God's coming peace?

In Matthew 2, it is pagan astrologers who recognize the King while Herod and Jerusalem's religious leaders react with fear or indifference — what does it look like today when those 'outside' recognize God's work more readily than those who should know better?

theme: Bethlehem's Ruler, and What Adonai Requires

questions.text: Micah 5:2 names Bethlehem Ephrathah as too small among the clans of Judah, yet the source of Israel's coming ruler — what does God choosing an overlooked town suggest about how He often works?
Micah 6:8 summarizes what Adonai requires: 'to do justly (mishpat), love chesed, and walk humbly with your God' — how do these three commands work together rather than as separate checkboxes?

Micah 5:2 describes this ruler's origin as 'from of old, from ancient days,' pointing to more than a merely human king — how does this connect to Matthew 3's account of Yeshua's baptism, where the Father's voice from heaven and the Ruach descending like a dove confirm His unique identity?

Micah 6:6-8 rejects the idea that God is impressed by lavish offerings instead of a changed, humble life — where might you be tempted to substitute a grand gesture for the harder work of justice, mercy, and humility?

John the Baptist calls the religious leaders a 'brood of vipers' and insists that being children of Abraham isn't enough without 'fruit worthy of repentance' — how does this challenge apply to anyone who relies on inherited identity or status rather than a transformed life?

theme: Who Is a God Like You? Nineveh's Reckoning

questions.text: Micah 7:18 asks, 'Who is a God like You, pardoning iniquity?' — a phrase that plays on the prophet's own name, Micah, meaning 'who is like the LORD?' — how does the prophet's own name become the theme of his closing words?

Nahum 1:15 declares, 'Behold, on the mountains the feet of him who brings good news, who proclaims shalom!' — how does this joyful herald image contrast with the violent judgment on Nineveh that follows in the rest of Nahum?

Micah 7:19 pictures God casting Israel's sins 'into the depths of the sea,' while Matthew 4:23 describes Yeshua 'proclaiming the good news of the kingdom' throughout Galilee — how do both texts frame God's mercy as something actively announced and enacted, not just felt?

Micah 7:7 says, 'As for me, I will look to Adonai; I will wait for the God of my salvation,' even amid betrayal by close family and neighbors — who or what circumstance this week calls you to that same posture of hopeful waiting rather than despair?

Nahum announces judgment on Nineveh generations after that same city repented under Jonah's preaching — what does the fact that a nation can repent, be spared, and later fall back into judgment teach about repentance being an ongoing posture rather than a one-time event?

theme: The Bloody City, and How Long, O LORD?

questions.text: Nahum 3:1 calls Nineveh 'the city of blood, full of lies and plunder' and describes its fall in vivid detail — why might God allow a prophet's tone to sound satisfied at an oppressor's downfall?

Habakkuk 1:2-4 opens with the prophet crying, 'How long (ad-anah), O LORD, will I call for help and You will not listen?' — how does this direct complaint to God model an honest, even confrontational form of prayer?

Habakkuk wrestles with God using a wicked nation, Babylon, to punish a less-wicked one, Judah, a tension left unresolved in chapter 1 — how does Matthew 5's Sermon on the Mount, especially 'blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness,' speak to that same longing for a justice not yet fully realized?
Habakkuk brings his confusion and frustration directly to God rather than suppressing it — what unresolved complaint could you bring honestly to God in prayer this week instead of avoiding it?

Habakkuk's core problem is that God's chosen method of judgment, using Babylon, seems more troubling than the sin it punishes — have you ever faced a situation where God's apparent solution seemed as hard to accept as the problem itself, and how did you work through it?

theme: The Righteous Live by Faith; Yet I Will Rejoice

questions.text: Habakkuk 2:2 has God tell the prophet to 'write the vision, make it plain on tablets, so that a runner may read it' — what does the emphasis on clarity and urgency suggest about how God's answers are meant to be shared, not hoarded?

Habakkuk 2:4 declares, 'the righteous will live by his faith/faithfulness (emunato)' — a verse later central to Sha'ul's letters to the Romans and Galatians — how does emunah carry both belief and steady faithfulness in a single word?

Habakkuk 3:17-18 resolves the book's tension not by removing hardship but with, 'though the fig tree does not blossom... yet I will rejoice in Adonai' — how does this directly parallel Yeshua's teaching in Matthew 6:25-34 not to worry about tomorrow but to seek first the kingdom of God?
Habakkuk's declaration of joy comes before circumstances change, purely as an act of trust — what circumstance in your life needs that same kind of anticipatory trust and rejoicing this week?

Habakkuk 2 pronounces a series of 'woes' on the proud oppressor while conceding 'the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of Adonai' — how do present injustice and the certainty of God's ultimate purposes coexist in this chapter, and what should that teach us about patience?

theme: The Great Day of the LORD Draws Near

questions.text: Zephaniah 1:4-5 lists specific idolatrous practices — worship of Baal, the 'host of heaven' on rooftops, swearing by Malcam — that God will sweep away from Judah; what does this specificity suggest about how thoroughly idolatry had blended into ordinary life?

Zephaniah 1:14-15 repeatedly calls it 'the day of the LORD' (Yom Adonai), a day of wrath, distress, darkness, and gloom — why might the prophet stack up so many near-synonymous terms rather than a single description?

Zephaniah 2:3 calls the humble of the land to 'seek Adonai... seek righteousness, seek humility; perhaps you will be hidden in the day of the LORD's anger' — how does this echo Matthew 7's warning that not everyone who calls Yeshua 'Lord, Lord' will enter the kingdom, but those who actually do the Father's will?

Zephaniah 2:3's 'perhaps you will be hidden' suggests urgency without presumption — what would it look like to seek God with that same earnestness this week rather than assuming you're automatically safe?

Matthew 7:24-27 contrasts the wise and foolish builders, both of whom heard the same words but responded differently — combined with Zephaniah's warnings, what do you think most determines whether someone truly builds their life 'on the rock' versus merely appearing to?

theme: He Will Rejoice Over You With Singing

questions.text: Haggai 1:4-6 confronts the returned exiles for living in 'paneled houses' while the Temple lies in ruins, noting they 'sow much and harvest little' — what connection does Haggai draw between misplaced priorities and unsatisfying results?

Zephaniah 3:17 says Adonai 'will rejoice over you with singing' — how does this picture of God singing over His people, rather than merely tolerating or forgiving them, change the emotional tone of the whole book's ending?

Zephaniah 3:17 declares 'Adonai your God is in your midst,' while Matthew 8 shows Yeshua actively present among the sick, the outcast, and the storm-tossed, healing a leper, a centurion's servant, and calming the sea — how do both texts picture God's nearness as active, not distant?
Haggai 1:5-7 repeats 'consider your ways' as a call to honest self-examination about priorities — what neglected priority in your own life deserves that same honest reconsideration this week?

The returned exiles in Haggai 1 assumed the time wasn't right to rebuild the Temple, while their own homes were finished and comfortable — how do we recognize when 'the time isn't right yet' is genuine discernment versus a convenient excuse for avoiding what God has actually asked of us?

theme: The Latter Glory, and a Call to Return

questions.text: Haggai 2:3 asks the older exiles who remembered Solomon's Temple whether this rebuilt version doesn't look like 'nothing' in comparison — what does God's response in 2:9, that 'the latter glory of this house will be greater than the former,' suggest about how He measures glory differently than we do?

Zechariah 1:3 opens with God's appeal, 'Return to Me... and I will return to you,' using shuv, 'to turn/return,' twice in a single verse — why might this reciprocal turning be central to how the prophet frames Israel's relationship with God after exile?

Haggai 2:6-7 promises Adonai will 'shake the heavens and the earth' until 'the desire of all nations will come,' filling the house with glory — how does Matthew 9's picture of Yeshua forgiving sins, healing diseases, and raising Jairus's daughter show a 'glory' filling ordinary lives that the rebuilt Temple alone could not provide?

Zechariah 1:3's call to 'return' assumes the exiles were still somewhat distant from God even after coming back to the land — what would it look like for you to make an intentional, specific 'return' to God this week rather than assuming proximity equals closeness?

Matthew 9:9-13 shows Yeshua calling Matthew, a tax collector, and eating with 'sinners,' provoking criticism from the Pharisees — how should a community balance holiness and welcome the way Yeshua does here, and where do modern faith communities tend to get that balance wrong?

theme: The Apple of His Eye, Filthy Garments Removed

questions.text: Zechariah 2:8 (Heb. 2:12) says whoever touches Israel 'touches the apple of His eye' — what does this vivid, protective image reveal about God's personal stake in His people's fate?

Zechariah 3 shows Joshua the high priest standing in filthy garments before the accusing Satan, then having them removed and replaced with pure garments — why would priestly clothing, of all images, be chosen to depict this cleansing?

Zechariah 3:8 introduces 'My servant, the Branch (Tzemach),' a Messianic title for one who removes iniquity 'in a single day' — how does this connect to Matthew 10, where Yeshua sends His talmidim out with authority to heal and cast out unclean spirits, extending His own cleansing work through them?

Joshua's filthy garments are removed not because he earned it but because 'the LORD has taken away your iniquity' — what would it look like to actually receive that kind of unearned cleansing this week rather than trying to clean yourself up first?

Matthew 10:34-39 has Yeshua warning that following Him may set family members against each other and requires taking up a cross — how do we reconcile this hard saying with the promise, seen throughout the prophets, of God's shalom and restoration for His people?

theme: Not by Might, but by My Ruach

questions.text: Zechariah 4 shows a golden menorah flanked by two olive trees continually supplying it with oil — what does this self-sustaining source of light suggest about where Israel's strength for rebuilding was meant to come from?

Zechariah 4:6 declares, 'Not by might, nor by power, but by My Ruach, says Adonai,' spoken directly to Zerubbabel about finishing the Temple against great obstacles — how does this phrase reframe what 'power' actually means in God's work?

In Matthew 11:2-6, John the Baptist sends disciples from prison to ask if Yeshua is 'the one who is to come,' and Yeshua answers by pointing to His works, the blind see, the lame walk, rather than a title — how does this echo Zechariah 4:6's principle that God's power is proven through action empowered by the Ruach, not through force or self-assertion?

Zechariah 5's vision of the flying scroll and the woman in the basket, 'Wickedness,' being carried away pictures sin being actively removed from the land — what specific sin or pattern do you need to actively carry away rather than just manage this week?

Matthew 11:28-30 offers rest and an 'easy yoke' right after Yeshua pronounces woes on unrepentant towns like Chorazin and Bethsaida — how do warning and invitation function together in Yeshua's ministry, and why might one not be complete without the other?

theme: Crowning the Branch, True Fasting

questions.text: Zechariah 6:9-13 has the prophet crown Joshua the high priest as a living sign of 'the Branch' who will 'build the temple of Adonai' and 'sit and rule on His throne,' combining priest and king in one figure — why is this combination of offices so unusual in Israel's history?

Zechariah 7:5-6 asks whether the people's fasting during the exile was really 'for Me' or simply 'for yourselves' — how does this challenge the assumption that religious discipline automatically pleases God?

Zechariah 6's picture of one figure holding both royal and priestly authority anticipates Matthew 12, where Yeshua declares Himself 'Lord of the Sabbath' and says 'something greater than the Temple is here' — how do both texts point toward an authority beyond what any single Israelite office had held before?

Zechariah 7:9-10 summarizes true worship as rendering true justice, showing chesed and compassion, and not oppressing the widow, orphan, stranger, or poor — which of these specific commands most needs your attention this week?

Matthew 12:1-14 shows Yeshua defending His talmidim's Sabbath grain-picking and healing on the Sabbath by appealing to mercy and human need — how do we discern, in our own convictions today, the difference between guarding a genuinely sacred practice and using rules to avoid showing mercy?

theme: Your King Comes on a Donkey, and Kingdom Parables

questions.text: Zechariah 8:4-5 pictures Jerusalem's streets full of old men and women leaning on canes and children playing — why would such an ordinary, unspectacular scene of daily life be the very image of restoration God chooses?

Zechariah 9:9 announces, 'Rejoice greatly, Daughter of Zion!... your king comes to you, righteous and endowed with salvation, humble (ani) and riding on a donkey' — how does the choice of a donkey rather than a war horse embody this king's humility?

Zechariah 9:9 is directly fulfilled when Yeshua enters Jerusalem on a donkey, and its vision of a coming humble king pairs with Matthew 13's parables describing what that King's kingdom is actually like — small as a mustard seed, hidden as leaven, worth more than any treasure?

Zechariah 8:16-17 calls the restored community to 'speak the truth to one another,' render true judgment, and not 'devise evil against your neighbor in your heart' — which of these practical instructions could most reshape your relationships this week?

Matthew 13:10-15 explains that Yeshua taught in parables partly so that only those genuinely seeking would understand, quoting Isaiah about people who hear but do not perceive — why would God choose to reveal truth in a way that simultaneously conceals it from the indifferent, and is that fair?

theme: The Good Shepherd and the Worthless One

questions.text: In Zechariah 11 the prophet acts out being a shepherd hired by the flock's own merchants — notice what happens to his two staffs, No'am ('Favor') and Chovlim ('Union'), and what their breaking signifies for Judah and Israel.

Zechariah 11:12-13 records thirty pieces of silver thrown 'to the potter' in the house of Adonai — what does this specific, almost throwaway detail suggest about how Zechariah's generation valued their true shepherd?

Zechariah condemns a 'worthless shepherd' who does not care for the flock (11:15-17), while Matthew 14 shows both a failed shepherd of Israel (Herod, who beheads John) and Yeshua, who has compassion on the crowds and feeds them himself — what does the contrast reveal about true and false leadership?
Where in your own life have you settled for a 'worthless shepherd' — a source of guidance or comfort that ultimately cannot feed or protect you?

Zechariah's shepherd is paid an insultingly small wage, yet Matthew records that this very sum was later used to betray the Messiah — discuss what it means that Scripture's fulfillment can run through such an ordinary, even sordid, transaction.

theme: A Pierced One Mourned, a Fountain Opened

questions.text: Zechariah 12:10 describes 'the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem' looking on someone 'whom they have pierced' and mourning as for an only child — notice who does the piercing and who does the mourning in this verse.

Zechariah 13:1 promises a fountain (maqor) opened for 'the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem' for sin (chatat) and impurity (niddah) — what do these two specific terms together suggest about the scope of the cleansing pictured?

Yeshua tells the Canaanite woman in Matthew 15 that he was 'sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel,' yet grants her request anyway — how does this scene anticipate Zechariah's vision of a cleansing fountain and a mourning that eventually draws in more than Jerusalem's own household?
Zechariah 13:9 describes a remnant refined 'as silver is refined' — what is one area of your life Adonai may currently be refining through difficulty?

In Matthew 15 Yeshua redefines defilement as coming from the heart rather than unwashed hands — discuss how this connects to, or differs from, Zechariah's language of a fountain that cleanses from sin and impurity.

theme: One King, One Name, and Peter's Confession

questions.text: Zechariah 14 describes living waters flowing out from Jerusalem toward both the eastern sea and the western sea — notice what this detail suggests about the reach and permanence of Adonai's future reign from that specific city.

Zechariah 14:9 says 'Adonai will be echad, and His name echad' — the same word used in the Shema for God's oneness — what might it mean for that oneness to be established 'over all the earth' on that day?

Malachi 1:11 declares that Adonai's name will be great 'from the rising of the sun to its setting' among the nations, and in Matthew 16 Kefa confesses Yeshua as 'the Messiah, the Son of the living God' — how do these two declarations, one about Adonai's name and one about the Messiah's identity, belong together?
Malachi 1 rebukes priests for offering Adonai blemished, leftover sacrifices — what would it look like this week to give God your first and best rather than your leftovers?

Right after Kefa's confession, Yeshua announces he must suffer and be killed — discuss why recognizing Yeshua as Messiah and King, as in Zechariah 14's vision of Adonai as King over all the earth, had to be immediately followed by the announcement of the cross.

theme: The Messenger, Eliyahu, and the Refiner's Fire

questions.text: Malachi 2 condemns priests who 'corrupted the covenant of Levi' and dealt treacherously with the wives of their youth — notice how Malachi ties faithfulness in worship directly to faithfulness in the most intimate human relationships.

Malachi 3:1 promises 'my messenger' (mal'ach) to prepare the way, and the chapter closes with the promise of Eliyahu the prophet before the great Day of Adonai — what does the shared 'messenger' role suggest about how these two figures relate?

On the mountain in Matthew 17, Moshe and Eliyahu appear with Yeshua, and afterward the talmidim ask about Eliyahu's coming — how does Yeshua's answer that 'Eliyahu has already come' connect directly back to Malachi's closing promise?
Malachi 3:10 invites Israel to 'test Me in this' regarding the tithe — what would genuine trust in Adonai's provision look like in your finances or resources this week?

Malachi pictures Adonai as a refiner's fire purifying the sons of Levi, and the Transfiguration shows Yeshua's face shining 'like the sun' — discuss what it means that glory and refining fire appear together in both texts, and what that suggests about the cost of holiness.

theme: The Accuser, Job's Integrity, and Childlike Trust

questions.text: In the heavenly scene of Job 1-2, notice exactly what ha-satan ('the accuser') is permitted to touch and what he is explicitly forbidden to touch in each round of testing.
The Hebrew tam, translated 'blameless' or 'of integrity,' describes Job in 1:1 and is exactly what the accuser challenges — what does it mean for integrity to be tested rather than merely assumed?

Job's friends sit with him in silent solidarity for seven days before a word is spoken (2:13), while Matthew 18 teaches about the seventy-times-seven forgiveness that holds a community together — how do both passages picture what genuine covenant community actually costs?

Job responds to catastrophic loss with worship: 'Adonai gave, and Adonai has taken away; blessed be the name of Adonai' (1:21) — what would it look like to bring that same posture to a current disappointment in your own life?

Job's wife tells him to 'curse God and die,' but Job replies, 'Shall we accept good from God, and not accept adversity?' — discuss whether this answer is the right response to suffering, or whether it can be misused to silence honest lament.

theme: Cursing the Day of Birth, Questioning Human Righteousness

questions.text: Notice the contrast between Job's raw lament in chapter 3, cursing the day of his birth, and Eliphaz's calm, confident theology in chapter 4 — what does this gap suggest about the distance between suffering and merely observing suffering?

Eliphaz asks in Job 4:17, 'Can a mortal be more righteous (tzadaq) than God? Can a man be more pure than his Maker?' — what assumption is Eliphaz already smuggling into this seemingly rhetorical question?

Job 4:17's question about human righteousness before God echoes in Matthew 19 when the rich young man asks what good deed he must do, and Yeshua replies that 'with man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible' — how do both passages point past self-achieved righteousness?
The rich young man in Matthew 19 walks away sorrowful because of his many possessions — what is one possession, ambition, or comfort Yeshua may be asking you to hold more loosely?

Job wishes he had never been born, yet the book never rebukes him for saying so — discuss what this suggests about how honest despair is treated in Scripture, especially compared to Eliphaz's tidy answers.

theme: Undependable Friends and an Unfailing Wage

questions.text: In Job 6:15-20, Job compares his friends to a wadi that runs dry in summer heat exactly when travelers need it most — notice how visual and specific this complaint is compared to a generic accusation of unfaithfulness.

Eliphaz says in 5:17, 'happy is the man whom Eloah corrects,' using a form of musar (discipline), a word that runs throughout Proverbs — how does its normal use in Proverbs 1 complicate or support Eliphaz's claim about Job's suffering?

The landowner in Matthew 20's parable pays every worker the same generous wage regardless of hours worked, and Yeshua explains he 'came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' (20:28) — how does this generosity answer the fickleness of Job's friends?
Job accuses his friends of failing him exactly when he needed them most — who in your life needs you to be a dependable presence rather than a dried-up wadi this week?

The workers hired first in Matthew 20 complain that latecomers received equal pay — discuss whether their complaint is fair, and how Job's friends' assumptions about who deserves what compare to the workers' assumptions.

theme: What Is Man, and Whose Praise Silences the Proud

questions.text: Job 7:17-18 asks, 'What is man, that you make so much of him... that you visit him every morning?' — notice how Job turns a phrase that elsewhere expresses wonder into a complaint about being relentlessly scrutinized.
Bildad in Job 8:11 asks, 'Can the papyrus grow up without a marsh? Can the reeds flourish without water?' — what does this image suggest about the fate he claims awaits anyone who forgets Adonai?

When children cry out 'Hoshia na to the Son of David' in the Temple, the chief priests object, and Yeshua answers with Psalm 8's language about praise from the mouths of infants (Matthew 21:16) — how does this reverse Job 7's anxious question about why God bothers with mere humans?

Yeshua curses the fig tree in Matthew 21 for having leaves but no fruit — what would honest self-examination reveal about the gap between your outward appearance of faith and its actual fruit this season?

Bildad insists that suffering proves guilt, using tidy nature imagery to make his case — discuss how the religious leaders in Matthew 21's parable of the tenants make a similarly tidy, self-serving argument, and how Yeshua responds to both kinds of reasoning.

theme: Job's Longing for a Mediator, David's Son and Lord

questions.text: In Job 9:32-33, Job says God is not a man that he could answer him in court, then longs for 'an umpire' (mokiach) who could 'lay his hand on us both' — notice exactly what qualification Job says this mediator would need.
The Hebrew mokiach in Job 9:33, sometimes translated 'arbiter,' names someone who stands between two unequal parties — where else in Scripture do you see this same need for a go-between filled?

Job longs for a mediator able to touch both him and God; in Matthew 22 Yeshua asks the Pharisees how the Messiah can be both David's son and David's Lord — how does this riddle describe exactly the kind of mediator Job was hoping for, fully human and yet able to stand before God?
Job feels he has no one to arbitrate his case before God — where do you need to remember that you already have such a mediator, and let that truth shape how you pray this week?
Job insists he could argue his case if only he had a fair hearing — discuss whether his confidence in his own righteousness is admirable honesty or something Job himself will later need to repent of.

theme: God Who Humbles the Mighty, Woes to the Proud

questions.text: Job 12:17-21 describes Adonai leading priests away stripped and overthrowing the mighty, kings, and trusted counselors — notice how many different categories of powerful people Job lists in just a few verses.

Zophar asks in Job 11:7, 'Can you find out the depths of Eloah? Can you find the limits of Shaddai?' — what does pairing these two names for God suggest about the kind of knowledge Zophar claims Job lacks?

Job describes Adonai humbling the proud and powerful, and in Matthew 23 Yeshua pronounces woes on scribes and Pharisees who exalt themselves, before lamenting over Jerusalem like a mother hen — how do both passages hold together God's judgment on pride and his longing to gather and restore?
Yeshua warns against loving the seat of honor and the title of 'Rabbi' for its own sake — where might you be seeking recognition this week instead of simply serving?

Job argues that he has as much wisdom as his friends, even while admitting God's understanding is unsearchable — discuss the difference between confident faith and prideful self-assurance, and how Matthew 23's Pharisees illustrate the danger of confusing the two.

theme: Though He Slay Me: Hope Beyond Death

questions.text: Job 13:15 declares, 'Though he slay me, I will hope in him,' right after Job says he will argue his case to God's face — notice how defiant confrontation and desperate hope sit side by side in the same breath.

Job 14:14 asks, 'If a man dies, shall he live again?' — the Hebrew chayah (to live) used here reappears throughout Scripture's resurrection hope — what does it mean that this question is left open rather than answered within the book of Job itself?

Job's unanswered question about life after death in chapter 14 anticipates the hope Yeshua describes in Matthew 24 concerning his own return and the gathering of the elect — how does the certainty of the Messiah's coming answer the uncertainty in Job's cry?
Yeshua warns in Matthew 24 to stay alert since 'no one knows the day or the hour' — what specific habit could help you live watchfully this week rather than complacently?

Eliphaz's second speech in Job 15 assumes Job's suffering must stem from hidden arrogance — discuss how this same instinct to explain disaster as deserved judgment shows up in how people sometimes interpret the 'wars and rumors of wars' Yeshua describes in Matthew 24.

theme: A Witness in Heaven, a Coming Judgment

questions.text: In Job 16:19-20, Job says, 'Even now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and he who vouches for me is on high,' right after describing his friends as scorners — notice where Job turns for vindication when human comfort fails entirely.
The Hebrew sahed (witness) and melitz (the one who intercedes) in Job 16:19-20 describe someone advocating for Job before God — what kind of legal or covenantal relationship do these words imply?

Job appeals to a heavenly witness and advocate even as his earthly friends fail him, while Matthew 25's parable of the sheep and goats reveals the Son of Man as the one who will finally judge and vindicate 'the least of these' — how does the Judge of Matthew 25 answer Job's longing for someone to vouch for him?

The sheep in Matthew 25 are commended for concrete acts of mercy done to the hungry, the stranger, and the imprisoned — what specific, tangible act of mercy could you do for someone this week without expecting recognition?

Job feels mocked and abandoned by both friends and God in chapter 17, yet still speaks of a witness in heaven — discuss how genuine faith can hold despair and hope together, and how the wise and foolish virgins in Matthew 25 illustrate the danger of losing that hope through inattentiveness.

theme: My Redeemer Lives

questions.text: In Job 19:13-19, Job lists everyone who has turned against him, from brothers to close friends to his own household staff — notice how this list of abandonment builds right before his declaration of hope in verses 25-27.

Job 19:25 declares, 'I know that my Go'el (Redeemer) lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth' — what does the term go'el, elsewhere used for a kinsman who buys back land or avenges a family member, add to Job's confession?

Job's confession of a living Redeemer who will one day stand on the earth resonates with the Passover meal Yeshua shares with his talmidim in Matthew 26, where he speaks of his blood 'poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins' — how does the Redeemer Job hoped for become concrete at this table?

Job is betrayed and abandoned by those closest to him, just as Yeshua is betrayed by Judah (Judas) and denied by Kefa in Matthew 26 — where do you need to extend forgiveness this week toward someone who has failed you?

Job insists 'in my flesh I will see God' even while everyone around him has turned away — discuss what it costs to maintain hope in bodily resurrection or vindication when present circumstances offer no evidence for it, as Yeshua does in Gethsemane.

theme: The Prosperity of the Wicked, the Suffering of the Righteous

questions.text: Job 21:7-13 asks bluntly why the wicked 'live, grow old, and increase in power,' directly contradicting Zophar's confident claim in chapter 20 that the wicked's triumph is brief — notice how directly Job argues with the speech right before his own.

Matthew 27:9-10 cites 'thirty pieces of silver' and a potter's field, language drawn from Zechariah 11 — what does it mean that Matthew sees this specific, almost incidental economic detail fulfilled in Judah's betrayal money?

Job's protest that the wicked often prosper while the righteous suffer finds its starkest answer at the cross in Matthew 27, where the truly righteous one is condemned by a corrupt process while a guilty man, Bar-Abba, goes free — how does this scene both confirm and transform Job's complaint?
Job refuses to accept easy answers about suffering and justice — what current situation in your life or the world tempts you toward a tidy explanation that Scripture itself resists?

Job 21 essentially refutes his friends' entire theology by pointing to observable reality, yet the book does not fully resolve why the wicked prosper — discuss how the crucifixion in Matthew 27 both intensifies this problem and offers a different kind of answer than a philosophical one.

theme: Tested as Gold, Raised in Glory

questions.text: Job 23:3 says, 'Oh that I knew where I might find him, that I might come even to his seat!' — notice how Job's complaint has shifted from arguing his case to simply longing for God's presence.

Job 23:10 declares, 'when he has tried me, I shall come forth as gold' — the Hebrew bachan (tested) describes a refining process — how does this word reframe suffering as purposeful rather than meaningless?

Job longs to find God and trusts that testing will end in gold-like vindication; Matthew 28 shows exactly this pattern fulfilled as Yeshua, tested to the point of death, comes forth from the tomb in resurrection glory — how does the empty tomb answer Job's unresolved longing?

The resurrected Yeshua commissions the talmidim with the promise 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (28:20) — how does this promise address whatever version of Job's 'oh that I knew where to find him' you are currently wrestling with?

Eliphaz's third speech in Job 22 falsely accuses Job of specific social sins he never committed — discuss the danger of assuming we know why someone is suffering, and how the guards' false report in Matthew 28 shows a similar willingness to construct a convenient lie rather than face the truth.

theme: Can Mortals Be Righteous? The Beloved Son Answers

questions.text: Job 24 catalogs specific injustices, boundary markers moved, the poor stripped of clothing, orphans' donkeys driven away — notice how concrete and social these complaints are compared to the abstract theology of Job's friends.

Bildad's final, brief speech in Job 25:4-6 asks, 'How can a man be righteous (tzadaq) before God... how much less man, who is a maggot?' — what effect does ending on such a degrading image have on his argument?

Bildad's question of how any mortal could be righteous before God is answered at the Jordan in Mark 1, where a voice from heaven declares over Yeshua, 'You are my beloved Son, in you I am well pleased' — how does this declaration address, rather than dismiss, the human need Bildad describes?
Mark 1 shows Yeshua rising early to pray alone before a busy day of ministry — what would it look like to build that same rhythm of solitary prayer into your own routine this week?

Job describes real, ongoing injustice against the poor and vulnerable that seems to go unpunished — discuss how Yeshua's early ministry in Mark 1, focused on healing and casting out unclean spirits among ordinary people, begins to answer that kind of injustice in practice.

theme: Integrity Maintained, Sins Forgiven

questions.text: Job 26:7 describes God stretching 'the north over empty space' and hanging 'the earth on nothing' — notice how this ancient description of creation anticipates something modern readers recognize about the earth suspended in space.

Job 27:5-6 has Job insist, 'Till I die I will not put away my integrity (tummah) from me' — how does this word, related to tam from chapter 1, show Job's consistency of character even under his friends' pressure?

Job clings to his own righteousness as his only defense, while in Mark 2 the paralytic's friends bring him to Yeshua, who simply declares, 'Son, your sins are forgiven,' before the man does anything to prove his integrity — how does grace offered before any proof of righteousness change the terms of the conversation Job is having?
The scribes in Mark 2 privately question Yeshua's authority to forgive sins — where might you be withholding forgiveness from yourself or someone else because it feels too easily given?

Job insists he will not abandon his claim to innocence even to his last breath, while Mark 2 shows Levi abandoning his old life the moment Yeshua calls him — discuss the difference between defending your own righteousness and simply receiving a new calling.

theme: Where Wisdom Is Found, and Where It Is Twisted

questions.text: Job 28 describes miners tunneling for silver, gold, and precious stones in the darkest places on earth, only to conclude that wisdom cannot be found there — notice the contrast between the difficulty of mining metal and the simplicity of the chapter's final answer.

Job 28:28 concludes, 'the fear of Adonai (yirat Adonai), that is wisdom, and to turn away from evil is understanding' — how does this definition compare to how 'wisdom' is normally pursued elsewhere in Job's world, through mining, status, or counsel?

Job 28 insists true wisdom is the fear of Adonai, not intellectual achievement, while in Mark 3 the learned scribes attribute the Ruach ha-Kodesh's work in Yeshua to Beelzebul — how does their failure illustrate exactly the corrupted 'wisdom' Job 28 warns against?
Job 29 recalls his former life of honor and influence at the city gate — what would it look like to use whatever influence or respect you currently have to serve the poor and needy, as Job once did?

Mark 3 records Yeshua's own family thinking he was 'out of his mind,' right before he redefines family as 'whoever does the will of God' — discuss how Job's grief over his lost status in chapter 29 compares to the cost Yeshua and his followers pay for pursuing true wisdom over social approval.

theme: Reversal, Integrity, and the One Who Commands the Storm

questions.text: Job 30 describes being mocked by men younger than himself, men he says he would not have set with the dogs of his flock — notice how sharply this reverses the honor Job describes having in chapter 29.

Job 31:1 states, 'I made a covenant (berit) with my eyes; how then could I gaze at a virgin?' — what does using covenant language, normally reserved for relationship with God, suggest about how seriously Job treats even a private, internal commitment?

Job's long oath of innocence in chapter 31 ends with him longing for someone to hear his case, and in Mark 4 the talmidim, terrified in a storm, ask, 'Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?' — how does the one who calms the storm answer the kind of vindication Job has been seeking?
Job's covenant with his eyes in 31:1 is a proactive commitment made before temptation arises — what is one boundary you could set in advance this week rather than relying on willpower in the moment?

Job's oath in chapter 31 lists an extensive catalogue of sins he has avoided, essentially building a legal defense of his own righteousness — discuss whether this kind of self-examination is healthy spiritual practice or a subtle form of self-justification, and how it compares to simply trusting the one who commands the sea.

theme: A Mediator, a Ransom, and Restoration from Death

questions.text: Job 32 introduces Elihu, described as younger than the other three and angry both at Job and at the friends — notice how the text explains why Elihu waited so long to finally speak.

Elihu describes a possible 'mediator, one out of a thousand' who could tell a person what is right, so that God is gracious and says, 'Redeem him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom (kopher)' (Job 33:23-24) — what does this specific word, a price paid to substitute for a life, anticipate?

Elihu's picture of a mediator who secures a ransom from the pit is dramatically enacted in Mark 5, where Yeshua raises Jairus's daughter from apparent death and restores a woman after twelve years of suffering — how do these healings embody the ransom-and-restoration Elihu describes only in theory?

The woman with the flow of blood in Mark 5 reaches out in faith after twelve years of suffering and searching for help — what long-standing need are you tempted to stop bringing to God, and what would it look like to reach out one more time?

Elihu claims fresh insight the older friends lacked, yet much of what he says echoes their arguments — discuss how to discern genuine new understanding from simply repackaged old assumptions, whether in Job's friends or in our own certainty about why God allows suffering.

theme: Defending God's Justice, a Prophet Without Honor

questions.text: Job 34:10-12 has Elihu insist, 'far be it from God to do wickedness,' arguing God renders to each person according to his deeds — notice how confidently Elihu speaks compared to how tentatively Job has spoken about God's justice.

Elihu asks in Job 35:6-7, 'If you sin, what do you accomplish against Him?... If you are righteous, what do you give Him?' — what does his emphasis that human righteousness benefits other humans, not God directly, suggest about the purpose of ethical living?

Elihu insists God cannot act unjustly, yet Mark 6 shows the unjust beheading of John the Baptist by Herod and Yeshua being dishonored in his own hometown of Natzeret — how do these events press against Elihu's confident theology, even as the same chapter's feeding of the five thousand shows God's provision continuing regardless?

Yeshua sends out the Twelve in Mark 6 with minimal provisions, relying on hospitality along the way — what would it look like for you to trust God's provision more and your own preparation less in a specific task this week?

Mark 6 records that Yeshua 'could do no mighty work' in Natzeret because of the people's unbelief — discuss what this suggests about the relationship between human response and divine action, and how it complicates Elihu's simple cause-and-effect theology in Job 34-35.

theme: God's Word Versus Human Tradition

questions.text: Job 37 describes thunder, lightning, snow, and rain in vivid detail as Elihu prepares Job for Adonai's own speech — notice how this extended weather description functions as an overture before God finally speaks in chapter 38.

Elihu claims in Job 36:22, 'Behold, God is exalted in his power; who is a teacher (moreh) like him?' — how does this title, related to the word Torah itself, frame what Elihu believes he and the other friends have been attempting to do for Job?

Elihu positions himself as relaying God's true instruction against the friends' faulty wisdom, while in Mark 7 Yeshua rebukes the Pharisees for holding to human tradition, like Corban, while 'making void the word of God' — how do both passages distinguish between claiming to speak for God and actually doing so?
Yeshua teaches in Mark 7 that defilement comes from the heart, not from external ritual failures — what is one internal attitude, rather than outward behavior, you need to bring before God this week?

Elihu criticizes the other three friends for failing to answer Job properly, yet his own speeches largely repeat their arguments at greater length — discuss how Mark 7's Pharisees, who elevate their traditions to the level of Torah itself, illustrate a similar pattern of well-intentioned instruction becoming self-defeating.

theme: Where Were You? Who Then Is This?

questions.text: Job 38:1 says Adonai answers Job 'out of the whirlwind' rather than in a quiet voice or through a friend — notice how the setting itself, a violent storm, matches the overwhelming nature of the questions that follow.

In Job 39, Adonai describes the wild donkey (pere) who scorns the city and mocks the shouting of a driver — what does choosing this specific untamable animal suggest about the limits of human control Job is being confronted with?

Adonai's rhetorical questions in Job 38-39 humble every human claim to understanding, while in Mark 8 Kefa's confession that Yeshua is 'the Messiah' comes not from human deduction but from a kind of revelation — how do both passages suggest that true knowledge of God arrives as gift rather than achievement?

Yeshua heals a blind man in two stages in Mark 8, with sight coming gradually rather than all at once — where in your own spiritual life might growth be happening gradually rather than instantly, and how can you be patient with that process?

Job never receives an explanation for his suffering, only an overwhelming display of God's power and wisdom in creation — discuss whether this kind of answer is satisfying, and how it compares to Kefa's abrupt confession of Yeshua's identity followed immediately by his rebuke for rejecting the idea of the Messiah's suffering.

theme: Behemoth, Leviathan, and a Voice from the Cloud

questions.text: Job 40:2 has Adonai ask, 'Shall a faultfinder contend with Shaddai? He who argues with God, let him answer it' — notice how this directly challenges Job's earlier confident demand for a legal hearing.

Job 41 describes Leviathan (Livyatan) in extended, almost mythic detail as a creature no human can capture or tame — what does invoking this specific, fearsome sea creature communicate about the difference between human power and Adonai's?

Adonai's display of untamable creatures like Behemoth and Leviathan reveals a power beyond human mastery, and in Mark 9 the Transfiguration reveals Yeshua's own glory as his clothing becomes dazzling white while a voice from the cloud says, 'This is my beloved Son; listen to him' — how do both scenes reveal glory that human categories cannot contain?

Right after the Transfiguration, a father cries out, 'I believe; help my unbelief!' regarding his suffering son — where do you need to bring that same honest mixture of faith and doubt to God this week?

Job is finally silenced not by an explanation but by an overwhelming vision of God's creative power over creatures like Behemoth and Leviathan — discuss how this compares to the disciples in Mark 9 arguing about who is greatest immediately after witnessing Yeshua's glory, and what that says about how easily awe fades into self-concern.

theme: Now My Eye Sees You

questions.text: Job 42:5 has Job say, 'I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you,' right before he repents 'in dust and ashes' — notice that Job never receives the explanation he demanded, yet this personal encounter is enough to satisfy him.

Song of Songs 1:2 opens with the Shulammite's longing, 'Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth,' using intimate, direct address rather than distant description — what does this immediacy suggest about the kind of love the book will explore?

Job moves from hearsay to seeing God face to face, and in Mark 10 blind Bartimai cries out, 'Son of David, have mercy on me,' and receives his sight after persistent faith — how do both accounts show a movement from merely hearing about God to truly perceiving him?

Adonai restores Job's fortunes 'twice as much as he had before' after Job prays for his friends who wronged him (42:10) — is there someone who has wronged you that you need to pray for before you can move forward yourself?

Job's three friends are rebuked by Adonai for not speaking rightly about him, while Job himself is commended despite his raw complaints — discuss what this reversal suggests about how God values honest wrestling over confident but wrong theology.

theme: Arise, My Love: Two Processions of a King

questions.text: Song of Songs 2:11-12 describes winter passing, flowers appearing, and the voice of the turtledove heard in the land — notice how the Beloved uses the arrival of spring itself as an invitation to his bride.

Song of Songs 3:6-11 describes Solomon's wedding procession coming up from the wilderness, surrounded by sixty mighty men, with Solomon wearing the crown his mother placed on him — what does this royal imagery suggest about how the book frames love as something worth celebrating publicly?

Song of Songs 3 pictures King Solomon's grand procession into Jerusalem, and Mark 11 records a far humbler procession as Yeshua, greater than Solomon, enters the city on a young donkey while crowds shout 'Hoshia na' — how do the differences between these two royal entries reveal something about the kind of kingship Yeshua embodies?

The Shulammite searches through the city at night for her beloved in Song of Songs 3 before finally finding him — what practice of seeking God, prayer, Scripture, or quiet, might you need to pursue more persistently this week?

Yeshua cleanses the Temple in Mark 11, declaring it should be 'a house of prayer for all nations' — discuss what this vision of an inclusive house of prayer has to do with, or adds to, Song of Songs' celebration of covenant love and belonging.

theme: A Garden Locked, a Beloved Sought, a Cornerstone Rejected

questions.text: Song of Songs 4:12 describes the bride as 'a garden locked, a spring shut up, a fountain sealed' — notice how this image of enclosure and protection shifts by chapter 5 when the beloved comes knocking and the bride hesitates to open.

In Song of Songs 5:16, the bride describes her beloved as machamadim, often translated 'altogether desirable' — what does choosing a superlative, all-encompassing word like this suggest about how she wants her friends to understand him?

The bride in Song of Songs 5 delays opening the door and then searches desperately when her beloved is gone, while Mark 12's parable of the vineyard tenants pictures a beloved son being rejected entirely, becoming 'the stone that the builders rejected' who 'has become the cornerstone' — how do both passages explore the tragedy and reversal of a beloved being refused?

The bride in Song of Songs 5:3 makes excuses for not opening the door immediately when her beloved knocks — what excuse do you tend to make when God is asking for your immediate attention or obedience?

Yeshua identifies the greatest commandment in Mark 12 as loving Adonai with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and loving your neighbor as yourself — discuss how Song of Songs' portrait of wholehearted, undivided love between the bride and groom illustrates or deepens what that kind of love actually looks like in practice.

theme: Beautiful as the Dawn, Watching for the Day

questions.text: Song of Songs 6:10 asks, 'Who is this who looks forth like the dawn, fair as the moon, bright as the sun?' — notice how this question uses cosmic, sky-based imagery to praise the bride's beauty.

Song of Songs 7:10 declares, 'I am my beloved's, and his desire (teshuqah) is for me' — this same rare word appears in Genesis 3:16 and 4:7 describing desire bound up with conflict — what does it mean for that word to be redeemed here into a picture of mutual delight?

Song of Songs uses the imagery of dawn, sun, and moon to celebrate the bride's beauty, while Mark 13 describes the sun and moon being darkened before 'they will see the Son of Man coming in clouds with great glory' — how do these opposite uses of cosmic imagery, one celebrating intimacy and one announcing the end of the age, both point to a climactic, undeniable revealing?

Yeshua repeatedly says in Mark 13 to 'be on the alert' since no one knows the exact day or hour of his return — what specific habit of watchfulness could keep you spiritually alert this week rather than spiritually drowsy?

Song of Songs lingers over beauty, delight, and unhurried love, while Mark 13 warns urgently of wars, earthquakes, and coming judgment — discuss how a life of faith holds together both unhurried delight in God's gifts and sober watchfulness for what is still to come.

theme: Love Strong as Death, Loyalty That Crosses Borders

questions.text: Song of Songs 8:6-7 declares that love is 'strong as death' and that 'many waters cannot quench love' — notice that the book's climactic statement about love uses death and flood imagery rather than simply romantic language.

Ruth's declaration in 1:16, 'your people shall be my people, and your God my God,' echoes the Hebrew chesed used elsewhere in the book for covenant loyalty — what does it mean that a Moabite woman becomes a model of chesed toward Naomi and Naomi's God?

Song of Songs' declaration that love is stronger than death anticipates the Last Supper and Gethsemane in Mark 14, where Yeshua's sacrificial love culminates in his blood 'poured out for many'; Ruth's costly loyalty to Naomi similarly foreshadows the woman in Mark 14 who pours out expensive perfume on Yeshua, an act he says will be told 'wherever the gospel is proclaimed' — how do these acts of costly devotion illuminate one another?

Ruth chooses costly loyalty to Naomi with no guarantee of reward in a foreign land — what relationship or commitment in your life is asking for that same kind of loyalty regardless of what you receive in return?

Naomi tells the women of Bethlehem to call her Mara ('bitter') because of her losses, even while Ruth's faithfulness is already quietly setting up her redemption — discuss how Mark 14's account of betrayal and denial, occurring the same night as the Passover meal celebrating redemption, holds together bitterness and hope in a similar way.

theme: Under Whose Wings, Boaz the Kinsman-Redeemer

questions.text: Boaz tells Ruth in 2:12 that she has come to take refuge 'under the wings' of Adonai, the God of Israel — notice how this blessing, spoken to a Moabite gleaner, anticipates the actual protection Boaz himself will soon provide her.

Ruth asks Boaz in 3:9 to spread his 'wing' or garment (kanaph) over her, invoking the role of a go'el, a kinsman-redeemer who could buy back land and even marry a widow to preserve a family line — what specific qualifications did a go'el need to meet under Torah?

Boaz as go'el must be a near kinsman, willing, and able to pay the price of redemption for Ruth and Naomi, and Mark 15 shows Yeshua, our own near kinsman by his incarnation, willingly paying the ultimate price at Golgotha — how does Boaz's redemption in Ruth prefigure the redemption accomplished at the cross?

Ruth acts with boldness and vulnerability at the threshing floor, trusting Boaz's character before knowing the outcome — where do you need similar trust in God's character this week, even without knowing how a situation will resolve?

The centurion at the cross in Mark 15 declares, 'Truly this man was the Son of God,' immediately after witnessing how Yeshua died — discuss what it means that recognition of Yeshua's identity, like Boaz's recognition of Ruth's worth, often comes through witnessing costly, self-giving action rather than through words alone.

theme: From Redemption's Genealogy to the Empty Tomb

questions.text: Ruth 4:18-22 ends the book with a genealogy running from Peretz through Boaz and Oved to David — notice how a story that began with famine, death, and a bitter widow closes with a specific line leading to Israel's greatest king.

Lamentations 1:12 cries out, 'Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by? Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow' — this appeal uses the image of a road (derek) with indifferent travelers passing by — what does it mean that Jerusalem, personified as a grieving widow, calls out to those who simply pass her on the way?

Ruth's genealogy quietly builds toward David and ultimately the Messiah, Lamentations mourns Jerusalem's devastation as a widow with no comforter, and Mark 16 answers both threads at the empty tomb, where sorrow gives way to the announcement 'He is risen' — how does the resurrection function as the redemption that both Ruth's hopeful ending and Lamentations' grief are ultimately reaching toward?

Naomi began her story asking to be called Mara ('bitter') but ends it holding Ruth and Boaz's child in her arms as her own redemption — what current 'bitter' chapter of your life might Adonai still be writing a redemptive ending for?

Lamentations 1 offers raw, unresolved grief over Jerusalem's destruction with no immediate comfort in the chapter itself, while Mark 16 ends in its earliest manuscripts somewhat abruptly with the women fleeing the tomb in fear and amazement — discuss what it means that Scripture sometimes leaves grief and wonder unresolved on the page, trusting the reader to carry the story forward in faith.

theme: Zion's ruin, morning mercy, Messiah announced

questions.text: In Lamentations 4, notice how the poet contrasts the 'precious sons of Zion, worth their weight in fine gold' with their present state, valued like common clay pots. What does this reversal capture about the true cost of Jerusalem's fall?

In Lamentations 3:22-23 the poet says Adonai's chesed (loving devotion) never ceases and His faithfulness (emunah) is new every morning. What does pairing these two words together reveal about God's character even in the middle of judgment?

Zechariah's silenced doubt and Mary's costly yes both answer the kind of desperate hope Lamentations 3 clings to ('His mercies are new every morning'). How does Luke 1's announcement of Messiah respond to Jeremiah's lament over a broken Jerusalem?
Where in your life this week do you need to 'call this to mind,' rehearsing God's faithfulness the way Jeremiah does, rather than only rehearsing your circumstances?

Lamentations refuses to look away from real suffering, even slaughtered children and a captured king, while still affirming God's chesed. How should a community hold both unflinching honesty about suffering and confident hope together, and where do we tend to sacrifice one for the other?

theme: An unresolved lament meets vanity of vanities

questions.text: Lamentations 5 ends not with resolution but with a question, 'unless You have utterly rejected us.' Notice how the book refuses a tidy, comfortable ending.

Kohelet opens Ecclesiastes with 'hevel havalim,' usually translated 'vanity of vanities' but literally meaning vapor or breath. How does picturing life as a vapor change your sense of what Kohelet is arguing?

Ecclesiastes 1 laments that 'there is nothing new under the sun,' yet Luke 2 records something genuinely new breaking into history: Messiah's birth in Bethlehem announced to shepherds by an angelic host. How does Yeshua's coming answer Kohelet's search for lasting meaning?
What 'vapor' are you chasing this week that Kohelet would call you to release, in favor of simply receiving the day as a gift from God?

Is Ecclesiastes' verdict that 'all is vapor' pessimism, realism, or a form of worship? How does Simeon's readiness to 'depart in peace' in Luke 2 model a different way of facing life's fleeting nature?

theme: A time for everything, a King who fulfills the season

questions.text: In Ecclesiastes 2, notice the sheer scale of Kohelet's experiment: houses, vineyards, gardens, singers, silver and gold, before he concludes it was all 'chasing after wind.'

The famous poem of Ecclesiastes 3 turns on the word 'et,' time or season: a time to be born, a time to die. What does the structure of fourteen paired opposites suggest about God's sovereignty over time itself?

Luke 3 places Yeshua's baptism and genealogy, traced all the way back to Adam, at a specific historical moment, the fifteenth year of Tiberius. How does this show Messiah entering at God's appointed et, just as Ecclesiastes 3 insists every event has its season?
Which season from Ecclesiastes 3, mourning, silence, tearing, embracing, do you find yourself in right now, and how might naming it honestly change how you pray?
Kohelet says God 'has set eternity in the hearts of men' yet they cannot fathom His work from beginning to end (3:11). How do you live faithfully with mysteries you cannot resolve?

theme: A threefold cord and Yeshua's Nazareth manifesto

questions.text: Notice the specific image in Ecclesiastes 4:12, 'a threefold cord is not quickly broken,' following Kohelet's observations about the loneliness of the oppressed and the solitary worker with no heir.

Ecclesiastes 5:1 warns to guard your 'regel' (foot, or steps) when you go to the house of God, letting your words be few. What does this counsel about approaching worship with restraint rather than rash vows?

Where Kohelet observes oppression 'under the sun' with no one to comfort the victims (4:1), Yeshua stands in the Nazareth synagogue in Luke 4 and reads Isaiah 61, declaring good news to the poor and freedom for the oppressed fulfilled 'today.'
Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 warns it is better not to vow than to vow and not pay. What commitment have you made to God that needs either follow-through or honest release this week?

Yeshua's hometown neighbors move from admiration to trying to throw Him off a cliff within the same chapter. What does this sudden reversal reveal about how familiarity can breed both intimacy and offense?

theme: A good name, Agur's question, the fishermen's call

questions.text: Ecclesiastes 7:1 declares 'a good name is better than precious oil, and the day of death better than the day of birth.' Notice how Kohelet inverts ordinary expectations throughout this chapter.

Agur's words in Proverbs 30 open with confessed ignorance, 'I am more brutish than any man,' before he asks, 'Who has ascended into heaven and descended?' What is Agur searching for in naming God's Son in verse 4?

Kohelet warns against being 'excessively righteous' in a self-reliant way (7:16), while Luke 5 shows Yeshua eating with tax collectors like Levi and declaring He came to call sinners, not the righteous, to repentance.
Ecclesiastes 7:8 says 'the end of a matter is better than its beginning, and patience is better than pride.' Where do you need to trade impatience for endurance this week?

Peter's response to the miraculous catch of fish is, 'Depart from me, for I am a sinful man' (Luke 5:8). Why does an encounter with God's power often produce this reaction of self-awareness, and is it the right first response?

theme: One fate for all, a woman of valor, blessings and woes

questions.text: Ecclesiastes 9:11 observes that 'the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong.' Notice how chapter 9 wrestles with the same fate, death, coming to wise and fool, righteous and wicked alike.

Proverbs 31's closing acrostic praises the 'eshet chayil,' woman of valor or strength, the same phrase used elsewhere for warriors. How does this word choice elevate the woman described beyond mere domestic virtue?

Ecclesiastes 9:4 says 'a living dog is better than a dead lion' because 'there is hope' for the living, while Luke 6's Sermon on the Plain pronounces blessing on the poor, hungry, and weeping precisely because reversal is coming through Messiah's kingdom.
Kohelet counsels, 'whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your might' (9:10). What task have you been doing half-heartedly that deserves full effort this week?
Luke 6 pairs blessings with corresponding woes. How does this structure challenge assumptions about who is truly fortunate, and where do you find it most uncomfortable?

theme: Bread on the waters, wisdom's cry, a widow's son restored

questions.text: Ecclesiastes 11:1 pictures 'casting your bread upon the waters' to find it again 'after many days.' Notice how this image sits among Kohelet's counsel to act despite uncertain outcomes.
Proverbs 1:7 declares 'yirat Adonai,' the fear of Adonai, is the beginning of da'at, knowledge. How does this opening verse frame everything that follows in the book?

Wisdom personified cries out in the streets in Proverbs 1, while in Luke 7 John the Baptist's disciples ask Yeshua directly whether He is 'the one who is to come,' and Yeshua answers by pointing to His works, the blind seeing, the dead raised, as evidence.
Ecclesiastes 11:4 warns that one who watches the wind will never sow. What step of faith are you delaying while waiting for perfect conditions?

When Yeshua raises the widow of Nain's only son, the crowd declares 'God has visited His people.' What does this specific act of compassion toward an otherwise unprotected widow reveal about how Messiah's power intersects with mercy for the vulnerable?

theme: Remember your Creator; a queen's costly refusal

questions.text: Ecclesiastes 12 pictures old age through poetic imagery, 'the silver cord is snapped,' 'the golden bowl is broken,' before Kohelet's final verdict: fear God and keep His commandments.

In Esther 1, the king's officials fear that Vashti's refusal to appear will teach wives throughout the empire to despise their husbands' authority. What does this anxiety reveal about the political stakes driving the decree that follows?

Ecclesiastes 12:14 says God will bring every deed into judgment, including hidden things, an idea that anchors Luke 8's parable of the sower, where Yeshua says nothing hidden will fail to be disclosed, and the seed's fruitfulness reveals the true state of the heart.
'Remember your Creator in the days of your youth' (Ecclesiastes 12:1). What would it look like to build that remembrance into your habits now rather than waiting?

Vashti's refusal costs her the throne, yet the text never explicitly condemns her. How do you read her choice, and what does the narrative's silence about her motives invite you to consider about power and dignity?

theme: Esther's hidden identity, Haman's plot, Messiah confessed

questions.text: Notice in Esther 2 that Mordechai instructs Esther not to reveal her people or kindred. Her Jewish identity stays hidden through her rise to the throne and into chapter 3's crisis.

In Esther 3, Haman casts 'pur,' the lot, to determine the date for destroying the Jews, the very word from which the feast of Purim later takes its name. What does gambling with a people's fate by lot reveal about Haman's contempt?
Esther risks her hidden identity to intercede for her people, while in Luke 9 Peter openly confesses Yeshua as 'the Messiah of God.' Both moments turn on a costly, decisive declaration of identity.
Where might God be calling you, like Esther, to stop concealing your convictions and act at personal risk for the sake of others?

Haman's rage is provoked entirely by Mordechai's single refusal to bow. How does the narrative use one man's wounded pride to nearly annihilate an entire people, and what does that say about how hatred escalates from personal insult to systemic evil?

theme: For such a time as this; the Good Samaritan's mercy

questions.text: Notice Mordechai's challenge to Esther in chapter 4, 'who knows whether you have attained royalty for such a time as this?', and Esther's costly response, 'if I perish, I perish.'

Esther calls for a 'tzom,' a fast, of three days before approaching the king unsummoned. What does this specific act of collective fasting communicate about the seriousness of the risk she is about to take?

Esther's willingness to approach the king at the risk of her life anticipates the self-giving love Yeshua describes in Luke 10's parable, where the Samaritan crosses every social boundary to save a stranger's life.
'For such a time as this' has become a call to courageous action. What situation in your life right now may be exactly the moment God has positioned you to act?

The lawyer in Luke 10 asks 'who is my neighbor?' seeking to limit his obligation. How does Yeshua's parable redefine neighbor as a verb, showing mercy, rather than a category of person, and who is hardest for you to see as your neighbor?

theme: Reversal at the banquet; the sign of Jonah

questions.text: Notice the irony in Esther 6: on the very night the king cannot sleep, he has the court records read to him and discovers Mordechai's unrewarded loyalty, just as Haman arrives to request permission to hang him.

Esther finally reveals her identity to the king in chapter 7, naming Haman 'tzar v'oyev,' adversary and enemy. How does this precise, formal accusation seal Haman's fate at the very banquet he expected to secure his triumph?

The gallows Haman built for Mordechai become the instrument of his own destruction, an ironic reversal echoed in Luke 11 when Yeshua says only 'the sign of Jonah' will be given, death and vindication turned upside down for those who plotted evil.
Where do you need to trust God's timing for reversal rather than taking justice into your own hands, as Esther waited for the right banquet and the right moment to speak?

Esther says nothing at the first banquet and waits for a second. What does her patience and timing teach about the difference between speaking truth prematurely and speaking it at the moment it will be received?

theme: Purim instituted, treasure examined

questions.text: Notice how Esther 8's new decree does not reverse the original edict, since Persian law could not be revoked, but instead grants Jews the right to defend themselves. The salvation comes through resistance, not repeal.
Esther 9 institutes the festival of Purim, named after the very 'pur,' the lot, Haman cast to destroy the Jews, celebrating how the instrument of intended destruction became a name for deliverance.

Psalm 16:10, 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor let Your Holy One see decay,' is quoted by Kefa in Acts 2 as prophecy of Messiah's resurrection. Read it alongside Esther's story of reversal from decreed death to life for the Jewish people.
Luke 12 warns against the rich fool who stores up treasure but is not 'rich toward God.' What would it look like to actively store up the kind of treasure that lasts this week?

Purim commemorates a deliverance where God's name never appears explicitly in the text. How does Esther's hidden providence shape how you notice, or fail to notice, God's hand in your own ordinary circumstances?

theme: Daniel's resolve, a barren fig tree's mercy

questions.text: Notice in Daniel 1 that Daniel 'resolved in his heart' not to defile himself with the king's food, a quiet, deliberate decision made before any test was even proposed.

Daniel and his friends are given new Babylonian names, Belteshazzar, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, replacing names that honored the God of Israel. What does the empire's renaming attempt to accomplish, and why does Daniel's resolve resist it?

Daniel's ten-day test of faithfulness in exile anticipates Luke 13's parable of the barren fig tree, where the gardener pleads for one more season of patient cultivation before judgment. Both stories hold tension between testing and mercy.
What resolve of the heart, like Daniel's, do you need to make now, before the pressure to compromise actually arrives?

Daniel thrives under Babylonian training without abandoning Torah faithfulness. How do you navigate living faithfully within a culture that doesn't share your values, without either total assimilation or total withdrawal?

theme: The stone cut without hands, the fourth man in the fire

questions.text: Notice that in Daniel 3, the king's astonishment isn't just that Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego survive the furnace, but that he sees a fourth figure walking with them, 'like a son of the gods.'

In Daniel 2, the stone that destroys the statue is 'cut out without hands,' emphasizing that this is entirely God's doing, not human achievement. What does this phrase suggest about the kingdom the stone represents?

The stone that becomes a mountain filling the whole earth (Daniel 2:35) points to an everlasting kingdom, the same kingdom Yeshua describes in Luke 14's parable of the great banquet, where those first invited are replaced by the poor, crippled, blind, and lame.
Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego say 'even if He does not' deliver them, they will not bow (Daniel 3:18). What would unconditional faithfulness look like for you this week, regardless of outcome?

Nebuchadnezzar builds a golden statue on the very plain where Daniel earlier interpreted a dream about a statue's downfall. What does this suggest about how power resists truth even after directly witnessing it?

theme: A king humbled, a king weighed, a father who runs

questions.text: Notice that Daniel 4 is written in Nebuchadnezzar's own voice, a pagan king's public testimony after seven years of madness that 'the Most High rules the kingdom of men.'

The handwriting on the wall in Daniel 5 reads 'Mene, Mene, Tekel, Upharsin.' Daniel interprets 'Tekel' as 'you have been weighed on the scales and found wanting.' What does this image of weighing suggest about how God evaluates a reign?

Nebuchadnezzar's restoration after humbling himself (Daniel 4:34-37) foreshadows the pattern in Luke 15's prodigal son, where recognition of one's true state precedes the return home and restoration by the father.
Belshazzar had Nebuchadnezzar's example before him yet ignored it (Daniel 5:22). What warning or lesson from someone else's life do you need to actually apply rather than merely admire?

All three of Luke 15's parables, lost sheep, lost coin, prodigal son, end in celebration. Why does Yeshua tell three versions of the same point to a crowd that included grumbling religious leaders, and what does the elder brother's absence from the party reveal?

theme: The lions' den, the Ancient of Days, one like a son of man

questions.text: Notice that Daniel 6 shows Daniel continuing his prayer practice exactly as before, windows open toward Jerusalem, three times a day, even after learning of the decree designed to trap him.

Daniel 7's vision introduces 'bar enosh,' one like a son of man, who approaches the Ancient of Days and receives an everlasting dominion. This title becomes Yeshua's own favorite self-designation in the Gospels.
The 'son of man' figure given dominion, glory, and a kingdom in Daniel 7:13-14 stands directly behind Yeshua's repeated claim in Luke to be the Son of Man who will come in glory.
Daniel's unwavering prayer routine cost him nearly everything. What regular spiritual discipline in your life would you maintain even if it became personally risky?

Proverbs 10:19 warns 'in the multitude of words sin is not lacking,' while Daniel's few, faithful actions speak louder than any defense he could offer the king. When is restrained silence wiser than argument in defending your convictions?

theme: Seventy sevens, a kingdom in your midst, ten cleansed

questions.text: Notice that Daniel 9 opens with Daniel searching Jeremiah's prophecy about seventy years of exile, which then prompts his own extended prayer of confession on behalf of his people.

Gabriel's prophecy to Daniel speaks of 'shavu'im shivim,' seventy sevens, decreed to bring the people's transgression to an end and to anoint the Most Holy. This specific timeframe has been read messianically for centuries.

Daniel 9's prophecy of an anointed one being 'cut off' resonates with Luke 17, where Yeshua says the kingdom of God is 'in your midst,' already present in His person even while its fullness remains future.

Daniel's prayer in chapter 9 models corporate confession, 'we have sinned,' even for sins he personally didn't commit. Where might you need to intercede for your community's failures rather than only your own?

Of the ten lepers healed in Luke 17, only the Samaritan returns to give thanks. Why does gratitude often correlate with being an outsider, and what does this pattern suggest about who tends to notice grace most readily?

theme: The prince of Persia, forsaken and vindicated, a persistent widow

questions.text: Notice in Daniel 10 that the angelic messenger was delayed three weeks by 'the prince of the kingdom of Persia' until Michael came to help, a rare glimpse into unseen spiritual conflict behind earthly events.

Psalm 22 opens with 'Eli, Eli, lama azavtani,' my God, my God, why have You forsaken me, the exact words Yeshua cries from the cross, and the psalm goes on to describe pierced hands and feet and garments divided by lot.

Psalm 22's movement from abandonment to vindication, 'all the ends of the earth will remember and turn to Adonai,' mirrors Daniel's visions of history's unseen battles resolving in God's favor, and anticipates Yeshua's own suffering followed by resurrection glory.
Daniel fasted and prayed for three weeks before receiving an answer. Where do you need patient persistence, like the widow in Luke 18's parable, rather than giving up when God seems delayed?

Luke 18 pairs the persistent widow with the Pharisee and tax collector's contrasting prayers. What does it mean to pray with both bold persistence and genuine humility at the same time, and is that combination difficult for you?

theme: Those who sleep in the dust awake; Cyrus sends exiles home
