questions.text: Notice Daniel 12's striking promise that 'many who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake, some to everlasting life,' one of the clearest resurrection statements in the Tanakh.

Ezra 1 records that Adonai stirred the 'ruach,' spirit, of Cyrus king of Persia to issue his decree, the same word used for God's Spirit moving over creation, now moving a pagan king's will to restore Israel to the land.

Cyrus's decree releasing exiles to rebuild the Temple is a partial, historical restoration that anticipates the fuller restoration Yeshua weeps over in Luke 19 as He approaches Jerusalem, longing for the city to recognize 'the things that make for peace.'

Daniel is told to 'go your way until the end' and rest, for he will 'rise for his allotted portion at the end of days' (12:13). What does it look like to trust God with parts of His plan not yet revealed to you?
Cyrus, a pagan king, is used as an instrument of God's restoration of Israel. How does this challenge easy categories of insider and outsider in how God accomplishes His purposes in history?

theme: Foundation laid amid weeping and joy

questions.text: Notice the striking detail in Ezra 3 that when the Temple's foundation is laid, some old men who remembered the first Temple weep loudly while others shout for joy, the two sounds becoming indistinguishable.

Ezra 2's long genealogical list preserves the specific families and numbers of those who returned. Why would meticulous record-keeping of names and numbers matter so much to a people rebuilding their identity after exile?

Psalm 24 asks 'who may ascend the hill of Adonai' and answers with clean hands and a pure heart, anticipating the King of glory entering His gates, a question Luke 20 revisits when religious leaders challenge Yeshua's authority to be in the Temple at all.

The mixed emotions at the foundation-laying, grief for what was lost alongside joy for what is being restored, may describe your own life right now. How can you hold both without forcing false resolution?

Ezra 3 shows the altar rebuilt before the Temple foundation, meaning sacrifices resumed even while the building itself remained unfinished. What does prioritizing worship before completion of every plan suggest about the order of our own spiritual priorities?

theme: Opposition, delay, and a Temple completed

questions.text: Notice in Ezra 4-6 the extended gap in the narrative timeline: work on the Temple halts for years due to local opposition and official letters to the Persian court, until Darius's decree and the prophets Haggai and Zechariah spur completion.

Ezra 6 records the Temple's completion and celebration of Passover 'with joy, for Adonai had turned the heart' of the Assyrian king toward them. Note how a foreign king's heart being turned resolves the crisis.

The long delay and eventual completion of the Second Temple, achieved through prophetic encouragement rather than political power alone, parallels Luke 21's Olivet discourse, where Yeshua predicts this very Temple's future destruction and calls His followers to persevere through delay before ultimate restoration.
The returned exiles needed the prophetic encouragement of Haggai and Zechariah to resume a stalled task. What stalled work in your life needs a fresh word of encouragement to get moving again?

Opposition to the Temple's rebuilding came partly from people who claimed to also worship God but were refused a role in the work (Ezra 4:1-3). When is exclusion from a shared project the right call, and when does it needlessly divide people who could be genuine allies?

theme: Ezra's resolve, the Last Supper's new covenant

questions.text: Notice that Ezra 8 records Ezra's deliberate choice not to ask the king for soldiers to protect the journey to Jerusalem, 'since we had said to the king, the hand of our God is on all who seek Him.'
Ezra 7:10 says Ezra had 'set his heart' to seek, darash, the Torah of Adonai, to do it, and to teach it in Israel. Notice the deliberate order: study, obedience, then teaching.

Ezra's fast for a safe journey without military protection anticipates the trust Yeshua models in Luke 22's garden of Gethsemane, submitting to the Father's will even as arrest and death approach, and refusing Peter's sword in His defense.
Ezra's threefold pattern, set the heart, study, do, then teach, offers a model for spiritual growth. Where are you skipping a step, perhaps teaching or discussing before you've truly practiced?

Yeshua institutes the Last Supper within a Passover seder, saying the cup is 'the new covenant in My blood' (Luke 22:20). How does understanding this meal's Passover context change how you read Yeshua's words about His body and blood?

theme: Confession over covenant unfaithfulness, the innocent one crucified

questions.text: Notice Ezra's physical response to learning of the people's intermarriage with idolatrous nations in chapter 9: he tears his garment, pulls hair from his head and beard, and sits appalled before he even speaks.
Ezra's prayer in chapter 9 uses the phrase 'ashmah gedolah,' great guilt, to describe the people's condition, tracing their unfaithfulness all the way back to warnings given through Moses.

Ezra's grief over covenant unfaithfulness and the community's costly repentance in chapter 10 contrast with Luke 23, where Pilate repeatedly declares Yeshua innocent, yet the truly guiltless one is the one who is crucified.
The community's repentance in Ezra 10 was costly and disruptive to family life. What costly, disruptive step might genuine repentance require of you, not just a change of feeling?

Ezra's solution to intermarriage, having men separate from foreign wives and children, is one of the most ethically difficult passages in the Tanakh. How do you wrestle with a text that prioritizes covenant faithfulness in a way that causes real human pain?

theme: A ruined wall, a night ride, an empty tomb

questions.text: Notice that Nehemiah 2 shows him surveying Jerusalem's broken walls by night, alone except for one animal, before revealing his plans to anyone, careful private assessment before public action.
Nehemiah 1:11 asks God to grant him 'chesed,' favor or mercy, 'in the presence of this man,' referring to King Artaxerxes, before he ever speaks to the king about his grief.

Nehemiah's grief over Jerusalem's ruined walls and his resolve to rebuild echoes in Luke 24, where two disciples on the road to Emmaus grieve over what seems like a ruined hope in Yeshua, until the risen Messiah Himself opens their eyes to the Scriptures.
Nehemiah prayed for months before acting on his burden for Jerusalem. What burden have you been carrying that needs sustained prayer before you take action?

Nehemiah weeps, fasts, and prays for days upon hearing bad news, yet also takes immediate practical steps once opportunity arises. How do you balance genuine lament with decisive action rather than letting one crowd out the other?

theme: Builders with a trowel and a sword; the Word made flesh

questions.text: Notice Nehemiah 3's long list of specific families and individuals, each rebuilding the section of wall 'in front of his own house,' a communal project made of countless personal contributions.

John 1:14 says the Word, echoing the Hebrew concept of God's creative dabar, 'became flesh and dwelt,' literally tabernacled, among us, connecting Yeshua's incarnation to the Tabernacle where God's presence dwelt with Israel.

Nehemiah's workers build 'with one hand' while holding a weapon in the other against Sanballat and Tobiah's opposition, a picture of vigilant construction that parallels John 1's portrayal of the Word entering a world that 'did not recognize Him,' yet building anyway.
What wall in your life or community needs rebuilding even while facing ridicule or opposition, as Nehemiah's workers faced Sanballat's mockery?

John the Baptist calls Yeshua 'the Lamb of God' and Nathanael calls Him 'the Son of God, the King of Israel,' within the same chapter. What does the range of titles given to Yeshua in John 1 suggest about how differently people were prepared to recognize Him?

theme: Justice for the poor, opposition foiled, water into wine

questions.text: Notice Nehemiah 5's account of Jewish nobles charging interest and even enslaving their own countrymen's children during a famine. Nehemiah's anger is directed inward at his own people's injustice, not just at outside opposition.

In John 2, Yeshua tells His mother 'my hour' has not yet come, a phrase John repeatedly uses to track the timeline toward the cross, making the wedding at Cana miracle a preview of what's still ahead.

Nehemiah's cleansing of economic exploitation among his own people in chapter 5 parallels Yeshua's cleansing of the Temple in John 2, both confronting corruption that had crept into the very heart of covenant community life.
Nehemiah models confronting injustice even among his own allies rather than staying silent for the sake of unity. Where might you need similar courage this week?

Nehemiah completes the wall in fifty-two days despite constant plots, Sanballat's ambush attempts, Shemaiah's false prophecy. What does his refusal to be distracted, 'I am doing a great work and cannot come down,' teach about protecting focus from well-intentioned but misguided interruptions?

theme: Torah read aloud, tears turned to joy, born from above

questions.text: Notice Nehemiah 8's vivid scene: Ezra reads the Torah aloud from a wooden platform from daybreak until noon, while the Levites move through the crowd translating and explaining as they go.

In John 3, Yeshua tells Nicodemus he must be born 'anothen,' a Greek word meaning both 'again' and 'from above,' creating the very ambiguity that confuses Nicodemus about being born a second time physically.

The people weep upon hearing the Torah read in Nehemiah 8 but are told 'this day is holy, do not grieve,' then go on to celebrate Sukkot with great joy, paralleling how Nicodemus's confusion presumably turns toward the joy of new birth Yeshua describes.

The Israelites in Nehemiah 8 respond to hearing God's word by celebrating rather than only feeling guilty. How might you respond to conviction this week with joyful obedience rather than lingering shame?

Nicodemus comes to Yeshua 'by night,' cautious and searching, yet is later named among those who bury Yeshua's body. How do secret, hesitant beginnings of faith sometimes mature into open courage, and what does that suggest about how you view someone's slow journey toward belief?

theme: A recounted history, a sealed covenant, living water

questions.text: Notice that the Levites' prayer in Nehemiah 9 retells Israel's entire history from creation through the exodus, wilderness, conquest, and exile, a communal confession built on remembering both God's faithfulness and the people's rebellion.
Nehemiah 10 describes the people entering a written, sealed 'amanah,' a firm agreement or covenant, renewing their commitment to Torah obedience with specific, binding terms.

The Samaritan woman in John 4 represents exactly the kind of religious and ethnic division Nehemiah's returned exiles were working hard to avoid falling back into, yet Yeshua offers her living water that transcends the Jerusalem-versus-Gerizim worship dispute entirely.
Nehemiah 9's prayer models remembering both grace received and failures committed in the same breath. What would an honest recounting of your own spiritual history include this week?

The Samaritan woman becomes an evangelist to her whole town immediately after her encounter with Yeshua, despite her complicated personal history. What does her quick, effective witness suggest about who God chooses to use, and does that challenge assumptions about qualification for ministry?

theme: Repopulating Jerusalem, dedication with song, healing at Bethesda

questions.text: Notice that Nehemiah 11 records people volunteering, and others chosen by lot, to resettle Jerusalem itself, since the rebuilt walls needed inhabitants, not just structure.
Nehemiah 12's wall dedication features two large 'todah,' thanksgiving, choirs processing in opposite directions along the wall before meeting at the Temple, a term rooted in public, vocal gratitude.

The joyful, musical dedication of Jerusalem's walls in Nehemiah 12 contrasts with John 5's account of a paralyzed man waiting thirty-eight years by the pool of Bethesda for healing that never came until Yeshua simply spoke and restored him.

Nehemiah 11's volunteers chose personal sacrifice, leaving comfortable towns, to strengthen the community's core. What would it look like for you to volunteer for something unglamorous but structurally important this week?

Yeshua heals the man at Bethesda on the Sabbath and then tells him 'sin no more, lest something worse befall you.' What does this specific pairing of physical healing and moral warning suggest about how Yeshua understood suffering and sin in this case, without it becoming a universal rule?

theme: Final reforms, genealogies from Adam, bread from heaven

questions.text: Notice that Nehemiah 13, the book's final chapter, shows Nehemiah returning to Jerusalem after time away only to find the reforms he'd established, Sabbath-keeping, tithes, separation from foreign influence, already eroding, requiring him to confront the problems all over again.
1 Chronicles 1 opens the genealogy simply with 'Adam, Seth, Enosh,' tracing an unbroken line meant to root Israel's entire post-exilic identity in the very beginning of humanity itself.

1 Chronicles 1's genealogy tracing humanity back to Adam finds its counterpart in John 6, where Yeshua identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven, greater than the manna Israel's ancestors ate in the wilderness, sustaining life back to its very source.

Nehemiah's discouragement at seeing his reforms undone quickly, requiring him to physically throw Tobiah's furniture out of the Temple chamber, shows that faithfulness often means returning to the same battle more than once. What reform in your own life needs recommitment rather than abandonment?

The Chronicler begins Israel's story not with Abraham but all the way back with Adam. Why might tracing the genealogy back to the very first human matter for a people just returned from exile trying to understand their place in the world, and what does it suggest about how Scripture connects Israel's story to all humanity?

theme: David's Royal Line and Living Water at Sukkot

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 3 traces David's descendants all the way through the Babylonian exile to Zerubbabel — notice that the Chronicler kept meticulous records of the royal line even after the throne had fallen.

Proverbs 25:2 says it is the kavod (glory) of God to conceal a matter, but the kavod of kings to search it out. What does the double use of kavod suggest about the difference between divine and human 'glory'?

In John 7, during Sukkot, Yeshua stands in the Temple courts on the feast's last day and cries out about rivers of living water (7:37-38), echoing the festival's water-drawing ceremony — He speaks as the rightful heir of the very line just recorded in 1 Chronicles 3.
How does it change your outlook to know God keeps careful record of your identity and place in His story even during seasons that feel like exile?
Why would God preserve such a detailed genealogy through captivity and collapse — what does that persistence teach a group about covenant faithfulness when circumstances look like failure?

theme: Jabez's Bold Prayer and the Light of the World

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 4:9-10 interrupts a long genealogy with Jabez's short prayer, 'Oh that You would bless me indeed' — notice how the text pauses just to say that God granted his request.

Jabez (Ya'bets) sounds like the Hebrew for pain, since his mother said she bore him b'otzev — in pain. What does it mean that a man named for sorrow asked God to enlarge his territory and keep him from harm?

John 8:12 has Yeshua declare, 'I am the light of the world,' and later, 'before Abraham was, I AM' (8:58), claiming the divine Name itself — how does this compare to Jabez calling out to that same God by name for blessing?
Jabez's prayer was specific and unashamed rather than vague — where in your life this week could you pray with that same directness instead of a generic request?

In John 8, Yeshua tells the accusers of the woman caught in adultery, 'let him who is without sin cast the first stone' — discuss how a community holds both truth and mercy together without collapsing into either legalism or license.

theme: The Levitical Line, Iron Sharpening Iron, and Eyes Opened

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 6 traces the priestly line through Aaron and then lists the Levitical cities, including the cities of refuge scattered among the tribal territories — notice how provision for the accidental manslayer was woven into the nation's map.
Proverbs 27:17 says barzel b'barzel yachad — iron sharpens iron. What does the image of two hard metals striking each other suggest about the friction real friendship sometimes requires?

In John 9, Yeshua heals the man born blind by sending him to wash in the Pool of Siloam, whose name means 'sent' — connect this to Yeshua as the One sent by the Father, opening eyes both physically and spiritually.
The healed man in John 9 grows bolder in his testimony the more he is questioned — where might God be asking you to keep testifying to what you know, even under pressure to recant?

The Pharisees in John 9 insist they can see while remaining spiritually blind — discuss what willful spiritual blindness looks like today, and how a person can recognize it in themselves rather than only in others.

theme: Gatekeepers Restored to Jerusalem and the Good Shepherd

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 9 describes the priests, Levites, and gatekeepers resettling Jerusalem after the exile, with gatekeepers specifically posted on all four sides of the camp — notice how much attention is given to who guards the entrances.
The gatekeepers are called shoharim, from a root meaning to seek early or watch closely. What does this word suggest about the vigilance required of those entrusted with guarding sacred space?

John 10 has Yeshua declare both 'I am the gate' (10:9) and 'I am the good shepherd' (10:11) who lays down His life for the sheep — how does this connect to the gatekeepers of 1 Chronicles 9 who stood watch over God's house?
Yeshua warns of hired hands who flee when danger comes (John 10:12-13) — where in your responsibilities this week are you tempted to abandon your post rather than stand watch?

Yeshua says He has 'other sheep not of this fold' who will become 'one flock, one shepherd' (10:16) — discuss what this means for the relationship between Israel and the nations without erasing either's distinct identity.

theme: Saul's Unfaithful End, David's Mighty Men, and Life from the Dead

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 10 closes with the Chronicler's own verdict on Saul's death: he was unfaithful to Adonai and did not inquire of Him — notice that this summary judgment, not just the battle report, is what the text emphasizes.

David's elite warriors in 1 Chronicles 11 are called the gibborim, mighty men, including three who broke through Philistine lines just to bring David water from Bethlehem's well. What does gibbor suggest about strength devoted to loyal service rather than self-glory?

In John 11:25, Yeshua tells Martha, 'I am the resurrection and the life,' just before raising Lazarus — set this against Saul's unfaithful death in 1 Chronicles 10 as two very different pictures of what happens when a person does, or does not, seek Adonai.

David's mighty men risked their lives for something David didn't even ask for outright, just voiced a longing — what costly loyalty might God be inviting from you this week, beyond what's strictly required?

Yeshua weeps at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35) even though He knows He is about to raise him — discuss what this reveals about God's empathy for human grief even when resurrection and hope are already secured.

theme: Israel United Under David, Uzzah's Error, and the Grain of Wheat

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 12 lists warriors streaming to David at Hebron from every tribe, described as coming 'with a whole heart' to make him king — notice how the text piles up tribe after tribe to stress the unity of the moment.
Agur's sayings in Proverbs 30:5 call every word of God tzaruf — refined or tested, like metal purified by fire. What does it mean to trust words that have been tzaruf rather than untested claims?

John 12:24 has Yeshua say that unless a grain of wheat falls into the ground and dies it remains alone, spoken just before Passover as He foretells His own death — connect this to David uniting a fractured Israel, and to Messiah gathering a scattered people through His own death.
Uzzah dies for touching the ark against God's instructions even though he meant well (1 Chronicles 13:9-10) — where might good intentions be leading you to shortcut God's stated way of doing things?

Discuss the tension in Uzzah's death: how do we hold together God's holiness and the tragedy of a well-meaning man struck down, and what does the later correction in chapter 15 teach about learning from a failed attempt at obedience?

theme: The Ark Carried Rightly, the Woman of Valor, and Servant Love

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 15 shows David insisting this time that only Levites carry the ark on poles on their shoulders, 'as Moses commanded' — notice how explicitly the text contrasts this with the earlier failed attempt on a cart in chapter 13.

Proverbs 31 closes with eshet chayil, a woman of valor or strength, using chayil, a word elsewhere applied to mighty warriors. What does it suggest that the same word for battlefield strength is used to praise this woman's daily labor?

John 13 has Yeshua kneel to wash His disciples' feet and give a new command to love one another as He loved them — connect this posture of humble, obedient service to David's insistence on carrying the ark God's way rather than the convenient way.

David corrected his approach to worship once he learned it was wrong rather than defending his first attempt — what is one area where you need to humbly adjust how you're doing something, even if it means admitting an earlier mistake?
Why did the specific manner of carrying the ark matter so much to David the second time — discuss the balance between sincere devotion and careful obedience to how God actually asks to be worshiped.

theme: The Everlasting Davidic Covenant and the Way to the Father

questions.text: In 1 Chronicles 17, Nathan brings David God's promise that his house and throne will be established forever, and David responds not with pride but with humble prayer, asking, 'who am I, Adonai, and what is my house?'
Psalm 42:1 pictures a deer that ta'arog, pants or longs, for flowing streams. What does this word's intensity add to the psalmist's description of longing for God's presence?

John 14:6 has Yeshua declare, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through Me,' while also promising the Ruach ha-Kodesh — how does this fulfill the everlasting throne promised to David's line in 1 Chronicles 17?
David's response to an enormous promise was humility, not entitlement — how can receiving good news or blessing this week produce more humility in you rather than pride?

Discuss why an eternal covenant given to one man's house (David's) would ultimately become good news for all nations, and how that shapes the way we read promises made to Israel throughout the Tanakh.

theme: An Insult to David's Envoys and Abiding in the True Vine

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 19 records Hanun shaving off half the beards of David's envoys and cutting their garments at the hips — a calculated public humiliation in that culture — notice David's measured response of letting the men stay at Jericho until their beards grew back.

Proverbs 2 describes seeking chochmah, wisdom, like hidden treasure or silver. What does searching for wisdom 'as for silver' suggest about the effort required, compared to simply waiting for it to arrive?

John 15:4-5 has Yeshua say, 'I am the true vine... abide (meno) in Me,' warning that a branch cut off withers — connect this image of being cut off to the shame of Hanun's men having their garments literally cut, and consider what it means instead to remain attached to Messiah.

David protected his humiliated men from public shame by giving them time to recover privately — where could you extend that kind of patient protection to someone around you who has been shamed or embarrassed?
Discuss the wisdom in David's patient, non-retaliatory response to an insult before ultimately going to war — when is restraint the wiser path, and when does a wrong require an immediate response?

theme: David's Costly Threshing Floor and Sorrow Turned to Joy

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 21 recounts David's sinful census, the plague that follows, and his purchase of Ornan's threshing floor at full price, insisting, 'I will not offer to Adonai that which costs me nothing' — notice that this site becomes the future Temple location.

The goren, threshing floor, of Ornan sits on Mount Moriah, the very mountain where Abraham once bound Isaac. What significance might it hold that both stories center on costly sacrifice at the same site?

John 16:20-22 has Yeshua tell His disciples their grief will turn to joy like a woman in labor, and promises the Ruach ha-Kodesh will come — connect the plague's grief being transformed into the very ground of future Temple worship with sorrow becoming joy through the Spirit's coming.
David refused to offer God something that cost him nothing — where in your own worship or giving this week might you be tempted to offer only what is easy or convenient?
Discuss David's principle that worship which costs nothing is not really worship — how should this shape modern ideas about giving, sacrifice, and what we bring to God?

theme: David Prepares Solomon for the Temple and Yeshua's High Priestly Prayer

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 22 has David gather vast materials for the Temple and charge Solomon to 'be strong and courageous,' explaining that he himself cannot build it because he is a man of war who has shed much blood — notice this is a righteous man barred from a good task for a specific reason.

1 Chronicles 24 organizes the priests into mishmarot, divisions or watches, from mishmeret, meaning a charge to keep. What does organizing worship into ongoing 'watches' suggest about sustaining devotion across generations?

John 17, Yeshua's high priestly prayer just before His arrest, pleads 'Father, glorify Your Son' and prays for those the Father has given Him — compare this final act of preparation and intercession to David's final charge to Solomon before 'resting with his fathers.'
David spent his last active years preparing someone else to finish a work he would not see completed himself — what are you building or preparing this season that someone else may finish after you?
David is excluded from building the Temple because of bloodshed even though his wars defended Israel — discuss what this teaches about consequences that follow even justified or necessary actions.

theme: Musicians Appointed to Prophesy, God Our Refuge, and Yeshua's Arrest

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 25 appoints musicians under Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun 'to prophesy with lyres, harps, and cymbals' — notice that making music is described using the same verb as prophetic speech.
Psalm 46:1 calls God our machaseh, refuge or shelter, and strength, 'a very present help in trouble.' What does machaseh add to the image of God as a place one physically runs to for safety?

In John 18, Yeshua responds to the arresting soldiers with 'I am he' — ani hu, echoing the divine Name — and the soldiers fall backward, showing sovereign composure amid chaos; connect this to Psalm 46's call to 'be still and know that I am God.'
The Levitical musicians treated their craft as an act of prophetic ministry, not mere performance — what ordinary skill or task of yours could you offer this week as worship rather than routine?
Kefa denies Yeshua three times in John 18 despite earlier drawing a sword to defend Him — discuss the gap between bold intentions and actual courage under real pressure, and what closes that gap.

theme: The Divine Pattern for the Temple and the Cross

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 28:19 has David tell Solomon that he received the tabnit, the pattern, for the Temple 'in writing from the hand of Adonai' — notice the claim that this design was divinely revealed, not David's own invention.

Tabnit, pattern or model, is the same word used in Exodus 25 for the tabnit of the Tabernacle shown to Moses on the mountain. What does reusing this term across centuries suggest about God's consistency in how He wants to be worshiped?

John 19 records Yeshua's crucifixion, including His seamless tunic, echoing the high priest's garment, and His final words, 'It is finished' — connect the divinely given pattern for a physical Temple with Yeshua's body as the greater Temple whose work is completed on the cross.

David followed a pattern given to him rather than designing worship by his own preference — where this week might you need to set aside your own preferences to follow God's clearly stated instructions instead?

David insists the Temple plans came 'from the hand of Adonai' rather than from his own creativity — discuss the tension between honoring received tradition/instruction and the freedom to innovate in worship today.

theme: Willing Offerings, Solomon's Request for Wisdom, and the Resurrection

questions.text: 1 Chronicles 29 describes the people giving a nedavah, a freewill offering, for the Temple with great joy, and David's closing prayer declares, 'all things come from You, and of Your own we have given You'; then 2 Chronicles 1 has Solomon ask God at Gibeon for wisdom rather than riches or long life.
Nedavah, freewill offering, comes from a root meaning to be willing or moved voluntarily. How does this word distinguish David's people's giving from an obligatory tax or tithe?

John 20 recounts the empty tomb, Mary Magdalene's encounter with the risen Yeshua, and Thomas's confession, 'My Lord and my God' — connect David's declaration that everything ultimately comes from God with the ultimate gift of resurrection life freely given in Messiah.
Solomon could have asked for anything and chose wisdom to govern well — if you could ask God for one thing to help you serve others better this year, what would it be?

Thomas doubts the resurrection until he sees for himself, then makes the Gospel's boldest declaration of Yeshua's deity — discuss the difference between honest doubt that leads to deeper faith and doubt that simply hardens into unbelief.

theme: Solomon Builds the Temple, Wisdom at Creation, and Peter Restored

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 2-3 describes Solomon's correspondence with Huram of Tyre and the Temple's construction, including two great bronze pillars named Jachin and Boaz standing at its entrance — notice that these pillars were given names rather than left unmarked.
Proverbs 8 personifies Chochmah, Wisdom, saying she was beside God 'as a master craftsman' (amon) at creation. What does it add to picture wisdom as present and active at the world's very beginning?

John 21 has the risen Yeshua restore Kefa by the Sea of Galilee, asking 'Do you love Me?' three times to match his three denials, then commissioning him to 'feed My sheep' — connect Wisdom's presence at creation with Yeshua's presence at Kefa's re-establishment, echoing Jachin, 'He will establish.'
Kefa's failure was not the end of his story but the doorway to a deeper commission — what past failure of yours might God want to use as the very place He restores and recommissions you?

Discuss why Yeshua chose to restore Kefa with a threefold question matching his threefold denial rather than simply moving past it in silence — what does this teach about how God deals with failure in leaders?

theme: The Glory Cloud Fills the Temple and Yeshua Ascends

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 5 describes trumpeters and singers becoming 'as one' in praise the moment before the cloud of Adonai's kavod, glory, fills the Temple so thickly the priests cannot continue ministering — notice the unity of worship precedes the manifestation of glory.
Kavod, glory, literally carries the sense of weightiness. What does it mean for God's presence to be so 'heavy' that human ministry has to stop?

Acts 1 records Yeshua ascending as a cloud receives Him out of the disciples' sight, with the promise He will return 'in the same way' — connect the cloud of glory filling the earthly Temple with the cloud receiving the risen Yeshua, Himself the greater Temple.
The disciples in Acts 1 are told to wait for the Ruach's power rather than act immediately on their own plans — where in your life this week is God asking you to wait rather than rush ahead?

The disciples ask Yeshua in Acts 1:6 whether He will now restore the kingdom to Israel, and He redirects them to Spirit-empowered witness instead of answering directly — discuss how to hold together hope for Israel's future restoration with a present call to mission.

theme: Fire Falls at the Dedication and Fire Falls at Shavuot

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 7 records fire coming down from heaven to consume Solomon's offering, with the glory filling the Temple so fully that the people bow with their faces to the ground, declaring, 'He is good, His chesed endures forever' — notice the fire and the people's worship arrive together.

God's promise in 2 Chronicles 7:14 calls His people to yikanu, humble themselves, and shuv, turn, from their wicked ways. What is the difference between these two actions, and why might both be necessary for healing?

Acts 2 describes tongues of fire resting on each believer on Shavuot as the Ruach ha-Kodesh is poured out — connect fire descending on Solomon's offering at the Temple's dedication with fire descending on the believers, both marking God's acceptance at a pilgrimage feast.
2 Chronicles 7:14 calls for humbling, prayer, seeking God's face, and turning from wicked ways — which of those four specific actions is hardest for you personally to actually do this week?
2 Chronicles 7:14 is often quoted for national revival — discuss what genuine corporate humility and repentance would concretely look like among believers today, as opposed to just quoting the verse.

theme: The Queen of Sheba's Awe and a Beggar Healed at the Gate

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 9 has the Queen of Sheba test Solomon with chidot, hard questions, and leave so overwhelmed that 'there was no more breath in her,' explicitly crediting Adonai's love for Israel for putting Solomon on the throne — notice she gives God, not Solomon, the ultimate credit.

Chidot, riddles or hard questions, is the same kind of wordplay found in Samson's riddle to the Philistines. What does testing a king with riddles reveal about how ancient rulers demonstrated true wisdom?

Acts 3 has Kefa heal a lame beggar at the Temple's Beautiful Gate 'in the name of Yeshua the Nazarene,' immediately deflecting praise from himself to Messiah — connect this to the Queen of Sheba's recognition that Israel's God, not Solomon's own greatness, was the true source of what she witnessed.
The Queen of Sheba came a long way and asked hard questions before she was convinced — what honest, difficult questions about faith are you willing to bring to God rather than avoid?

A Gentile queen publicly praises the God of Israel because of what she witnesses in Solomon's kingdom — discuss how a believing community's visible life today might provoke that same kind of recognition from outsiders.

theme: Rehoboam's Foolish Counsel and Bold Testimony Before the Sanhedrin

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 10 has Rehoboam reject the elders' gentle counsel for the harsh advice of his peers, telling the people 'my little finger is thicker than my father's loins' — notice this single arrogant line costs him ten tribes.

Rehoboam threatens to make the people's yoke kavad, heavy, using the same root as kavod, glory or weight. What does it suggest that the word for a crushing burden shares its root with the word for glory?

Acts 4 has Kefa and Yochanan stand before the same Sanhedrin that condemned Yeshua and declare, 'there is salvation in no one else' (4:12) — connect the rulers' rejection of wise counsel in Rehoboam's story to the rulers again rejecting the testimony offered them here.
Rehoboam chose the counsel of peers who told him what he wanted to hear over the wisdom of elders — whose voice are you listening to right now, and is it the wise one?
Discuss how communities and leaders choose which counsel to trust, and what it costs, as in Rehoboam's case, when popularity or ego is prioritized over tested wisdom.

theme: Abijah's Covenant Appeal and the Seriousness of Integrity

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 13 has King Abijah appeal to a brit melach, covenant of salt, given to David's line, contrasting Judah's true priesthood and Temple worship with Jeroboam's golden calves — notice his confidence, 'God is with us at our head.'
Brit melach, covenant of salt, is an ancient idiom for an enduring, binding agreement, since salt does not spoil. What does invoking this specific phrase add to Abijah's appeal for legitimacy?

Acts 5 recounts Ananias and Sapphira's deceit before the Ruach ha-Kodesh resulting in sudden death, producing great fear in the community — connect the seriousness of covenant faithfulness in Abijah's appeal with the seriousness of integrity expected within the early Messianic community.
Abijah drew a clear line between true worship and compromised worship even while facing a much larger army — where do you need similar clarity about what you will and won't compromise this week?

Ananias and Sapphira's sin looks modest compared to others in Scripture, yet God deals with it severely — discuss what this reveals about the standard of integrity expected within a gathered community of faith.

theme: Asa's Bold Reliance on God and the Choosing of the Seven

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 14 has Asa remove foreign altars and high places, then pray before facing Zerah the Cushite's vast army, 'it makes no difference to You to help, whether with many or with those who have no strength' — notice the content of his prayer focuses entirely on God's power, not Judah's odds.
Asa prays, 'we nish'anu, rely, on You,' using a root meaning to lean on for support. What does 'leaning' on God suggest that simply 'believing in' Him might not fully capture?

Acts 6 has the community appoint seven men, including Stephen who was full of the Ruach, to serve tables so the apostles could focus on prayer and the word — connect Asa's reforms clearing away distractions to focus worship on Adonai alone with this structural change to protect the apostles' focus.
Asa's prayer named God's power directly rather than his own strategy — try praying this week by naming God's specific attributes rather than just listing your requests.

Asa relies wholly on God here but falters in later chapters — discuss why present faithfulness does not guarantee it will be sustained, and what safeguards or habits help faith endure over a lifetime.

theme: Asa's Later Failure and Stephen's Bold Witness

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 16 has Asa, in stark contrast to his earlier faith, hire the king of Aram against Baasha instead of trusting Adonai, then imprison the seer Hanani for rebuking him — notice the text also says he sought physicians rather than Adonai for his foot disease.
Hanani is called a chozeh, seer, one who 'sees' God's message. What does it cost a king to silence the very office meant to see and speak truth to him?

Acts 7 recounts Stephen's long retelling of Israel's history to the Sanhedrin before his martyrdom, in which he sees 'the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God' — connect the pattern of rejecting prophetic correction, as Asa rejected Hanani, to the Sanhedrin rejecting Stephen as they had rejected Yeshua.
Asa's drift moved from bold faith to self-reliance to hostility toward correction — what early warning sign of that same drift might you need to watch for in your own walk right now?

Discuss what it reveals about a person's heart when they respond to correction with hostility rather than reflection, as Asa did with Hanani — how should a community handle prophetic rebuke of its leaders today?

theme: Micaiah Stands Alone and Philip Reaches Samaria

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 18 has four hundred court prophets tell Ahab and Jehoshaphat to go to war and win, but Micaiah alone tells the truth, saying, 'as Adonai lives, I will speak what my God says,' even knowing it means prison rations.

The vision in 2 Chronicles 18:20-22 describes a ruach sheker, a lying or deceiving spirit, sent into the mouths of Ahab's prophets by permission of the heavenly court. What does this vivid scene suggest about how easily people can be given exactly the message they want to hear?

Acts 8 has Philip explain Isaiah 53's suffering servant to the Ethiopian eunuch, 'like a sheep led to slaughter' — connect the danger of a king surrounded only by voices telling him what he wants to hear with the eunuch's genuine openness to a truth he did not expect.
Micaiah spoke the truth knowing it would cost him comfort and freedom — where this week might faithfulness require you to say something unwelcome rather than something popular?

Discuss the courage it took for Micaiah to stand alone against four hundred voices agreeing with the king — what does genuine faithfulness look like when an entire group, even a religious one, is aligned against the truth?

theme: A Battle Won Through Praise and Sha'ul's Sudden Reversal

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 20 has Jehoshaphat appoint singers to march ahead of the army praising the hadrat kodesh, splendor of holiness, of Adonai, and the enemy coalition of Moab, Ammon, and Mount Seir destroys itself in confusion before Judah even fights — notice Judah spends three days afterward only gathering plunder.

Hadrat kodesh, the splendor or beauty of holiness, describes what the singers praised as they marched ahead of the soldiers. What does putting worship, rather than weapons, at the front of the army suggest about where Jehoshaphat placed his confidence?

Acts 9 recounts Sha'ul's encounter with the risen Yeshua on the road to Damascus, struck blind and then restored through Ananias — connect God fighting battles His people cannot win themselves with God's complete reversal of Sha'ul from persecutor to apostle.
Jehoshaphat's strategy against overwhelming odds was worship, not tactics — what overwhelming situation in your life this week could you respond to with praise before you respond with a plan?
Discuss what it practically looks like for a community facing a genuine threat to respond first with worship rather than only with strategizing, and whether that is wisdom or presumption.

theme: The Hidden Heir Joash and the Vision That Opens the Door to the Nations

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 22-23 has Athaliah murder the royal seed to seize the throne, but the infant Joash is hidden by Jehoshabeath in the Temple for six years until the priest Jehoiada crowns him — notice the Davidic line nearly went extinct in this moment.

The text uses a form of ganav, to steal away, to describe how Jehoshabeath 'stole' the infant Joash from among the king's sons about to be killed. What does this dramatic verb add to the urgency of the rescue?

Acts 10 has Kefa receive a vision of a sheet with unclean animals before visiting the God-fearing centurion Cornelius, opening the door of the good news to the nations — connect the preservation of David's line against near-annihilation, crucial for Messiah's eventual coming, with the expanding scope of that Messiah's redemption to all peoples.
A single hidden act of courage by Jehoshabeath preserved God's covenant promise for generations — what small, hidden act of faithfulness might matter far more than you realize right now?

Discuss what it means that God's covenant promise to David hung, humanly speaking, on one woman's hidden courage — how does this shape how we view seemingly fragile moments in our own history as under God's sovereign protection?

theme: Joash's Temple Repairs, His Fall, and the Church at Antioch

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 24 has Joash repair the Temple under Jehoiada's guidance, but after the priest's death he turns to idolatry and has Jehoiada's own son Zechariah stoned in the Temple courts for confronting him, with Zechariah's dying words, 'may Adonai see and avenge.'

The Temple repair in 2 Chronicles 24 uses language of chazak habedek, strengthening or repairing the breach. What does 'repairing a breach' suggest about the ongoing maintenance faithful worship requires, not just a one-time fix?

Acts 11 has Kefa report to the Jerusalem believers about Gentile inclusion, and in Antioch the disciples are first called by the name that would come to mark the movement — connect the repair of the Temple's physical breaches with the ongoing repair and expansion of the believing community through the good news.
Joash followed God faithfully only as long as his mentor Jehoiada was alive to guide him — is your own faith resting on someone else's convictions, or have you made it truly your own?

Discuss the danger of a faith that is borrowed or inherited from a mentor rather than personally owned, as seen in Joash's collapse after Jehoiada's death — what safeguards help faith survive the loss of a spiritual mentor?

theme: Uzziah's Prideful Overreach and Herod's Deadly Pride

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 26 has Uzziah's great success lead him to enter the Temple to burn incense, a role reserved for priests alone, and he is struck with tzara'at on his forehead — notice that eighty priests confront him before the affliction even appears.

Tzara'at, often translated leprosy, was a scale-disease carrying ritual impurity that required separation from the community. What does it mean that this particular affliction strikes Uzziah specifically for overstepping a boundary reserved for the priesthood?

Acts 12 has Herod Agrippa kill Ya'akov and imprison Kefa, who is freed by an angel, while Herod himself is later struck down for accepting the crowd's worship as though he were a god — connect both kings being struck down for claiming honor that belongs to God alone.
Uzziah's downfall came at the height of his success, not during hardship — where in an area of current strength or success might pride be quietly creeping in for you?
Discuss why prosperity and power often prove more spiritually dangerous than hardship, using Uzziah and Herod as two examples of rulers undone at the peak of their success.

theme: Hezekiah Cleanses the Temple and Sha'ul Is Sent Out

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 29 has Hezekiah, in the very first month of his very first year as king, reopen the Temple doors his father Ahaz had shut, with the Levites consecrating themselves and hauling out the accumulated filth — notice how immediately he acts upon taking the throne.

The cleansing in 2 Chronicles 29 removes tumah, ritual defilement, from the Temple before worship can resume. What does this concept of clearing tumah before restored worship suggest about the order in which renewal must happen?

Acts 13 has the Ruach ha-Kodesh set apart Sha'ul and Barnabas for the work of proclaiming Messiah, and Sha'ul's synagogue sermon at Pisidian Antioch traces God's dealings from the Exodus through David to 'a Savior, Yeshua' — connect Hezekiah's immediate, decisive reform upon taking the throne with Sha'ul's immediate, decisive obedience once set apart.
Hezekiah acted swiftly to undo his father's damage rather than easing gradually into reform — what compromise inherited from your own past deserves a swift, decisive break rather than a slow fade?
Discuss what it costs, and what it's worth, to make a clean and immediate break from an inherited pattern of compromise, as Hezekiah did upon becoming king.

theme: Hezekiah's Great Passover and Unlikely Believers at Lystra

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 30 has Hezekiah celebrate Passover a month late, using the Torah's provision for those unable to keep it on time, and invite the scattered remnant of the northern tribes — notice that some from Ephraim, Manasseh, Issachar, and Zebulun came humbly even though others mocked the invitation.
2 Chronicles 30:25-26 describes simchah, joy, in Jerusalem unlike anything since Solomon's days. What does this word for communal, shared celebration add beyond individual happiness?

Acts 14 has Paul heal a lame man at Lystra, only to have the crowd mistake him and Barnabas for pagan gods before Paul is stoned and left for dead, yet rises and continues on — connect the theme of unexpected, mixed audiences responding to God's work, from fallen northern tribes at Hezekiah's Passover to pagan Gentiles at Lystra.
Some at Hezekiah's Passover humbled themselves and came despite others mocking the invitation — is there an invitation to return to God you've been putting off because of what others might think?
Discuss what determines whether a person responds humbly to an invitation to return to God, as some northern Israelites did, versus scoffing at it, as others did in the same story.

theme: Manasseh's Astonishing Repentance and the Jerusalem Council's Grace

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 33 has Manasseh, Judah's most wicked king, carried captive to Babylon in bronze hooks, humble himself there, and be restored by Adonai to his kingdom, where he then reverses many of his own former evils — notice this reversal comes after, not before, his captivity.

Manasseh vayikana, humbled himself, in captivity, using the same root as 2 Chronicles 7:14's call for God's people to yikanu, humble themselves. What connection does this shared word draw between Solomon's promise and Manasseh's own story generations later?

Acts 15 has the Jerusalem Council conclude that salvation comes by grace through faith for Jew and Gentile alike, without requiring Gentile believers to be circumcised, while affirming that Moses continues to be read in synagogues every Sabbath — connect Manasseh's story, that even the worst can be genuinely restored, with the Council's grace-centered resolution.
Manasseh's restoration came only after he hit bottom and humbled himself — is there a place in your own life where pride is keeping you from the humbling that would open the door to restoration?

Manasseh is arguably the most wicked king in Judah's history yet receives genuine restoration — discuss whether anyone can rightly be considered 'too far gone,' and how this story should shape how we view repentance in others.

theme: Josiah's Scroll, Exile, and Cyrus's Hope-Filled Decree

questions.text: 2 Chronicles 34 has young King Josiah begin seeking Adonai at sixteen and purging idolatry at twenty, and then, during Temple repairs, the priest Hilkiah finds the long-lost 'scroll of the Torah of Adonai given through Moshe,' at which Josiah tears his clothes upon hearing it read — notice how much of this reform starts with the rediscovery of a forgotten text.

Cyrus's closing decree calls the exiles to ya'al, go up, to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple — the same root behind aliyah, the term still used today for immigration to the land of Israel. What weight does it add to know this word has carried God's people home across so many centuries?

Acts 16 has Paul and Silas imprisoned in Philippi, singing at midnight until an earthquake opens the doors, leading the jailer and his household to believe and be immersed that same night — connect this pattern of imprisonment giving way to sudden deliverance and release with Cyrus's decree releasing the exiles to return home and rebuild.

As this year's read-through of the Tanakh closes on Cyrus's invitation to 'go up and rebuild the House of Adonai,' consider what your own year of reading has rebuilt or restored in your walk with God, and what new chapter, like the Philippian jailer's, is opening next.

2 Chronicles 36, and with it the traditional Hebrew Tanakh, closes not on judgment or the fall of Jerusalem but on Cyrus's open invitation to return and rebuild — discuss why ending the Hebrew Scriptures on this note of hope shapes how we read the entire story arc of Scripture, and how it anticipates the ultimate restoration promised in Messiah.
